articles.title: La-Meme: Distorted Attachment to Anger-Based Authority and the Reproduction of Psychosocial Harm in Workplace Bullying
articles.authors: Koichi Hiraoka
articles.abstract:
This paper contributes to research on workplace bullying, abusive supervision, and 
psychosocial harm by proposing the concept of La-Meme, or Love-Anger Meme. 
Existing studies have clarified how hostile supervisory behavior, workplace bullying, 
workplace incivility, emotional contagion, social learning, organizational culture, 
psychological safety, and organizational silence harm individuals and organizations. 
However, less attention has been paid to how anger-based workplace cultures may be 
preserved not only through fear, imitation, and social learning, but also through 
distorted attachment to anger-based authority. 
La-Meme is defined as a distorted attachment pattern in which anger and attachment 
become mutually entangled within organizational relationships. The concept operates in 
two directions. First, leaders or authority figures who rely on anger may favor members 
who imitate their anger, interpreting them as similar others, loyal subordinates, or 
successors. Second, members harmed by anger may still seek recognition, belonging, 
renewed proximity, or restored self-worth from the same anger-based authority. 
Through this ambivalent interdependence, anger-based authority can be preserved not 
only by intimidation but also by attachment. 
The paper argues that La-Meme helps explain several forms of psychosocial harm in 
workplaces, including reduced psychological safety, organizational silence, downward 
aggression, turnover, distorted evaluation criteria, and the obscuring of responsibility. 
Rather than treating workplace anger only as individual emotional dysregulation or 
isolated misconduct, the concept highlights the relational and cultural processes through 
which harmful anger may be protected, imitated, and affectively maintained. The paper 
concludes that interventions against workplace bullying should examine not only visible 
expressions of anger, but also who imitates anger, who protects anger-based imitators, 
who seeks recognition from anger-based authority, and how distorted attachment 
sustains harmful workplace patterns.

articles.title: Distributed Mediated Personhood: Relational Continuity and Parasocial Relationship Anchoring in Hybrid Human–Avatar Systems
articles.authors: Xin Yun Choong
articles.abstract:
Parasocial relationship (PSR) theory was developed for media environments in which embodiment, identity, and agency were typically bundled within a single social figure. VTubers challenge this assumption by separating symbolic embodiment (the avatar) from the human source of agency (the operator), creating a unique opportunity to examine where audiences locate relational continuity when these elements can vary independently. Drawing on survey data from 978 VTuber viewers, this study distinguishes between relationship formation and relationship anchoring as two analytically distinct dimensions of parasocial engagement.
The first dimension concerns the relational conditions under which parasocial engagement develops. Correlational analyses indicate that interaction-oriented engagement, emotional gratifications, and community participation are associated with stronger identification, emotional connection, and trust. These findings suggest that parasocial engagement in VTuber environments emerges through many of the same social and affective processes documented in prior PSI/PSR research.
The second dimension concerns relationship anchoring: where audiences locate relational continuity once parasocial relationships become established. Across multiple indicators, viewers increasingly oriented toward the human operator over time, were substantially less tolerant of operator replacement than avatar replacement, and expressed strong preferences for human-operated rather than AI-operated virtual performers. Taken together, these patterns suggest that audiences place greater weight on continuity of the operator than on continuity of visual representation alone.
Building on these findings, the study proposes a distinction between relationship formation and relationship anchoring and develops the concept of distributed mediated personhood to account for parasocial targets whose embodiment and agency are distributed across multiple components. More broadly, the findings suggest that durable digital intimacy depends less on representation itself than on the perception of relational continuity across interactions. As mediated personhood becomes increasingly distributed across human and artificial systems, the findings suggest that understanding where audiences locate relational continuity constitutes an important extension of PSI/PSR theory in increasingly hybrid human–avatar–AI environments.

articles.title: Procedural Objective AI (POAI): A Multi-Inference Framework for Deliberation
articles.authors: Sherif Rashwan
articles.abstract: This article introduces Procedural Objective AI (POAI) as a proposed term for a multi-inference architecture in which several inference engines, reasoning roles, retrieval channels, or model instances deliberate before a final answer is produced. The concept responds to a growing concern in human--AI interaction: conversational systems may become overly agreeable, preference-sensitive, or sycophantic when they adapt too closely to user framing. Rather than treating objectivity as an impossible claim of perfect neutrality, the article defines objectivity procedurally. In this framework, an answer becomes more objective when it has passed through structured disagreement, adversarial critique, evidence checking, uncertainty reporting, and adjudication. Drawing on research on AI debate, multi-agent debate, self-consistency, chain-of-thought reasoning, retrieval-augmented generation, constitutional critique, language-model evaluation, and sycophancy, the paper proposes a social-scientific framework for studying how multiple inference engines can work together using currently available resources. It specifies the problem, conceptual boundaries, mechanisms, implementation patterns, evaluation criteria, limitations, and research agenda for Procedural Objective AI as a practical alternative to single-agent response generation.

articles.title: Developments in the field of reliability and agreement studies in health, psychological, and educational measurement: a methods review protocol
articles.authors: Merle-Marie Pittelkow; Maximilian Mönch; Lidwine Mokkink; David L. Streiner; Stig Brorson; Mohamed Shoukri; Jan Kottner
articles.abstract:
Introduction: The Guidelines for Reporting Reliability and Agreement Studies (GRRAS) were developed to improve the completeness and transparency of reporting of reliability and agreement of health measurement instruments. However, since their publication in 2011 methodological standards, both for reporting guideline development and reliability, agreement, and measurement error studies, have advanced, highlighting the need for aligning and updating. In addition, related initiatives like COnsensus-based Standards for the selection of health Measurement Instruments (COSMIN) have emerged, offering opportunities to harmonize terminology and promote consistency across diverse types of measurement instruments. This methods review aims (1) to systematically identify and synthesize commentaries on and evaluations of the original GRRAS, and (2) to map recent methodological developments in the planning, conduct, and interpretation of reliability, agreement and measurement error studies in health science, psychology and education that should be reflected in the reporting, to inform the development of the GRRAS-COSMIN reporting guidelines. 
Methods and analysis: Two complementary search strategies will be employed. First, forward direct citation tracking of the original GRRAS publications will be conducted in Web of Science. We will include sources providing critique, commentaries, or suggestions related to the GRRAS. We will exclude publications that used GRRAS just for structuring their report, withdrawn or retracted articles, textbooks, peer reviews, supplements, and conference materials without full-text publications. Included articles will be analysed using descriptive content analysis. Second, we will perform a systematic search in Medline, PsycINFO, Embase, Eric, and Cinahl supplemented by key references identified by or known to the author team. We will include studies, reviews, commentaries, editorials, methodological papers, tutorials and guidance documents that discuss or critically reflect on methodological aspects of the measurement properties reliability and/or agreement/measurement error published between 01.01.2015 and 30.06.2026 in health sciences, psychology, or education. Eligibility will be assessed by two independent reviewers and included sources will be analysed using ‘codebook’ thematic analysis. Findings will be presented narratively supplemented by visuals and tables if appropriate.
Ethics and dissemination: This study involves publicly available data. No ethical approval is needed. Results will be disseminated through an open-access journal publication following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses-Scoping Review Extension and conference presentations.

articles.title: CAse STudy in public health and medicine related to Disasters (CAST-D): A consensus-based reporting guideline
articles.authors: Yoshitaka Nishikawa; CAST-D Group
articles.abstract:
Background: Case studies are a critical research method for capturing lessons learned from disasters. Unlike standard clinical case reports, disaster-related case studies encompass a broad spectrum, ranging from micro-level cases with clinical or public health implications to meso-level organizational or community responses and macro-level national policies. Currently, no consensus-based reporting guideline specifically addresses this diverse range, within the context of disaster response, recovery, preparedness, and management.
Methods: The case studies in public health and medicine related to Disasters (CAST-D) guideline was developed according to the EQUATOR Network guidelines. Drawing on a prior systematic search and review of disaster-related case studies, existing reporting gaps were identified. Subsequently, a modified Delphi process was used to generate and refine checklist items. The Delphi panel comprised 25 experts from six continents (Japan, USA, UK, Australia, Ethiopia, Argentina, Brazil, France, and Ghana), recruited through purposive sampling via professional networks. Two survey rounds were completed between December 2024 and June 2025. Consensus through both agreement and non-disagreement was defined using the RAND/UCLA Appropriateness Method (RAM). The CAST-D short version was developed using a higher consensus threshold for use in short articles or conference abstracts. The final item list was confirmed in January 2026. 
Findings: The CAST-D checklist comprises 33 items with 27 headings, organized into sections including title, abstract and keywords, introduction, methods, results, discussion, conclusion, and additional information. It emphasizes explicit reporting of ethical considerations, case definition, timeline, geographical information, and lessons learned across micro, meso, and macro levels. The CAST-D short version was also developed and comprises 15 items.
Conclusions: CAST-D provides a structured framework to enhance the transparency and utility of disaster-related case studies in public health and medicine. Its adoption will facilitate the synthesis of the evidence needed to strengthen global health security and disaster risk reduction.

articles.title: Brazilian L2 English Accentedness Ratings by LLMs: Systematic Bias and the Limits of Automated Perception
articles.authors: Felipe Flores Kupske; Laura Zorzi
articles.abstract: This study investigates whether audio-capable large language models (LLMs) categorize L2 English accentedness in ways similar to human raters. Evaluations produced by Google Gemini Flash and Gemini Pro are compared against those of native American English raters, using recordings of Brazilian speakers from the CSLU: Foreign Accented English Corpus (Lander, 2007). Analysis revealed systematic divergences: both LLMs consistently overestimated accentedness, assigning strong accent ratings at proportions absent from the human baseline. Gemini Pro exhibited the greatest bias (OR = 8.25), contradicting the assumption that greater computational sophistication yields judgments more aligned with human perception. These findings are consistent with the hypothesis that the models examined lack the socio-phonetic tolerance that characterizes human accent assessment and suggest that they should not be regarded as reliable substitutes for human raters in L2 accentedness evaluation tasks.

articles.title: Criação de mapas com R (tmap)
articles.authors: Felipe Flores Kupske
articles.abstract: Este guia é uma introdução prática à criação de mapas no R com o pacote tmap, desenvolvida como material de apoio para estudantes e pesquisadores das áreas de linguística, sociolinguística, sociofonética e áreas afins que desejam representar dados em mapas geográficos. O guia cobre: (1) instalação e carregamento dos pacotes necessários, com notas sobre problemas comuns de instalação; (2) criação de mapas   nativos do tmap, incluindo mapas estáticos, interativos, com múltiplas camadas e facetas; (3) criação de mapas do Brasil e de estados a partir de arquivos shapefile(.shp) do IBGE; (4) integração de dados externos ao shape file para visualização de variáveis quantitativas em mapas; e (5) exportação de mapas em diferentes formatos. O pacote sf (Simple Features) é adotado como substituto do rgdal, descontinuado em dezembro de 2023. Parto do pressuposto que o leitor tenha familiaridade básica como R e o RStudio, como instalar pacotes, abrir arquivos e executar funções simples.

articles.title: Unpacking Conceptions of Being American in Relation to Identity and Mental Health Among Emerging Adults of Color
articles.authors: Dulce Wilkinson Westberg; Michelle Pasco; Bethany Cruz; Shima Golmohamadi; Alan Meca
articles.abstract: This study sought to examine how U.S. young adults of color understand American identity in the context of heightened sociopolitical tensions and how these understandings relate to measures of cultural identity and mental health. We used qualitative methods to identify themes in open-ended responses about American identity from 450 young adults of color during the first administration of Donald Trump. Quantitative methods were then used to examine themes in relation to cultural identity and mental health outcomes. Extending past work, we identified themes characterizing what it means to be American, ways youth felt American, and ways youth felt not American. Themes related with measures of cultural identity and mental health and varied based on race/ethnicity, gender, and nativity, revealing diversity in how American identity is experienced. Findings underscore the complexity of national identity among young adults of color who are navigating what it means to be American amid rising sociopolitical conflict.

articles.title: Systematic review of genetic associations between irritability and mental health disorders
articles.authors: Nhu Huynh; Christopher Kendall; Evelyn Ayaji; Nicole Roslin; Roxy O'Rourke; Christie Burton; Jacob Vorstman; Jennifer Crosbie
articles.abstract: Irritability is a transdiagnostic trait associated with a wide range of psychiatric disorders, but the extent to which this co-occurrence is related to genetic factors is unclear. This systematic review aims to provide an overview of studies examining the genetic associations between irritability-related traits and diverse mental health disorders and traits. Studies published up to May 2025 were retrieved from MEDLINE, EMBASE, PsycINFO, Web of Science, and the reference lists of included studies. Studies of human participants reporting quantitative genetic associations using twin or genome-wide genetic analyses (i.e., genome-wide association study [GWAS] and polygenic risk score [PRS] studies) were screened for inclusion based on pre-determined criteria. A total of 30 studies met the inclusion criteria, including six twin and 24 genome-wide genetic analyses. The results indicated shared genetic liability (e.g., positive genetic correlation) between irritability-related traits and specific mental health outcomes, with the most replicated evidence observed for internalizing (i.e., depression; rg=0.46-0.56) and externalizing (i.e., ADHD; rg=0.29-1.00) domains. Emerging evidence for other domains, such as neurodevelopmental disorders, psychotic disorders, and substance use disorders, remained inconclusive due to the limited number of studies on these disorders. Research using standardized measurement of the irritability phenotype and more ancestrally-diverse samples is needed to better understand the genetic architecture of irritability and its transdiagnostic role in mental health disorders.

articles.title: Faith and the Other. Revisiting the Transcendence of the Other in the Psychology of Religion
articles.authors: Heinz Streib
articles.abstract: This article is an exercise in revisiting the concept of ‘faith’ in the psychology of religion. It addresses questions such as: What is faith? Is faith different from religion? In what ways? What does it mean defining faith as “ultimate concern”—as some colleagues already do? Answers are inspired by the philosophies of Paul Tillich and Emmanuel Levinas. An examination of Tillich’s philosophy of religion reveals that “ultimate concern” is a highly critical concept that, exactly by pointing to the Ultimate, entails a specific, radically semiotic understanding of transcendence. From reading Levinas, we conclude that ethics is grounded in the primacy of the Other, the transcendence one encounters in the face of the Other, and in the diachrony of the Good. Taken together, the insights from Tillich and Levinas suggest conceptualizing ‘faith’ as “ultimate concern” by which we are “grasped and turned to the infinite” (Tillich) and respond to the experience of transcendence that speaks to us in the face of the Other (Levinas). This confronts psychology with the challenge of operationalizing such experiences of transcendence of the Other. While the model of faith styles, which is based on Fowler’s theory of faith development, can be regarded as a beginning, the article concludes with the suggestion of a research program that features xenosophia.

articles.title: Efficacy of Imitation Training for Individuals with Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Systematic Review
articles.authors: Hooshang Dadgar; Zahra Moshtaghi Fard; Yang Zhang
articles.abstract: This review examined imitation training for children with autism. Based on 38 articles—including 27 single-subject designs and 6 randomized controlled trials (RCTs)—findings suggest imitation-based approaches, especially reciprocal imitation training (RIT), may be a helpful first option. These approaches can lead to moderate-to-strong gains in imitation skills, potentially supporting growth in other social/developmental areas. Effectiveness may depend on the child's age, delivery format, imitation type, and existing skills. Structured methods might bring quick progress, while naturalistic, socially responsive approaches could lead to more lasting and transferable skills. New methods like telehealth and robot-assisted training appear promising but need more study. Overall, imitation training seems to work best when started early, tailored to the child, and done with care.

articles.title: When I say... clinically significant change
articles.authors: Christoph Lindner; Steffen Zitzmann
articles.abstract: Clinically significant change is a central but ambiguous concept in clinical practice and medical education. This brief “When I say...” manuscript clarifies three practical meanings of the term: recovery, response, and interpretation using additional reference points such as patient experience, clinical judgement, functioning, and follow-up course. The article aims to help learners and clinicians distinguish detectable change from change that matters for patient care.

articles.title: How is AI impacting people?
articles.authors: Christopher Summerfield; Rebecca Anselmetti; Shahar Avin; Vanessa Cheung; Tom Eccles; Anna Gausen; Kobi Hackenburg; Keno; Hannah Rose Kirk; Lennart Luettgau
articles.abstract: AI systems are now used by a billion people worldwide. What is their impact on human users? Popular narratives of concern argue that AI may be undermining our cognitive abilities, polluting our information ecosystem, corroding human relationships, damaging our mental health, and rendering us vulnerable to persuasion, manipulation, emotional dependence, and social engineering. Does the empirical evidence support these claims? Emerging research has begun to study the impact that AI has on people, via model evaluations, user studies, analysis of consumer chatlogs, and agent-based simulations. Here, we review this research, to summarise what we know about how AI is impacting people.

articles.title: Reassessing purported prospective links between mobile phone addiction, bedtime procrastination, and physical activity: A commentary on Xu and Li (2026)
articles.authors: Kimmo Sorjonen; Bo Melin; Marika Melin
articles.abstract: Xu and Li recently reported prospective associations between mobile phone addiction, bedtime procrastination, and physical activity based on analyses using cross-lagged panel models (CLPMs). However, estimates obtained from CLPMs are known to be vulnerable to statistical artifacts and may not necessarily reflect genuine prospective influences. We reanalyzed the reported correlational structure using alternative analytic specifications and obtained markedly different conclusions depending on model choice, including positive, negative, and null effects. When these discrepant estimates were meta-analytically synthesized, the overall effects were indistinguishable from zero. These findings suggest that the reported associations may not provide convincing evidence of prospective influence and that the conclusions drawn by Xu and Li may be premature. More generally, the present commentary highlights the importance of evaluating findings across multiple plausible analytic approaches and avoiding strong inferences based on a single model specification.

articles.title: The Link Between Diverse Words and Original Ideas Is Weakening  in the AI-Era College Admissions
articles.authors: Kibum Moon; Kostadin Kushlev; Andrew Bank; Benjamin Lira Luttges; Indre Viskontas; James C. Kaufman; Dan Richard Johnson; Angela Lee Duckworth; Adam Green
articles.abstract: Concerns that AI homogenizes human thinking appear at odds with findings that LLM writing is perceived as more creative than human writing. We propose that LLMs enhance surface-level diversity while simultaneously homogenizing underlying ideas. Four large-scale natural experiments tested this preregistered “semantic disjunction” hypothesis in the high-stakes context of college admissions. Comparing personal statements written by 372,793 applicants after-versus-before ChatGPT’s 2022 release, multi-level semantic analyses revealed increasing surface diversity of words simultaneous with decreasing conceptual diversity of sentences (within essays) and whole documents (between essays). Three controlled experiments provided causal evidence that AI use produced semantic disjunction, including in applicants from the natural experiment, and indicated an apparent root mechanism: a positive association between surface and conceptual diversity in humans was weakened or reversed in LLM writing. Despite conceptual homogenization, raters perceived post-ChatGPT essays as more creative. Semantic disjunction explained this apparent paradox: increasing word-level diversity explained increasing creativity judgments, overriding effects of decreased conceptual diversity, even when raters (human and AI) considered many essays together. Avoiding over-reliance on word-level surface diversity by considering multiple semantic levels is likely to support more complete understanding of AI’s effects on human thinking. Disjunctive trends were widespread, but were strongest in minoritized applicants.

articles.title: Making Room for Theory in DIF and Measurement Invariance Analysis with Covariate Effects on Groups of Items
articles.authors: Sanford R Student; Klint Kanopka
articles.abstract: We propose and study an extension of moderated nonlinear factor analysis that models covariate effects—that is, differential item functioning (DIF)—at the level of item groups rather than, or in addition to, individual items. This allows for a more theory-driven approach to DIF and measurement invariance testing in which researchers can form and test hypotheses about why items differ in how they function across an arbitrary number of categorical or continuous covariates. As a motivating example, we demonstrate gender effects on a developmental measure where scores that assume invariance overstate boys’ cognitive development and understate their fine and gross motor development, with opposite implications for girls. Simulation demonstrates the empirical identifiability and predictable statistical performance of the proposed model, and additional empirical examples demonstrate the approach’s substantial flexibility.

articles.title: Intention and foresight in deliberate actions: An ERP study
articles.authors: Tomáš Dominik; Emma Chen; Maverick Morales; Ilai Gavish; Melissa M. Brillhart-Gara; Gideon Yaffe; Uri Maoz
articles.abstract: The distinction between intention and foresight is critical for law and morality. For example, a firefighter who forces a hose through a parked car to reach a hydrant may foresee the damage without intending it—unless the car belongs to a rival, in which case the damage may be intended after all. This study aimed to disentangle the neural correlates of intention and foresight using a novel paradigm. Twenty-five participants completed a task inspired by the game Frogger, where hitting objects could be desirable (earning points) or inconsequential. Outcomes varied by likelihood (high vs. low) and actuality (hit vs. miss), resulting in a 2×2×2 design: intention (high/low) × outcome (hit/miss) × expectedness (expected/unexpected). Electroencephalography (EEG) revealed that feedback-related negativity (FRN) amplitude was larger for participants during high-intention than low-intention outcomes while the P3 potential was larger for unexpected than expected outcomes. However, neither signal exclusively reflected intention or foresight; the FRN also indexed expectedness, and the P3 reflected intention. To address this overlap, we used exploratory effect-matched spatial (EMS) filtering, which identified a widespread brain-activity pattern that better dissociated intention from foresight. These findings enhance our understanding of the neural processes underlying complex decision-making, with potential applications in ethical and legal frameworks.

articles.title: AI Consciousness Will Divide Society
articles.authors: Lucius Caviola
articles.abstract: As AI systems become increasingly advanced and human-like, societies will face a fundamental question: are these systems conscious, and if so, do they deserve moral or legal rights? This chapter argues that rather than converging on a shared answer, society is likely to enter a period of confusion, uncertainty, and deep disagreement. Drawing on recent empirical evidence, it shows that both experts and the public are already divided on the possibility of AI consciousness and the moral status of digital minds. It argues that this division is driven by structural features of the issue, including the difficulty of conceptualizing and measuring consciousness, conflicting economic, political, and emotional incentives, and a disconnect between AI systems’ internal architectures and their outwardly human-like behavior. Such disagreement creates risks of political polarization, regulatory instability, geopolitical tension, and an increased risk of ultimately misattributing moral status. It concludes by outlining strategies for improving expert coordination, fostering constructive public discourse, designing AI systems to reduce moral confusion, and reducing exclusive reliance on consciousness in moral and policy decisions.

articles.title: The influence of visual landmarks on spatiotopic localization and object-location binding
articles.authors: Zitong Lu; Julie Golomb
articles.abstract: Despite receiving visual inputs based on retinotopic (gaze-centered) coordinates, we are able to perceive the spatiotopic (world-centered) locations of objects. Intriguingly, some studies have reported strong evidence that object-location binding occurs in retinotopic coordinates, but other recent studies have explored whether more naturalistic contexts and factors can trigger spatiotopic object-location binding. Here, we examined the influence of visual landmarks. While previous work has supported a benefit of landmarks on spatial localization, it remains unclear whether this benefit would also extend to object-location binding across saccades. In separate blocks, participants completed two tasks: a spatiotopic localization task and a location-irrelevant object identity judgment task known to elicit a spatial congruency bias (SCB, a measure of object-location binding); we examined performance on both tasks with and without visual landmarks. As expected, in the localization task, landmarks consistently improved spatiotopic location memory precision. However, in the object identity judgement task, landmarks alone were not sufficient to induce spatiotopic object-location binding. In Experiment 1, when participants performed a single saccade during the delay between two stimuli (static context), the SCB was purely retinotopic and not different between landmark conditions (present vs. absent). However, in a more dynamic context (Experiment 2) when participants performed additional eye movements during stimulus presentation, the presence of landmarks led to a significant spatiotopic SCB. In summary, landmarks alone aren’t enough to trigger spatiotopic object-location binding, but they can help in a more dynamic context. These results provide new insight into visual stability across eye movements in complex, dynamic environments.

articles.title: Neuroscience-informed Psychoeducation (NIPE): How Brain Awareness Supports Brain Recovery
articles.authors: Tara Rezapour; Jamie Berry; C. Munro Cullum; Behzad Elahi; Islam Elbanna; Medhat Elsabbahy; Mehdi Farokhnia; Nabila Haque; Jing Jin; Mario Juruena
articles.abstract: Over the past two decades, Neuroscience-Informed Psychoeducation (NIPE) has gradually evolved and become integrated into various therapy modules for psychiatric and neurological disorders. Although NIPE shares certain features with conventional psychoeducation, it is distinguished by key content and structure-related elements. Its content centers on brain-based explanations of the mechanisms and symptoms underlying psychiatric and neurological disorders, as well as the neural processes associated with treatment response, remission, and recovery. NIPE promotes self-awareness and insight and may contributes to motivation for treatment and treatment adherence. It also informs patients, caregivers, and communities how to play an active role in the prevention, treatment, and recovery process based on this mechanistic understanding. Structurally, NIPE is designed to align with how the brain learns and adapts, improving the learning experience to promote more enduring outcomes. By clarifying the processes and goals of brain recovery, increasing self-awareness and self-efficacy, and supporting adherence to prescribed treatments, NIPE may enhance motivation and retention, and facilitate recovery while reducing shame and stigma. This paper provides an introductory overview of NIPE including its definitions and terminology, the current evidence base, clinical implications, and directions for future development.

articles.title: The Open Science Movement and Clinical Psychology Training: Rigorous Science is Transparent Science
articles.authors: Donald R Lynam; David Sbarra; Jennifer L Tackett; P. Priscilla Lui; Riley McDanal; Katherine Schaumberg; Aidan G.C. Wright; Yexinyu Yang; Joshua Miller
articles.abstract: Clinical psychology, like other disciplines, is facing a replication/credibility crisis that undermines the evidentiary basis of our science. The Open Science Movement (OSM) offers solutions through practices such as preregistration, Registered Reports, and sharing of data, materials, and code. Clinical psychology has been slow to adopt these reforms, leaving trainees underprepared for emerging norms. This article reviews the factors that contributed to the crisis, outlines the necessary role of transparency in distinguishing rigorous from flawed research, and documents the limited uptake of open science practices in clinical journals and graduate training. We argue that transparency is an ethical as well as methodological imperative and propose a competency-based model for embedding open science principles into doctoral education. We conclude by calling on programs, journals, and accrediting bodies to make transparency a core requirement, essential for restoring credibility and advancing a cumulative, trustworthy clinical science.

articles.title: Cognitive load disrupts different stages of prediction in second-language speakers during a visual world task
articles.authors: Naomi Nota; Ruth Corps; Lauren Hadley; Graham Naylor; Martin John Pickering
articles.abstract: There is evidence that language prediction involves two stages: an automatic (associative) stage and a non-automatic (strategic) predictive stage. Prior work with L1 speakers shows that cognitive load disrupts the distinction between both stages and selectively delays associative prediction, leaving strategic prediction unaffected. Notably, L2 speakers show delayed strategic prediction compared to L1 without external cognitive load, raising questions about whether L2 processing imposes load-like demands. We conducted a preregistered visual world eye-tracking experiment with L2 English speakers, manipulating load via a verbal working memory task. Under no load, L2 speakers showed two prediction stages, with delayed strategic prediction relative to L1. Under load, the distinction between stages disappeared and associative prediction was delayed. These findings support a distinct-constraints account: associative and strategic prediction differ in their dependence on cognitive resources, and delayed strategic prediction in L2 speakers may reflect reduced processing capacity rather than a qualitatively different predictive mechanism.

articles.title: The Perils of Partialing Optimism: Relationships with Personality and Health
articles.authors: Suzanne Carrie Segerstrom; Donald R Lynam
articles.abstract: The question of whether optimistic or pessimistic items in the Life Orientation Test-Revised better predict psychological and physical outcomes is often addressed by entering both subscales in the same model. This approach risks the “perils of partialing” in which partial constructs have lower reliability and different nomological nets than their unpartialed counterparts. The present studies tested the effects of partialing optimism and pessimism using structural equation modeling to test differences in external validity and intraclass correlations (ICC) to test similarity of profiles. In Study 1 (N = 2,281 older adults), the optimism-pessimism correlation was modest (r = -0.32), but nomological nets for unpartialed and partialed constructs with personality and health were significantly different (all p < .00001). In Study 2 (N = 739 younger and midlife adults), the optimism-pessimism correlation was higher (r = -0.61). Nomological nets were significantly different (all p < .00001). Examination of item correlations indicated that partialed constructs may have lost convergent validity with key theoretical elements of trait optimism. Differences between optimism and pessimism were fewer than those between each and its partialed counterpart, and results with partialed optimism and pessimism constructs should be viewed with caution.

articles.title: How Adults in Spain Perceive Love:  Individual Differences in Cultural Attunement and Evaluative Style
articles.authors: Saida Heshmati; Lindy Williams; Dana Hussein; Jaymes Paolo Rombaoa; Royce Anders; Zita Oravecz
articles.abstract: Love is culturally universal in presence, yet highly variable in how people perceive it. The present study examined individual differences in cultural attunement and judgment styles related to love. Adults (N = 502) living in Spain rated 61 everyday scenarios according to how likely each would make most people feel loved. Using the Continuous Response Model within Cultural Consensus Theory, we captured individuals’ alignment with the shared understanding of love (i.e., their cultural attunement) in Spanish culture. Greater attunement was observed among women, individuals with higher perceived support from a significant other, those with lower anxiety, and those who valued benevolence. Weaker attunement, on the other hand, was associated with endorsement of power and traditional values. In addition, individuals differed in how they expressed their judgments: older adults and men used a narrower range of responses, whereas those higher in extraversion and conscientiousness showed greater differentiation across scenarios. More educated individuals were less likely to judge situations as loving overall, whereas those higher in agreeableness were more likely to do so. These findings highlight that judgments of love experiences reflect not only shared cultural knowledge but also systematic individual differences in how that knowledge is applied.

articles.title: Neural Responses to Conflicting Self- and Partner-Directed Feedback Correlate with Trait Emotion Regulation and Daily Support
articles.authors: Shanshan Ma; Erin L Maresh; Andrea M Coppola; Katherine E. Richard; Leonie Koban; Diego Guevara Beltran; David Sbarra; Jessica R. Andrews-Hanna
articles.abstract: Empathy lies at the core of social interactions, particularly in intimate relationships, yet few laboratory tasks are designed to capture empathy in interpersonal contexts—especially those requiring individuals to simultaneously process social information directed toward both the self and a romantic partner. In this exploratory study, 131 adults (from 71 romantic couples) completed a neuroimaging paradigm that manipulated whether positive or negative partner-directed feedback was presented alone or alongside self-directed feedback. The partner-alone feedback contrasts (partner-directed feedback alone versus a neutral control condition) robustly engaged brain regions associated with empathic responding, including the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex, anterior insula, dorsomedial prefrontal cortex and temporoparietal junction. Notably, during the incongruent feedback contrasts—simultaneous conflicting self- and partner-directed feedback versus self-directed feedback alone—participants exhibited diminished affective and brain responses but evidenced unique brain activity in frontoparietal areas frequently associated with cognitive control (e.g., dorsolateral prefrontal cortex and supramarginal gyrus). Furthermore, when responding to positive partner-directed feedback in the presence of negative self-directed feedback, enhanced activity in the dorsomedial prefrontal cortex was associated with lower self-reported difficulties in emotion regulation and more partner-rated support provided in everyday life. Taken together, these findings suggest that salient self-focused information may dampen individuals’ emotional and neural responses to their partners’ uplifting and critical feedback, that emotion regulation tendencies may be an important between-subjects moderator of this effect, and that individual differences in spontaneous neural responses during this task were associated with meaningful real-world outcomes, although further validation and replication are warranted.

articles.title: Context-Specific Mentalizing in Early Parenthood: An Exploratory Cross-Sectional Study Comparing Parental and Romantic Partner Domains
articles.authors: Karolin Holy; Tobias Nolte; Sebastian Walter; Sarah Kittel-Schneider; Martin Walter; Anna Linda Leutritz
articles.abstract:
Abstract
Background: Mentalizing — the capacity to interpret behavior in terms of mental states — is assumed to vary across relational contexts, yet empirical comparisons between parenting- and partner-focused mentalizing in early parenthood are scarce.
Method: In an exploratory online study, 110 first-time parents of children under three years (n = 60 complete cases) completed parallel, context-adapted versions of the Parent Reflective Functioning Questionnaire (PRFQ) and selected OPD-Structure Questionnaire (OPD-SQ) subscales regarding child and partner. Additional measures included parental stress, depressive symptoms, attachment insecurity, global mentalizing difficulties, and relationship satisfaction. Analyses used paired t-tests, correlations, and multiple regressions.
Results: Mentalizing differed systematically by relational context. Across PRFQ subscales, parents showed lower partner-focused mentalizing than child-focused (more pre-mentalizing, higher certainty, lower curiosity for the partner). OPD indices indicated greater impairment in impulse control and self–other differentiation in the partner context, while affect tolerance was more impaired toward the child. Context-specific mentalizing correlated with global mentalizing difficulties, attachment insecurity, depressive symptoms, and parental stress (r = .42–.73). Child-focused mentalizing impairments were predicted by higher stress and depressive symptoms, whereas partner-focused mentalizing was linked to lower relationship satisfaction and depressive symptoms. Larger partner–child discrepancies were associated with higher parental stress and lower relationship satisfaction.
Conclusion: Mentalizing in early parenthood is dyad-specific and systematically lower in the couple domain, indicating a potential vulnerability even in well-functioning families. Brief self-reports can help identify context-dominant mentalizing profiles to guide tailored support of parenting and couple functioning in early interventions.
This manuscript has been submitted for publication and has not yet undergone peer review.

articles.title: Decision-making in Highly Sensitive Person: A Qualitative Research
articles.authors: Yee Lam So; Samuel Bennett
articles.abstract:
This UK-based qualitative study explored sensory processing sensitivity (SPS), decision-making and self-regulation in highly sensitive persons (HSP) using the revised HSP Scale (Pluess et al., 2024) and semi-structured interviews. Data were analysed using descriptive statistics and reflexive thematic analysis (Braun & Clarke, 2022).

30 valid HSP Scale responses were obtained. Scores were broadly continuous (M = 5.24, SD = 0.98). 20 participants were classified as HSP due to the recruitment strategy, alongside six medium-sensitive and four low-sensitive; no clear boundary separated the groups. Two HSP types emerged within the HSP group (M = 5.84, SD = 0.42): Type One comprised the majority, with one to two sensitivity dimensions, whereas Type Two had three to four. Females were the majority in both types, while Type Two consisted of fewer participants and exhibited higher, broader, and more balanced mean scores. 

16 interviews were conducted, generating three themes: (a) Negative Experiences and SPS Changeability, (b) Factors act as both facilitators and barriers, and (c) Binary Tendency in Self-Regulation and Decision Control. Seven factors were identified as influencing HSP decision-making: "negative experiences", "surrounding people", "depth of processing", "emotional states", "physical overstimulation", "time", and "information". The study also questioned the revised HSP Scale’s comprehensiveness due to its lack of negative-experience items and limited capacity to identify heterogeneity among HSP.

Self-regulation was primarily employed pre-and-post decision-making, with less engagement during plan execution. Despite reporting feelings of overwhelm, most participants expressed confidence in making high-quality decisions through reflective processing and specific decision-control strategies.

articles.title: Teacher Dashboard Use in Physics Classes: Effects of a Brief Informational–Motivational Intervention on Use and Students’ Performance
articles.authors: Katharina Marie Bach; Natalia Revenga-Lozano; Karina E. Avila; Stefan Küchemann; Steffen Steinert; Jochen Kuhn; Sarah Isabelle Hofer
articles.abstract: Dashboards collect students’ data from digital learning environments and present it to teachers in an accessible format. Despite their advantages in supporting classroom monitoring and adaptive teaching, dashboards remain underused. The present experimental study investigated the effects of presenting a short informational–motivational video to physics teachers, highlighting the value of dashboards and encouraging their use. Using data from 26 teachers and 314 eleventh-grade students, we examined teachers’ self-reported targeted use of the dashboard, their tracked log-ins, and their association with students’ posttest performance. Moreover, we researched whether the video activated teachers’ technology- or pedagogy-related teaching knowledge and, consequently, students’ posttest performance. Therefore, we qualitatively built competence profiles based on teachers’ TPACK scores. Our results indicated that teachers who watched the video used the dashboard significantly more often than the control group and were more likely to belong to the pedagogically competent profile. Both effects were associated with students’ higher post-test performance. Moreover, we explored how teachers used the dashboard, noting that all of them consulted it before or after the lesson, but not during. While they considered data at both the student and class levels to roughly the same extent, the instructional interventions focused exclusively on class-level data rather than on individual students. Our findings indicate that, in line with the Expectancy-Value Theory, convincing teachers of the value of dashboards can increase their use and foster their self-reported pedagogical competences, thereby leading to enhanced student outcomes. Given teachers’ busy schedules, integrating short yet effective prompts and reminders seems advisable.

articles.title: Parental Engagement and Disaffection in Education: Development of two Multidimensional Parent-Report Measures using Qualitative Methods
articles.authors: Richard Anthony Inman; Carina Mota Santos; Telmo Fernandes; Sofia Mendes
articles.abstract: Children and adolescents benefit in various ways when their parents are supportive of their education at home and work in healthy collaboration with schools and teachers. Yet, schools still often struggle to optimize parent engagement. In part, this may be because there is still little consensus on what it means for a parent to be engaged. Mostly, researchers and practitioners have defined parent engagement in terms of parent actions and practices. In this article, we broaden the discourse of parent influence on their children in behavioral terms by (1) considering how parent engagement manifests in parents’ thoughts, feelings and relationships; and (2) proposing that parent engagement should be considered in combination with parent disaffection. To this end, the first aim of this study was to use qualitative methods to understand how parents, teachers, and students conceptualize parent engagement and parent disaffection in child education. We conducted focus groups with parents (N = 26), teachers (N = 23) and students (5th – 12th graders; N = 28), specifically probing how individuals understood the phenomena in terms of parents’ thoughts, feelings, and actions. A thematic analysis of qualitative data supported a multidimensional dual-component framework, although participants found it easiest to describe engagement and disaffection in behavioral terms. The second aim of this study was to describe how qualitative information from focus groups was used to develop two new parent-report scales: the Multidimensional Measure of Parent Engagement (MMPE) and partner Multidimensional Measure of Parent Disaffection (MMPD).

articles.title: Heterogeneous, Adaptive Networks of Beliefs
articles.authors: Peter Steiglechner; Victor Poulsen; Mirta Galesic; Henrik Olsson
articles.abstract: People's beliefs about social or political issues can change in different ways, even when they encounter the same new information. Some people show compliance through lasting belief change, others show resilience through temporary belief change, and yet others show resistance by not changing their beliefs at all. Existing explanations often focus on belief content, evidence, motivation, personality, or social influence. We introduce Adaptive Networks of Beliefs, a theory describing how beliefs and belief relations co-evolve through internal and social adaptation. Internal adaptation changes belief relations based on the individual's belief changes. Social adaptation aligns belief relations across individuals. Simulations show that internal adaptation generates heterogeneous belief-network structures, causing individuals with similar targeted beliefs to show compliance, resilience, or resistance under the same external pressure. These response patterns depend on how the targeted belief is embedded in the belief network before the pressure is applied. Compliance is associated with less balanced, less clustered, and more dissonant belief networks. Resistance is associated with stronger connectedness and balance around the targeted belief. Social adaptation reduces belief-network heterogeneity but changes response patterns non-monotonically. Weak to moderate social adaptation increases compliance, whereas stronger social adaptation increases resilience. Finally, external pressure affects not only the targeted belief but also how it is embedded, and this affects future belief change. Our results illustrate how individual differences in belief change can arise from adaptive belief-network structures itself. Explaining why people's beliefs show compliance, resilience, or resistance therefore requires understanding how these networks change through internal and social adaptation.

articles.title: Comparing the Beta-Binomial and the Linear Models to Analyze Discrete, Bounded Scores for Normative Data from Neuropsychological Tests
articles.authors: Javier Oltra-Cucarella; Rafael de Andrade Moral; Rubén Pérez-Elvira; Beatriz Bonete-López; Miriam Sánchez-Sansegundo; Esther Sitges-Maciá
articles.abstract: Using data from 165 cognitively healthy individuals, we compared the linear model with the binomial and the beta-binomial models to interpret performance on a verbal memory test. Model adequacy was assessed using half-normal plots with a simulated envelope. Goodness-of-fit was assessed using AIC and BIC. Four examples were developed to show the advantages of the beta-binomial model. Compared to the linear model (BIC = 583.4), the binomial models, especially the beta-binomial, showed better model fit to the real data (BIC = 463.6), did not predicted scores without the real range, and showed a better model adequacy (points out of the simulated envelope for linear = 32.73%, beta-binomial = 1.82%). The beta-binomial model is more adequate than the linear model for discrete, bounded data from neuropsychological tests. R scripts are provided for researchers and clinicians to calculate randomized quantile residuals from beta-binomial models

articles.title: “I would not have done that”: Outcome knowledge distorts memory for decisions made minutes ago
articles.authors: Keishiro Sawa; Carla Zoe Cremer; Tobias Gerstenberg; Sanjay G Manohar; Matan Mazor
articles.abstract: Remembering one’s past decisions is critical for learning from delayed feedback and maintaining an accurate self-model. Across two gamified experimental paradigms and three experiments (total N = 500), we find that a selective forgetting of unsuccessful guesses operates within minutes, that it evades introspection, and that it affects confidence ratings in memory judgments. Analysis of memory errors reveals that, in reconstructing their past decisions, people integrate what they remember deciding, what they believe they would have decided, and what they would decide now if asked to make the same decision again.

articles.title: Linking Stress Reactivity to Changes in Health – Different Approaches (Sometimes) Yield Different Conclusions
articles.authors: Julia Sophia Neschen; Anne Grünert; Andreas B. Neubauer; Anna Jori Lücke
articles.abstract:
A growing body of research examines whether short-term within-person affect dynamics, such as stress reactivity, are associated with changes in health. However, this broad question can be addressed using different study designs and statistical models and it is somewhat unclear to what extent researcher degrees of freedom on choosing among these might affect the conclusions regarding the longitudinal link between stress reactivity and health. In the present work, we examined the robustness of conclusions across various data analytic decisions and study designs using data from a measurement-burst study. Data were collected in four bursts from 2023 – 2024, each of which covered 21 days (with up to four daily beeps and an end-of-day assessment) from a sample of N = 743 young adults (16-39 years old, 70 % female) in Germany. We employed 18 multilevel structural equation models representing different combinations of researchers’ degrees of freedom, including daily vs. momentary stress reactivity, stationarity of effects, and different ways of considering mean levels of negative affect. Due to limited ethnic and socioeconomic diversity, generalization to other populations is limited. The findings suggest reciprocal associations between stress reactivity and self-reported health over several months and illustrate the incremental predictive validity of stress reactivity beyond mean levels of negative affect. The pattern of results differed when reciprocal links were assumed to be stable vs. allowed to vary over time. The study highlights the importance of carefully considering study designs and analytical approaches when investigating affect dynamics and changes in long-term developments across multiple timescales.

Keywords: Stress reactivity; health; ambulatory assessment; experience sampling; measurement burst

articles.title: The role of linguistic knowledge in verbal fluency tests: How individual differences in language skills shape the mental lexicon
articles.authors: Kyla McConnell; Berit Reise; antje meyer
articles.abstract: “List as many words as you can that start with M.” The verbal fluency (VF) task is simple, yet even a typical university student only manages to produce about 15 words within one minute, and there is substantial variability around this mean. The present study examined how linguistic and domain-general abilities contributed to verbal fluency performance in a large sample of healthy adult native speakers of Dutch (N = 571). We assessed the effects of linguistic knowledge, processing speed, short-term/working memory, and fluid intelligence on performance in the VF task. To examine whether linguistic and domain-general abilities contribute differently across VF task types, we included semantic trials (category-based generation: animals, food) and phonemic trials (letter-based generation: words beginning with S or M). We assessed the total number of correct words produced and the time to first response. Mixed effects modeling showed that linguistic knowledge predicted the total number of correct responses in both semantic and phonemic VF. Short-term/working memory and processing speed were also significant predictors, but with smaller estimated effect sizes. Time to first response showed little effect of linguistic skills. We discuss how linguistic knowledge shapes the structure of the mental lexicon such that it affects both meaning-driven and form-driven access to lexical items. In addition, we provide updated norms for VF performance in Dutch and practical suggestions for using the task.

articles.title: More effort, more variability: Distributional modeling of effort-performance coupling
articles.authors: Lukas Hack; Katja Rewitz; Wanja Wolff
articles.abstract: Ratings of perceived exertion (RPE) are widely used to prescribe exercise intensities. When used this way, researchers and practitioners may implicitly or explicitly rely on assumptions about the coupling between effort and performance. These assumptions refer to two dimensions: the average shape of how effort translates to performance and the variability of performance across effort levels. Straightforwardly, effort-performance coupling can be thought of as linear, with constant variability across the intensity spectrum. However, classic psychophysical scaling laws (e.g., Fechner’s law) and motor control research (e.g., signal-dependent noise) predict that such coupling is non-linear, with intensity-contingent increases in variability. These assumptions are rarely made explicit and have not been jointly tested. To address this, we fitted and compared theory-driven Bayesian multilevel models reflecting distinct assumptions about the mean and the variability structure, using leave-one-out cross-validation for model selection. Data were collected from 101 experienced climbers performing an isometric handgrip task with effort prescribed using Borg’s CR-10 scale. We found robust evidence for a heteroscedastic variability structure, with a systematic increase in force variability with prescribed RPE levels. Additionally, the model comparison favored an exponential mean model over a linear mean model, indicating that force production increases disproportionately with prescribed effort. These effects were not gender specific and are broadly consistent with Fechner’s psychophysical scaling law and signal-dependent noise theory. Together, these findings suggest that a direct translation from effort to performance systematically misrepresents the actual relationship. Instead, practitioners and researchers should expect a disproportionate increase in performance with increasing performance variability at higher effort levels.

articles.title: Prodigious calculation without prodigious basics: Single-digit number processing in a world-record mental calculator
articles.authors: Krzysztof Cipora; Mateusz Hohol; Katharina Sautter; Venera Gashaj; Gert Mittring; Klaus Willmes; Hans-Christoph Nuerk
articles.abstract: Calculation prodigies achieve feats of mental arithmetic far beyond ordinary capacity, yet whether their skill rests on qualitatively distinct elementary number processing or on quantitative amplification of typical mechanisms remains unresolved. Hallmark effects of elementary symbolic number processing have rarely been examined in this population. In a preregistered single-case study, we tested Gert Mittring, a calculation prodigy holding multiple world records, on parity judgment (PJ) and magnitude classification tasks (MC) using single-digit Arabic numerals. His performance was compared with that of four control groups: professional mathematicians, engineers, social sciences/humanities professionals, and an unselected reference sample. To examine basic number processing in this study, we assessed the SNARC (PJ, MC), MARC (PJ, MC), Odd (PJ), Numerical Distance (MC), and Numerical Size (PJ, MC) effects, evaluating their presence at the individual level using bootstrap confidence intervals and comparing the prodigy with each control group using Crawford modified t-tests. In parity judgment, the prodigy showed robust MARC, Odd, and Size effects, but no SNARC effect. In magnitude classification, he showed robust SNARC and Distance effects, but neither a MARC nor a Size effect. Descriptively, he responded faster than the average participant in every control group, with low intraindividual variability, at the cost of slightly lower accuracy; crucially, all his scores fell within the range observed in controls. These findings indicate that exceptional calculation skill in this case is not grounded in qualitatively distinct elementary number processing; rather, it likely emerges at higher levels of the processing hierarchy.

articles.title: Quantifying the impact of careless responding
articles.authors: Max Welz; Aurore Archimbaud; Andreas Alfons
articles.abstract: Responses to rating-scale items are often plagued by biases stemming from careless responding. While there is consensus that carelessness can jeopardize the validity of survey measures through a variety of psychometric issues, the question when carelessness becomes a serious problem is less well studied. Existing literature on this question are based on simulations, which are always limited to certain design choices. We leverage statistical robustness theory to derive analytical expressions for the bias  caused by careless responding, with a focus on biases in Pearson's correlation coefficient. We find that carelessness incidence rates of as low as 5% can suffice to cause sign flips in correlational estimates. Furthermore, we show that the impact of carelessness varies by the type of carelessness and that psychometric scales that are designed to be reliable are those most vulnerable to the adverse effects of careless responding. Finally, we provide a free web application  that lets users explore the impact of carelessness.

articles.title: @Grok Is This True? LLM-Powered Fact-Checking on Social Media
articles.authors: Thomas Renault; Mohsen Mosleh; David Gertler Rand
articles.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly embedded directly into social media platforms, enabling users to request real-time fact-checks of online content. Using an exhaustive dataset of 1,671,841 English-language fact-checking requests made to Grok and Perplexity on X between February and September 2025, we provide the first large-scale empirical analysis of how LLM-based fact-checking operates in the wild. Fact-checking requests comprise 7.6% of all interactions with the LLM bots, and focus primarily on politics, economics, and current events. We document clear partisan asymmetries in usage. Users requesting fact-checks from Grok are much more likely to be Republican than Democratic, while the opposite is true for fact-check requests from Perplexity -- indicating emerging polarization in attitudes toward specific AI models. At the same time, both Democrats and Republicans are more likely to request fact-checks on posts authored by Republicans, and - consistent with prior work using professional fact-checkers and crowd judgments - posts from Republican-leaning accounts are more likely to be rated as inaccurate by both LLMs. Across posts rated by both LLM bots, evaluations from Grok and Perplexity agree 52.6% of the time and strongly disagree (one party rates a claim as true and the other as false) 13.6% of the time. For a sample of 100 fact-checked posts, 54.5% of Grok bot ratings and 57.7% of Perplexity bot ratings agreed with ratings of human fact-checkers, which is significantly lower than the inter-fact-checker agreement rate of 64.0%; but API-access versions of Grok had higher agreement with fact-checkers than did not significantly differ from inter-fact-checker agreement. Finally, in a preregistered survey experiment with 1,592 U.S. participants, exposure to LLM fact-checks meaningfully shifts belief accuracy, with effect sizes comparable to those observed in studies of professional fact-checking. However, responses to Grok fact-checks are polarized by partisanship when model identity is disclosed, whereas responses to Perplexity are not. Together, these findings show that LLM-based fact-checking is rapidly scaling, is generally informative although far from perfect, while also becoming entangled with polarization and partisanship. Our work highlights both the promise and the risks of integrating AI fact-checking into online public discourse.

articles.title: Mapping nonordinary experiences in a representative German sample: Phenomenology, prevalence and links to self-transcendence
articles.authors: Marc Wittmann; Julie Papastamatelou; Sepideh Ravahi; Liane Hofmann; Cecilia Bastos
articles.abstract: This study investigated the prevalence, phenomenology, and potential transformative significance of nonordinary experiences in a representative German sample. Across cultures and historical periods, such experiences have been widely reported and often culturally valued. To assess these phenomena, the study employed the Inventory of Nonordinary Experiences (INOE; Taves et al., 2023), a phenomenologically grounded instrument that focuses on subjective experiential qualities rather than explanatory frameworks. The INOE was administered alongside two validated measures: the Questionnaire on the Phenomenology of Exceptional Experiences (PExE-II), which assesses frequency and types of anomalous experiences, and the Inventory of Secular/Spiritual Wakefulness (WAKE-16), which measures positive self-transformation. Data from 440 participants revealed that nonordinary experiences are widely distributed. Exceptionally strong emotional experiences (e.g., awe, love, loss) were most frequently reported, while explicitly anomalous phenomena (e.g., telepathy, sensed presences) were less common but still notable, with prevalence rates of 10–30%. Correlational analyses showed positive associations between INOE domains and PExE-II dimensions, indicating that experiences more prevalent in the population tend to occur more frequently within individuals. In structural equation modelling, the INOE emerged as a significant positive predictor of the WAKE-16, indicating an association between nonordinary experiences and self-transcendent personality development. Overall, the findings support the perspective that nonordinary experiences constitute a widespread and meaningful aspect of human experience. While frequently interpreted within culture-specific and spiritual frameworks, they may play a role in psychological growth and transformation. The study further acknowledges methodological limitations and underscores the need for additional research into contextual and causal mechanisms.

articles.title: Loneliness and Social Isolation Among Older Adults in Slovakia: Prevalence, Sociodemographic Determinants, and Health-Related Correlates in a Population-Based Sample
articles.authors: Petra Brandoburová; Simona Krakovska; Marianna Mrva; Michal Hajdúk; Jakub Januška
articles.abstract:
Background
Loneliness and social isolation are increasingly recognised as significant public health challenges associated with adverse mental and physical health outcomes in older populations. This study aimed to examine the prevalence of loneliness and social isolation among older adults in Slovakia and to identify the sociodemographic and health-related factors associated with these phenomena.
Methods
Data were analysed from 664 adults aged 65 years and older participating in a population-based epidemiological study conducted in Slovakia in 2024. Loneliness was assessed using the 3-item UCLA Loneliness Scale, and social isolation was measured using the Lubben Social Network Scale (LSNS-6). Sociodemographic characteristics, health indicators, physical functioning, and physical activity were examined as factors associated with loneliness and social isolation. Binary logistic regression was used to examine factors associated with loneliness and social isolation.
Results
The prevalence of loneliness was 36.7% and of social isolation 24.5%. Loneliness was associated with lower education, divorced or widowed status, functional limitations, and poorer subjective mental health. Social isolation was associated with being single, lower physical activity, and treatment for mental health disorders. The two outcomes were also significantly associated.
Conclusion
Loneliness and social isolation affect a substantial proportion of older adults in Slovakia and are shaped by overlapping yet distinct sociodemographic and health-related factors. Their differential patterns of associated factors underscore the importance of distinguishing between these constructs in both research and clinical practice. Targeted public health strategies are needed to promote social participation, subjective mental health, and the ability to live independently.

articles.title: Predicting children’s early word learning using egocentric videos of their learning environments
articles.authors: Alvin Wei Ming Tan; Khai Loong Aw; Tarun Sepuri; Robert Zane Sparks; Jane Yang; Virginia A. Marchman; Bria Long; Michael C. Frank
articles.abstract: How do features of children's environmental input predict their word learning? Prior research has suggested that distributional features of the language children hear (e.g., frequency and syntactic complexity) predict variation in early word learning, but these studies have typically relied on aggregated estimates from disjunct samples to calculate input features and word learning outcomes. In this study, we use child-specific input features derived from 1118h of at-home egocentric video recordings to show that word frequency, length in phonemes, and syntactic complexity predict word knowledge within individual children (N = 29). The predictive power of these within-child features was greater than features aggregated across children, supporting theories positing a direct relationship between input distributions and children's language learning. Exploratory analyses involving object frequency, word–object cooccurrences, and context distinctiveness did not reveal any additional effects of these predictors. The use of child-specific naturalistic data provides an avenue for more precise and comprehensive investigations of input–outcome relationships for word learning, allowing researchers to adjudicate among theories of language learning in young children.

articles.title: From Social Interaction to Self-Evaluation: Attentional Mechanisms Underlying Post-Event Rumination in Social Anxiety
articles.authors: Binghui Li; Justina Tan Sue Ching; Kean J. Hsu
articles.abstract: Background: Post-event processing, a form of repetitive negative thinking that serves as a key maintenance factor in social anxiety, has been suggested to be connected with attentional bias to threat and negative self-evaluation. However, prior work has relied on stimuli and tasks that limit ecological validity and generalizability to real-world social experiences. This study examined how attentional bias, negative self-evaluation, and 24-hour post-event rumination are associated when socially anxious individuals undergo a brief dyadic social interaction. Method: A total of 166 participants (83 dyads, final N = 161; 81 participants with Social Anxiety Disorder and 80 healthy control participants) completed a dyadic interaction, subsequent interaction recording review, and a performance evaluation task. Participants rated self- and partner-interaction performance while gaze behavior was tracked. Results: Consistent with hypotheses, socially anxious participants reported higher state anxiety before and after the interaction and elevated post-event rumination relative to controls. Eye-tracking results revealed a significant attentional bias toward negative self-evaluation among socially anxious participants relative to controls, suggesting a self-focused attentional style that may amplify fear of negative evaluation. Both groups showed an attentional preference toward positive evaluation anchors as well. Associational variable analyses indicated that attentional bias at least partially accounted for the association between post-interaction state anxiety and post-event rumination. Discussion: These findings suggest that the cognitive mechanisms underlying state anxiety and post-event rumination in social anxiety extend beyond attentional bias. Findings suggest evaluative attention and distorted self-appraisals as key factors in the maintenance of social anxiety.

articles.title: What Sticks and What Doesn’t: How Do Children Process Irrelevant Information?
articles.authors: Gülce Akin; Lars-Michael Schöpper; Tarini; Christian Frings; Yana Fandakova
articles.abstract: Across childhood, improvements in action control enable children to act increasingly in a goal-directed manner. The present study investigated the degree to which the similarity of task-irrelevant information influences stimulus-response bindings and action control during development. To this end, we examined age differences in the generalization of stimulus-response bindings as a function of the similarity of irrelevant features in children between 4 and 10 years (N = 96) and adults (N = 40). Participants completed a prime-probe stimulus-response task in which two different monsters served as target stimuli, while their color, varying across five shades of grey, served as a distractor. Results showed robust stimulus-response binding effects across all age groups. Binding effects in error rates were comparable across groups; however, younger children showed stronger binding effects in response times than older children and adults. Compared to older children and adults, younger children showed a more pronounced decrease in distractor-response binding effects as distractor dissimilarity increased, indicating that they were more strongly affected by mismatches in task-irrelevant information between prime and probe trials. Together, these findings suggest that younger children’s stimulus-response binding effects are more sensitive to changes in irrelevant information, whereas older children are better able to flexibly update and adapt action control in response to changing stimulus contexts.

articles.title: Getting to the causes of depression: regime-dependent causal discovery of an N-of-1 study
articles.authors: Pedro Henrique Ribeiro Santiago; Alyssa Sawyer; Andreas Bauer; Elaine Fox; Lisa Jamieson
articles.abstract: Depression is a global public health challenge, with 332 million people experiencing depression. Given that more than half of depression patients experience relapse, researchers have argued that investigating the causes of depression at a symptom (rather than syndrome) level is required to progress understanding. This study investigated the causal structure of depressive symptoms using data from a study that followed a 57-year-old individual over 239 consecutive days. The study followed the analytical steps: (1) descriptive analysis; (2) regime identification; (3) regime-dependent causal discovery; (4) causal model estimation; and (5) centrality analysis. The findings indicated the existence of two regimes, aligned with pre- and post-transition to depressive symptoms. In the second regime (where symptom severity increased), deficits in concentration and low self-esteem constituted intervention targets. The current study showed that state-of-the-art causal methods can be used to gain new insights into causal processes underlying depression and inform personalised mental health interventions.

articles.title: Novel Word Learning from Social Robots by Autistic and Non-Autistic Children
articles.authors: Kristyn Sommer; Sarah Russell; Jessica Paynter
articles.abstract: This pre-registered study examined whether autistic preschool children could learn novel words from a social robot during a shared eBook reading session, how their engagement with the robot relates to word learning, and whether their performance differs from that of non-autistic peers. Thirty-one autistic preschoolers from an early intervention service completed a structured robot-mediated eBook session introducing four novel words, followed by a 12-trial two-alternative forced-choice receptive test, with engagement coded from video across five channels (Proximity, Eye Contact, Touch, Communication, and Emotion). Comparison data from 38 non-autistic children were collected using identical methodology in daycare settings, and all hypotheses were pre-registered (OSF: https://osf.io/sg79u/?view_only=55e46ce76b4c41c18eecade810277599). Word identification accuracy was significantly above chance in both groups, with no significant group difference. Pre-registered generalized linear mixed models found no significant association between any engagement channel and word learning in autistic children, although an exploratory group × touch interaction indicated that greater touch-based engagement was associated with lower accuracy specifically in autistic children. Non-autistic children showed higher eye contact and communication engagement, but the groups did not differ in overall engagement. Among autistic preschoolers able to participate in structured robot-mediated tasks, word learning appears comparable to that of non-autistic peers. The absence of engagement–learning associations suggests either that conventional behavioral engagement metrics do not capture what drives learning, or that structured story-based robot sessions support learning independently of engagement, both of which warrant mechanistic research before such interventions are scaled.

articles.title: Reading Anxiety in Children and Adolescents and Its Negative Association With Word Reading Performance
articles.authors: Silvia Siu Yin Clement-Lam; Deborah Anne Schneider-Richardson; Allison Kalva; Celinene M. Lay; Brianna Paquette; Caroline G. Richter; Devin M. Kearns; Fumiko Hoeft
articles.abstract: Over the past decade, research on reading development and reading disorders has expanded beyond its traditional focus on cognitive-linguistic factors to give increasing attention to socioemotional functioning. The present study leveraged two existing datasets (112 elementary school children aged 7 to 10 years and 110 early-and-mid adolescents aged 11 to 15 years) to examine various dimensions of reading anxiety (RA), including cognitive symptoms, physiological symptoms, and behavioral avoidance, as well as the overall RA effects. Our findings revealed that (1) RA was prevalent among both children and adolescents, although correlations among RA dimensions were stronger in adolescents than in children, (2) higher RA was moderately associated with lower word reading performance in both age groups, (3) no significant differences in RA were observed between children and adolescents or between girls and boys, and effects were not moderated by reading performance, and, lastly, (4) RA was negatively associated with reading performance across levels of reading ability, and this association was not stronger among readers at the lower end of the distribution. These findings reinforce the observed moderate negative association between RA and reading performance in prior research and suggest that RA may affect individuals across reading levels and developmental periods.
