Predictions derived from GPT-4, whose training-data cutoff predated the publication of many studies in our archive, were strongly correlated with actual treatment effects, achieving accuracy similar to pooled human forecasts. Correlations remained high for studies not published or publicly posted by the model’s training-data cutoff date, and for predictions from prominent open-weight models. Despite high correlations, predictions systematically overestimated effect sizes. In a secondary archive of 15 megastudies featuring 606 effects, correlations were lower but comparable to pooled expert forecasters. To assess implications for scientific practice, we surveyed 460 social scientists about likely uses and perceived risks, and used our archives to assess several applications (pilot testing, intervention selection, identifying effects needing replication) and risks (bias, misuse). Together, these results suggest LLMs can augment experimental methods in science and practice while raising important considerations for responsible use.

articles.title: Key Concepts in Online Learning and Decision Making for Personalizing Just-in-Time Adaptive Interventions (pJITAIs)
articles.authors: Asim H. Gazi; Susobhan Ghosh; Yongyi Guo; Daiqi Gao; Ziping Xu; Inbal Nahum-Shani; Susan Murphy
articles.abstract:
Background: Just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) provide digital health support that is timely and adaptive: intervention delivery is tailored to internal and external factors that may change rapidly, known as “tailoring variables.” JITAIs make decisions on how to tailor support using “decision rules.” Decision rules specify which intervention option should be selected (e.g., whether to send a text-based intervention message) based on tailoring variables. JITAI decision rules are typically designed via expert opinion – informed by domain knowledge and statistical analyses of past data – and kept the same throughout a JITAI’s deployment. A challenge for JITAIs is that decision rules informed by past data can become stale or outdated for several reasons, including differences between recipients of a JITAI from one deployment to the next and broader societal shifts (e.g., post-pandemic behavior shifts). As a result, JITAIs may suffer from reduced effectiveness in deployment.  

Purpose: Herein, we describe how online learning and decision-making algorithms create “personalizing JITAIs” (pJITAIs) to address this gap, enabling pre-specified, within-deployment modifications to decision rules based on streaming data collected during deployment. This streaming data provides up-to-date insights on how relationships between tailoring variables, intervention options, and outcomes may differ. 

Methods: We detail key concepts for online learning and decision-making algorithms, unifying two dominant perspectives in the pJITAI literature: reinforcement learning (RL) and control engineering. 

Conclusions: Although RL and control engineering approaches have generally progressed in parallel, we elucidate their substantial overlap and unify terminology to streamline the collaborative design of online learning and decision-making algorithms for pJITAIs.

articles.title: Text-measured cognitive complexity predicts belief revision in AI persuasion
articles.authors: Tamas Madl
articles.abstract: Conversational AI can durably change what people believe, and recent large-scale experiments report these effects as roughly uniform across users, treating benefit and risk as properties of the system rather than the user. Reanalysing Costello et al.'s 1{,}782 human--AI conspiracy-debunking dialogues, we find that integrative complexity---a text-measurable cognitive style indexing how readers differentiate and integrate competing perspectives---moderates belief revision in an inverted-U ($\beta_{\text{IC}^2} = -15.17$, BF$_{10} = 1{,}086$); about half persists within conspiracy-topic fixed effects, indicating the effect is not reducible to topic sorting. Among Costello's 24 pre-specified candidates, only trust-type variables replicably moderated, and trust does not index cognitive processing capacity. Mid-complexity users revise beliefs most; revision attenuates at both ends, consistent with McGuire's reception--yielding trade-off: at low IC, a reception bottleneck on evidence-dense argument; at high IC, reduced revision when new evidence is reconciled with an already-integrated belief structure. In the lowest-complexity fifth of users, the AI conversation reinforces conspiracy belief about as often as it produces substantial revision; as a proof-of-concept for user-aware safeguards, flagging this fifth would preserve 86\% of substantial revisions while capturing 25\% of adverse cases in leave-one-study-out cross-validation. Because the signal is measured from text users already produce, it supports AI conversations adapted to the individual rather than delivered uniformly---a candidate for the user-aware evaluation layer that current AI-safety frameworks have called for but not yet operationalised.

articles.title: Moving on from simple operationalism: Anchoring theoretical constructs in rich relational models
articles.authors: Peter D. Kvam; Abhay Alaukik
articles.abstract: Operational definitions have long been a core approach to measuring and relating observed data to theoretical constructs in psychological science. However, many contemporary modeling approaches conflict with the tacit assumptions of operationalism as it is implemented in psychology. Counterintuitively, these departures imbue model-based definitions of constructs with superior measurement properties, such as improved reliability and validity, when compared to their operationalizations based directly on observables. Instead of relying on operational definitions of constructs, we instead suggest that psychology should adopt relational definitions or relational operationalizations, representing constructs as latent variables in a multilevel generative model of behavior, self-report, or neuroimaging data. These model-based metrics can better reflect measurement error at multiple levels, account for the interactions between measurement devices (tasks, scales) and measurement objects (participants, processes), provide a holistic account of latent constructs and how they manifest across different measurements, facilitate convergence or discrimination tests among different tasks seeking to measure the same construct, and improve scientific communication by clarifying core psychological concepts. Relational definitions of important constructs should naturally emerge as we apply models more regularly, and these definitions and models will improve as we discover mathematical approaches that are suited to describing psychological processes.

articles.title: Appreciation of social experiences and solitude in everyday life: the SOcial experiences’ daiLy Occurrence scale (SOLO)
articles.authors: Daniela M Pfabigan; Erik Rimestad Frogner; Fatemeh Asgari; Uta Sailer
articles.abstract:
Introduction
While social interactions and actively chosen solitude both contribute to well-being, existing measures focus solely on one dimension. We developed the SOcial experiences' daiLy Occurrence (SOLO) scale—a brief end-of-day diary measure capturing enjoyment of social interactions (including brief encounters with "weak ties") and solitude experiences. 
Methods
Two scale development and validation studies were conducted: single-day (N=203) and 14-day (N=75) assessments. The final SOLO consists of 13 items forming two scales, the Social Interaction subscale and the Solitude subscale. 
Results
Both scales demonstrated adequate psychometric properties and meaningful relationships with well-being measures. The scales were independent, supporting conceptualization of social enjoyment and solitude preference as distinct dimensions. The SOLO is therefore presented as two distinct scales. A composite score can be calculated if required, although subscale-level analyses are recommended given the empirical independence of the two dimensions. 
Conclusion
The SOLO's brief format offers a practical compromise between ecological momentary assessment and retrospective measures, enabling investigation of the distinct roles of social engagement and solitude in everyday life.

articles.title: False discovery rate correction promotes confounded neuroimaging designs
articles.authors: Mark Allen Thornton
articles.abstract: False discovery rate (FDR) corrections are a popular and pragmatic approach to dealing with the problem of multiple comparisons in neuroimaging research. This paper describes a potentially problematic outcome of FDR correction. Specifically, the use of FDR will tend to promote the publication of poorly controlled experiments. This occurs because the presence of significant hypothesis tests in some parts of the brain will lower the threshold for significance elsewhere in the brain. In general, this adaptive thresholding is a feature of FDR. However, it can be problematic when a confounded design produces spurious activations, because they will lower the threshold for significance elsewhere in the brain, relative to a well-controlled study. Here I demonstrate this basic phenomenon using Monte Carlo simulations. I conduct further simulations to estimate how this phenomenon could affect more realistic confounding scenarios and how it could interact with publication bias. I conclude by suggesting mitigation approaches.

articles.title: The Case for a Neuropsychology of Artificial Agents
articles.authors: Taylor Kuhn; Gabriel Axel Montes; Leonardo Christov-Moore; Nicco Reggente; Anna Mikeda; Brandon In; Sarah Greif; Joseph M. Gullett
articles.abstract: Artificial agents now interact with hundreds of millions of people daily in settings of high consequence, including medicine, law, finance, and education. Yet, a validated psychometric framework has never been applied to any of these systems. The elements required to build such a measurement program are now in place: a substrate-agnostic validity methodology, a well-validated dimensional reference framework for affect and personality, mechanistic interpretability findings confirming that internal affective representations organize along valence-arousal axes and causally drive misaligned behavior, and prior theoretical frameworks that make this convergence theoretically intelligible rather than merely analogical. On this foundation, we propose agentic neuropsychology as one such approach: the extension of two methods of clinical neuropsychological assessment to artificial agents, namely latent-construct inference from behavioral output and, where interpretability access permits, direct measurement of internal state. Model activations and emotion vectors serve as the internal-state proxies that neural measures provide in human assessment. To operationalize this approach, we introduce the Nomos Battery as an initial five-domain instrument, the initial measurement framework for the psychometric infrastructure AI safety governance currently lacks.

articles.title: Learning to carve nature at its joints: the development of hierarchical event structures in perception and action
articles.authors: Tom Northrop; Andrew Tolmie; Sam Wass
articles.abstract: Adults have a semantically discretised, event-based perception of reality – such that events and goals are both hierarchically structured and have a beginning, middle and end. Here, we consider how we first develop the ability to parse reality hierarchically - as we learn, to use Plato’s phrase, to ‘carve nature at its joints’. Central to this process is the ability to make predictions on hierarchical timescales. We consider two aspects of predictability: first, the child’s passive capacity to use predictability to inform how they passively observe things as they happen; and second, children’s active capacity to generate predictable sequences of behaviour. We highlight two themes that are, we argue, common across both passive observation and active behaviour. First, flat (i.e. non-hierarchical) predictability initially increases over development – as shown for example by increases in perseveration – before decreasing as hierarchical predictability (i.e. nested, multi-level goals) increase. Second, for both observing and acting, learnt regularities in low-level environmental data (e.g., movement, contrast, over fine-grained timescales) feed into our internal predictions of higher-order events (e.g., goals, over more coarse-grained timescales).

articles.title: Bot or not: Can people tell the difference between stories written by a human or by an AI system?
articles.authors: Sydney Sears; Deena Skolnick Weisberg
articles.abstract:
How do people perceive AI-generated creative content? In Study 1, participants (1,498 adults recruited from Prolific) read either a human-written or AI-generated story. Half were told that
their story was written by a human, and half were told that their story was written by ChatGPT. Participants rated the AI-generated story as higher quality and more absorbing, but they rated the
story better on both measures when they were told it was written by a human. In Studies 2 and 3, participants (905 adults recruited from Prolific) read both types of story and guessed each story’s
origins. Participants were no better than chance at differentiating between the human-written and AI-generated stories. Self-reported expertise with AI, but not with fictional literature, was
positively correlated with correct performance. AI programs thus can generate human-level creative work but are not perceived as being able to do so.

articles.title: Preschoolers search semantic networks in a broader and more variable way than adults: Implications for hypothesis generation
articles.authors: Elizabeth Bonawitz; Caren Walker; Joshua Abbott; Thomas L. Griffiths; alison gopnik
articles.abstract:
Here we explore whether adults and children differ in the way they search
through a semantic network, and the role those differences may play in hypothesis
generation. Participants generated sequential guesses about a novel causal relation.
A week later, they completed a similarity spacing task and we measured the average
“distance” between guesses. We find that adults show greater dependencies
between sequential guesses than preschoolers, and generate a less diverse set of
options. These findings may support the idea that development can be viewed as
analogous to simulated annealing strategies in machine learning that start “hot” (in
early childhood), generating wider and more variable searches, and eventually cool
(in adulthood) to generate narrower searches.

articles.title: Reducing Concerns about Falling in Older Adults: Effectiveness of a multi-component intervention and the role of stress and inflammation markers - Long-term effects of the FEARFALL study
articles.authors: Sabine Britting; Anja Müller; Robert Kob; Ellen Freiberger; Nicolas Rohleder
articles.abstract:
Background: Concerns about falling (CaF) are common in older adults and are associated with reduced activity and poorer health. The aim of this study was to investigate, for the first time, the long-term effects of a multi-component intervention program designed to reduce CaF on psychological, functional, biophysiological, and anthropometric aspects. 
Methods: 160 community-dwelling older adults (≥ 70 years) were randomly assigned to either a 4-month intervention featuring exercise and cognitive-behavioral training or to a sham control group. CaF, perceived stress, and other psychological symptoms were tracked by self-reports. Salivary cortisol and α-amylase were analyzed. Physical function, activity, and body composition were evaluated. Blood levels of C-reactive protein (CRP), interleukin-6 (IL-6), interleukin-10 (IL-10), interleukin-1β (IL-1β) and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) served as surrogate markers for inflammatory status, assessed at baseline, post-intervention, and at an 8-month follow-up.
Results: All estimates are T0–T2 estimated marginal mean differences. CaF, operationalized by Falls Efficacy Scale-International (FES-I) (estimate = 3.85, p < .001), FES-I Avoidance Behavior (FES-IAB) (estimate = 2.12, p = .001) and Updated Perceived Control over Falling (UP-CoF) (estimate = −1.68, p < .001) decreased over time with no between-group differences. Functional outcomes marginally improved throughout the study. Total diurnal output of cortisol decreased (estimate = 0.66, p = .017) and of α-amylase increased (estimate = -10.07, p = .013) significantly. Inflammatory marker changes present a non-cohesive picture, as CRP and IL-10 remain stable, IL-6 (estimate = -0.06, p = .002) and IL-1β (estimate = -0.05, p = .004) increased, and TNF-α (estimate = 0.09, p < .001) decreased. 
Conclusion: CaF improved to a similar extent in both the intervention and sham control groups. Similarly, changes in basal stress system activity and inflammatory cytokines were highly comparable between groups. Contrary to our primary hypothesis, the intervention did not confer additional benefits over the sham control intervention with regard to reductions in CaF.

Trial registration: German Clinical Trials Register (DRKS): DRKS00029171. Registered 22 July 2022, https://drks.de/search/de/trial/DRKS00029171.

Keywords
Older adults, concerns about falling, inflammation, community-dwelling, stress, cortisol

articles.title: Two practices that understate the mental health risks associated with adolescent social media use
articles.authors: Jonathan Haidt; Zachary M Rausch
articles.abstract: Why do researchers analyzing the same body of evidence reach different conclusions about whether social media use poses risks to adolescent mental health? We offer a partial explanation: two common practices can lead researchers to underestimate harm and reach divergent conclusions. First, studies often do not account for risk elevations, relying instead only on correlation coefficients, which are more prone to misinterpretation. Second, researchers often rely on highly blended analyses that combine heterogeneous exposures (e.g., multiple digital activities, including “TV watching”), outcomes (e.g., depression with life satisfaction), and populations (e.g., girls with boys), and then treat weak aggregate associations as evidence that specific risks are negligible. This is especially problematic when the central question is whether heavy social media use is associated with internalizing symptoms among adolescent girls. We illustrate how sequential blending can transform a large and clinically relevant association into a negligible one, which helps explain the persistent disagreement in the literature. We conclude by urging researchers to present their findings in more practically interpretable terms, including elevated risks, and to investigate heterogeneity across platforms, usage levels, populations, and outcomes.

articles.title: Playing, Reading, Learning, and Having Fun? Knowledge, Interest, and Emotions in Video Game and Text-based Environments
articles.authors: Amedeo Viccari; Anna Kienitz; Alexander Eitel; Jens-Uwe Hahn; Richard Göllner; Marlit Lindner; Lisa Bardach
articles.abstract:
Background: Digital games are promising tools for supporting knowledge acquisition, situational interest, and academic emotions in digital learning environments. However, evidence remains mixed regarding whether video games lead to stronger learning outcomes than text-based formats, and little is known about how instructional framing shapes learners’ engagement with such environments.

Objectives: This study investigated the effects of content format and instructional framing on factual knowledge, situational interest, and academic emotions. It compared a historically accurate video game (Limes) with a structurally aligned interactive text application; each paired with instructions to focus on either learning or fun.

Methods: In a 2 (format: game versus text) × 2 (instruction: learning versus fun) between-subjects design, 143 university students engaged with either the video game or the interactive text application. Both formats presented the same content through main narratives, optional missions, and supplemental information in the form of Codex entries. Outcomes included knowledge acquisition, situational interest, enjoyment, and boredom. Additional analyses examined behavioural data on Codex engagement.

Results and Conclusions: Participants in the game condition reported higher enjoyment and greater triggered situational interest, whereas knowledge scores were higher in the text condition. No statistically significant effects emerged for instructional framing or for the interaction between format and framing; however, text participants engaged more extensively with Codex entries, and Codex engagement strongly predicted knowledge outcomes across both formats. These findings suggest that games can enhance positive affective experiences, but learning outcomes may depend less on the format itself than on how supplemental learning materials are integrated.

articles.title: Heightened adaptability to environmental volatility in adolescence
articles.authors: Kate Nussenbaum; Hannah Hamling; Haoze Zhu; Linda Kerbl; Lia Washington; Catherine A. Hartley
articles.abstract: To learn to make good choices, learners must calibrate how quickly they update their beliefs based on the statistics of their environments: When environments change rapidly, people should base their choices on the most recent outcomes they have experienced, whereas when environments are more stable, people should use a longer history of experienced outcomes to guide their decisions. Prior research has revealed that adults dynamically adapt their learning computations in this way, and that such adaptation is a hallmark of healthy psychological functioning. To date, however, it is unclear how this adaptive calibration of reinforcement learning computations changes across adolescence. Here, 105 adolescents and adults (ages 12 - 25) completed a probabilistic learning task in which they learned to avoid aversive outcomes through trial and error, across environments with both stable and volatile reward contingencies. Computational modeling of participant choices revealed that younger participants demonstrated greater adaptation of learning computations across task contexts, placing more weight on recent outcomes and making noisier choices in the volatile versus stable environment. However, concurrent pupil size recordings during the task revealed that participants across age demonstrated similar sensitivity to reward uncertainty and outcome surprise during learning. Together, these findings suggest that adolescence may be characterized by heightened responsivity to environmental volatility, and lay the groundwork for future studies investigating how deviations in trajectories of adaptive learning may relate to the emergence of psychopathology.

articles.title: A large-scale investigation of domain-general and experience-based sources of individual differences in syntactic processing
articles.authors: Van Rynald T Liceralde; Michelle Perdomo; Anna Chrabaszcz; Ariel N. James; Tessa Warren; Doriana Lacitignola; Emily Phillips Galloway; Duane Watson
articles.abstract: Domain-general and experience-based theories of language processing predict distinct sources of individual differences in processing complex sentence structures (e.g., relative clauses and temporary ambiguities) during reading. Previous work testing these predictions has yielded mixed support for each class of theories, but most studies only focus on one class and are often psychometrically inadequate for studying individual differences in syntactic processing. Improving on these conditions, we report a large-scale individual-difference study on processing relative clauses and temporary ambiguities using self-paced reading, with a diverse sample of 300+ participants, 18 tasks measuring six constructs invoked by domain-general and experience-based theories, more experimental items than typical studies, test-retest measurements, and enhanced estimation and analysis techniques of people’s syntactic effects. Although we found the expected group-level syntactic effects and that these effects varied by age, the individual-difference constructs only modulated syntactic effects on comprehension accuracy but not the effects on reading times. These results are at odds with highly-cited foundational studies on individual differences in syntactic processing and are consistent with an alternative view that individual-difference tasks and reading tasks engage shared cognitive mechanisms differently. Moreover, although there was substantial variability in people’s syntactic effects, most effects remained unreliable, with only 6-60% of participants robustly showing the effects that were observed at the group level, depending on the syntactic structure and outcome measure. We discuss the implications of these results for future work on individual differences in syntactic processing and for theories of language processing, which often assume the generalizability of group-level syntactic effects.

articles.title: Shared Music Preference Enhances Social Bonding During Group Music Listening
articles.authors: Emily Wood; Rachael Finnerty; Rafael Román-Caballero; Cindy He; Laurel Trainor
articles.abstract: Music is a powerful social tool, and people tend to feel closer to others who share their music preference. Social bonding through music, however, often unfolds without explicit knowledge of others’ tastes. The present study tested whether shared music preference can promote social bonding during passive listening, even in the absence of such knowledge. Ninety-six undergraduate students were assigned to 24 four-person groups and listened to a playlist of either pop or K-pop. Within each group, all participants were either fans or nonfans of the genre they heard, resulting in four crossed conditions: pop fans, pop nonfans, K-pop fans, and K-pop nonfans. Social bonding was indexed through pre–post changes in interpersonal liking and perceived closeness, alongside measures of mood, cooperation (via a public goods game), and group movement synchrony derived from motion capture. Fans showed greater increases in both liking and closeness from pre- to post-listening than nonfans, as well as higher acceleration-based movement synchrony during listening. Mediation analyses indicated that movement synchrony accounted for the fan-group advantage in interpersonal liking, whereas increases in positive mood mediated the fan-group advantage in perceived closeness. No significant differences were found in cooperation. Together, these findings demonstrate that shared music preference can shape social bonding during passive listening, even without explicit awareness of others’ preferences, through coordinated movement and changes in affective state.

articles.title: Maternal Versus Paternal Adversity and Adolescent Psychopathology: Multi-Informant Evidence from the Millennium Cohort Study
articles.authors: Tochukwu Nweze; Elias Crawford; Sadie Werner; Adeola Mosunmola Olowokudejo
articles.abstract: Childhood adversity has been implicated in child and adolescent psychopathology. However, few studies have simultaneously compared the unique contributions of proximal maternal adversity vs paternal adversity, and youth self-reported adversity on adolescent externalizing and internalizing symptoms using multi-informant reports. In this study, using path model and data from the Millennium Cohort Study (N = 6,391; 51.1% female; M age = 17.16 years), we examined the associations of contemporaneous maternal vs paternal adversity (parent-reported at child age 3), youth self-reported adversity (age 14), and adolescent externalizing and internalizing symptoms (age 17) assessed using both youth self-report and parent report. Results showed that maternal adversity consistently predicted increased externalizing and internalizing symptoms across both informants' reports. Paternal adversity was associated with increased externalizing symptoms (youth- and parent-reported) and youth-reported internalizing symptoms, but showed weaker or non-significant effects compared to maternal adversity, particularly for parent-reported internalizing symptoms. Youth self-reported adversity predicted greater externalizing symptoms but was not associated with youth-reported internalizing symptoms and was negatively associated with parent-reported internalizing symptoms. These findings underline the prominent contributions of maternal adversity in adolescent mental health outcomes and highlight the value of multi-informant, multi-source designs to tease apart nuances of adversity pathways to psychopathology.

articles.title: Reactivity of Positive and Negative Affect to Momentary Stress and Discrimination
articles.authors: Jacqueline Rodriguez-Stanley; Katherine M. Knauft; Kristin Davis; Nataria Tennille Joseph; Samuele Zilioli; Lance M. Rappaport
articles.abstract: Affective reactivity to stress and the subsequent persistence of distress may explain how stressful experiences in daily life impact health and well-being. However, many existing studies either assess one type of stressor or assess stress exposure in an omnibus manner, both limit our understanding of how various types of stressors may differentially influence affect. Ecological momentary assessment (EMA) was used to examine affective reactivity to racial discrimination and general stress broadly construed in 493 African American adults (Mage = 65.8, SD = 5.5, range 55–75; 72% female) from The Heart of Detroit Study. Momentary negative (NA) and positive affect (PA), experiences of racial discrimination, and other stress experiences were reported up to four times daily during a seven-day EMA protocol and analyzed with residual dynamic structural equation models. Momentary racial discrimination concurrently predicted higher NA and lower PA. However, these associations did not survive adjustment for general stress, which was also independently associated with higher NA and lower PA. Findings suggest a novel, cyclical pathway by which, in addition to concurrent associations, elevated NA predicted higher intraindividual stress and elevated PA predicted lower intraindividual stress four hours later. General stress was not related to future NA or PA. Between-person elevation in depressive symptoms and neuroticism were associated with higher mean general stress, higher mean NA, lower mean PA, and greater intraindividual variability in PA. Study results highlight the importance of differentiating between categories of stressful experiences to identify the proximal correlates of affective variation.

articles.title: Intolerance of Uncertainty Predicts Physiological and Subjective Responses to Experiential Uncertainty
articles.authors: Tess Reid; Jolie B. Wormwood
articles.abstract: Individual differences in people’s tolerance for uncertainty are associated with mental health and well-being. However, much of the literature on responses to uncertainty has relied on retrospective self-report measures of how one believes they tend to act in uncertain contexts or has inferred how people feel about uncertainty based on their behavior in decision-making tasks. Here, we piloted a novel experiential uncertainty task in which participants were asked to insert their hands into an opaque box and feel an unknown object, and we assessed subjective affective reactions and physiological reactivity in anticipation of completing the task. Across both exploratory studies, we found participants higher in intolerance of uncertainty (IU) demonstrated less parasympathetic nervous system reactivity during uncertainty compared to those lower in IU. In Study 2, we found that this association was disrupted by presentation of biofeedback during the task, particularly false biofeedback indicating a rapid heart rate. Additionally, we found participants high in IU reported heightened experiences of several negative emotions (i.e., nervousness, worry, and stress) during experiential uncertainty (Study 2). Taken together, these findings suggest that individuals high in IU not only maintain unfavorable attitudes toward uncertainty in broad retrospective self-report measures, but they also experience instances of uncertainty differently than individuals lower in IU in the moment, both subjectively and physiologically.

articles.title: Household socioeconomic status mediates the association between dimensions of childhood adversity and reward processing
articles.authors: Tochukwu Nweze; Marin Davidson; Becca Clarke; Adeola Olowokudejo
articles.abstract: Past studies have implicated childhood adversity and poverty in impaired reward processing and risk-taking behavior. However, little is known about the mechanism underlying the interrelations among childhood adversity, poverty, and reward processing. In this study, we test the hypothesis that both chronic and the initial levels of poverty measured by household socioeconomic status (SES) will mediate the relationship between childhood adversity and decision-making or risk taking under uncertainty, measured by the Cambridge Gambling Task (CGT). Using latent change mediation analysis, we analyzed data from the Millennium Cohort Study obtained across 3 waves (age 3 for adversity, ages 11, and 14 for SES and CGT) in a sample of 9,816 participants (Males = 4,835; Females = 4,981; Mean age at 14 = 13.75; SD at age 14 = 0.45). The principal component analysis identified five dimensions of childhood adversity, of which the dimensions of parental alcohol abuse and global adversity had a contrasting relationship with household SES and CGT outcomes. Specifically, parental alcohol abuse predicted higher baseline SES and an increase in SES across time, which in turn predicted better quality of decision-making as well as decreased risk taking across time. In contrast, greater global adversity predicted lower baseline household SES and a decrease in SES over time, which predicted poorer quality of decision-making and increased risk-taking across ages 11 and 14. As household poverty is likely to co-occur with multiple adverse experiences in the dimension of global adversity, considerably more so than in parental alcohol abuse, these findings partly support the hypothesis that chronic poverty drives the association between childhood adversity and decision-making or risk-taking under uncertainty. These findings underscore the importance of differentiating patterns of childhood adversity and highlight the critical role of socioeconomic change as a potential intervention target.

articles.title: Guiding Memory Search: Speakers Calibrate Descriptions to Perceived Memorability
articles.authors: Ben Morris; Urvi Suwal; Qi Lin; Paula Rubio-Fernandez; Julian Jara-Ettinger
articles.abstract: When talking about the world in front of us, humans are remarkably efficient communicators. Our referential expressions help listeners find what we’re talking about by strategically adding adjectives as needed. But most conversations are about things that are not physically in front of us. Does this efficiency break down when referents are in the mind (rather than on the table), or are we also able to efficiently help a listener retrieve an item from memory? Across three experiments, we asked participants to describe images to help a listener recall each image. In Experiment 1 (total n = 600), participants showed efficiency, spontaneously incorporating expectations about memorability by providing relatively longer descriptions for images that people expect to be less memorable. Participants did not need access to or knowledge of their listener’s prior experience to adjust efficiently (Experiment 2, n = 300), but when shared experience directly modulated expected efficiency participants used that context accordingly (Experiment 3, n = 300). Interestingly, people’s descriptions were more aligned with subjective estimates of memorability, rather than objective, empirically-derived metrics. Together, this work provides new evidence that speakers spontaneously guide listeners’ mental processes to effectively facilitate recall.

articles.title: Disposition for Moral Indignation Scale (DMI-13):  Validation with a German sample
articles.authors: Rune Miram; Rahel Geppert; Marko Zetović; Jürgen Maes
articles.abstract: Indignation and outrage dominate much of public discourse. But what exactly constitutes the emotion of indignation, and do different people experience it in different amounts and intensities? The present work sought to examine the trait-like disposition for moral indignation and its differences to anger tendencies as well as its relationship with other personality characteristics concerning morality. In a multi-study approach with a total of 3,053 German respondents, we validated the Disposition for Moral Indignation (DMI-13) Scale. The samples for Studies 1–3 were approximated to German demographic norms for age, gender, and highest level of education. In Study 1 (n = 776), the factorial structure of the scale was explored and redundant items reduced. In Study 2 (n = 1,748), the one-factor structure could be confirmed. In Study 3 (n = 288), we found evidence for the convergent validity of the measure through its positive correlation with anger measures, concerns for justice, and motivations regarding interpersonal transgressions. Evidence for discriminant validity fell short of expectations, since anger measures showed just as strong if not stronger correlational pattern with morality than the disposition for moral indignation did. This result, however, is more indicative of the ambiguity of the anger scales used, with some of their items also expressing moral indignation. In Study 4 (n = 241), we found evidence for metric invariance across different instructions which opens up the possibility to adapt the instruction of the scale to the context the DMI-13 is used in. Limitations and implications for future research, like the need for the construction of further scales to distinguish indignation and anger, are discussed.

articles.title: Associations of harsh parenting with externalizing and internalizing symptoms vary across subpopulations
articles.authors: Tochukwu Nweze; Jamie Lars Hanson
articles.abstract:
Background: Past studies show harsh parenting increases externalizing and internalizing symptoms in children, yet not all children are adversely affected, and associations may differ for externalizing versus internalizing symptoms, particularly in low-income and ethnic minority families.
Methods: Using data from the Millennium Cohort Study (N = 12,573), we used finite mixture regression across ages 3, 5, and 7 to identify subpopulations in the association of harsh parenting with externalizing and internalizing symptoms.
Findings: The results showed four subpopulations that differed from one another in their relational effects. In the “All-Psychopathology” group, harsh parenting predicted increases in externalizing and internalizing symptoms. Harsh parenting predicted a decrease and increase in externalizing symptoms in the “Low-Risk” and “Externalizing” groups respectively. There was no significant association with internalizing symptoms in either subpopulation. In the “Mixed Psychopathology” group, harsh parenting predicted increased externalizing but decreased internalizing symptoms. Socioeconomic status and ethnicity were associated with membership in all but the “All-Psychopathology” group, with low-income and ethnic minority children significantly over-represented in the “Mixed Psychopathology” subgroup.
Conclusion: Taken together, these findings support evidence of heterogeneity in the association of harsh parenting with externalizing and internalizing symptoms, observed even among risk groups.

articles.title: From Friend to Threat: Stereotypes of Dogs are Ambivalent and Context Dependent
articles.authors: Lindsay Palmer; Remy Frost; Sophia Ruppel; Anamarie C Johnson; Andrew Johnson; Clive DL Wynne
articles.abstract: Research on animal stereotypes often lacks nuance, focusing on animals broadly rather than specific subgroups or contexts. In Study 1, participants (N=172) listed stereotypes about Dogs, Aggressive Dogs, and Friendly Dogs in open-ended responses. We identified 17 themes for Dogs (e.g., “good companions”), 15 for Aggressive Dogs (e.g., “dangerous to children”), and 20 for Friendly Dogs (e.g., “affiliative”). In Study 2, we examined stereotype endorsement, policy support, and the influence of related belief systems on their endorsement. Using items created from Study 1 responses, participants (N=305) rated their personal endorsement of stereotypes (Dogs, Friendly Dogs, or Aggressive Dogs). We found that greater belief in stereotypes about Dogs and Aggressive Dogs decreased support for policies to improve welfare, and only stereotypes about Aggressive Dogs predicted increased support for restrictive policies. We found that beliefs in essentialism and dominance theory consistently predicted greater stereotype endorsement.

articles.title: Algorithmic Exposure to Body Image Content on TikTok: A Mixed-Methods International Study with Implications for Mental Health and Platform Accountability
articles.authors: Dawn Branley-Bell; Claire Naomi Murphy-Morgan; Libby Moore
articles.abstract: Social media platforms, such as TikTok, represent a significant and rapidly evolving frontier for public health. With billions of users globally, TikTok’s recommendation algorithm curates personalised feeds that can expose individuals to a spectrum of body image content - from body acceptance material to content promoting extreme thinness, fitness ideals, and pro-eating disorder (pro-ED) messaging. This study investigates the extent and nature of algorithmic exposure to body image content on TikTok, its psychological impacts, and the coping strategies users employ, aiming to identify actionable implications for digital public health interventions, platform accountability, and policy.

articles.title: The Cyberbehavioral Reconstruction Model (CRM-CSAM): A Framework for the Behavioral Analysis of CSAM-Related Activity
articles.authors: Aleksandra Krasowska
articles.abstract: Forensic assessment of individuals involved in child sexual abuse material (CSAM) frequently relies on counting and classifying files, supplemented by largely unstructured clinical judgment. Such approaches provide limited guidance for case formulation, treatment planning, and management decisions, because they say little about how CSAM-related behavior is organized, regulated, and integrated into the person's broader sexual functioning over time. This paper presents the Cyberbehavioral Reconstruction Model for CSAM-Related Behavior (CRM-CSAM), a structured, behavior-based, dual-source framework designed to address this gap. CRM-CSAM comprises thirteen dimensions across four domains — mode of engagement, structuring, regulation and change, and psychosexual integration — each rated on an interpretive continuum within defined time windows and supported by source-tagged evidence records. Digital traces and clinical examination data are treated as two evidence streams informing the same dimensions; their convergence strengthens confidence, while their divergence is recorded and interpreted as clinically and forensically informative rather than treated as noise. The model is descriptive rather than predictive: it does not yield a risk score, but produces a configuration of dimensional codes that can support transparent case formulation, the differentiation of behavioral pathways, and the identification of treatment targets — for example, distinguishing persistent but weakly integrated use from patterns suggestive of progressive psychosexual integration, or separating genuine inhibitory effort from collection maintenance and concealment. A worked example illustrates the application of the model. CRM-CSAM was developed through iterative application to forensic clinical cases and is currently undergoing an inter-rater reliability study; the present paper provides the conceptual and procedural foundation for its clinical and forensic use.

articles.title: Assessing children and youth's socio-emotional behavior in rural Côte d’Ivoire: Construct validity and links to caregiver mental health and stress
articles.authors: Andrea De Bartolo; Julianna Mary Corbett; Amy Ogan; Sharon Wolf; Kaja Kinga Jasińska
articles.abstract: Côte d’Ivoire is a country with high poverty rates and poor mental health. We examined the validity of the Strengths and Difficulties Questionnaire (SDQ) in rural Côte d'Ivoire and how caregiver mental health and stress are associated with child socio-emotional problems across development. Findings indicate an adapted three-factor SDQ model (responsibility, internalizing, and externalizing) had best fit. Participants were primary caregivers (N=1879) with children between four and seventeen years old. Caregivers with poor mental health were associated with greater child externalizing problems, and parenting stress was associated with problems across all categories (responsibility, internalizing, and externalizing). These associations did not differ across child age. The adapted factor structure strengthens the validity of the SDQ and its use and interpretation in Côte d'Ivoire. Additionally, caregiver anxiety, depression, and stress were related to children’s socio-emotional outcomes in distinct ways, underscoring the need for targeted support and resources for families in Côte d’Ivoire.

articles.title: Heritability of children’s Secure Base Script Knowledge in middle childhood: A twin study with the Attachment Script Assessment
articles.authors: Jana Runze; Annemieke Witte; Marinus H. van IJzendoorn; Marian Bakermans-Kranenburg
articles.abstract:
Background
Are individual differences in attachment security inborn or shaped by the social environment? In infancy and early childhood the evidence points to a substantial role of the environment but a large twin study in early adolescence (Fearon et al., 2014) showed considerable heritability. Here we examined the twin heritability of attachment in middle childhood. We hypothesized that in middle childhood some heritability would emerge. Furthermore, we expected a role for cognitive and language abilities in explaining variance in attachment in middle childhood, partly related to the measurement of attachment, and we therefore examined associations with IQ.

Methods
This pre-registered study included 415 same-sex twin pairs (52% girls, 58% monozygotic) between 8 and 11 years old (M = 9.59, SD = 0.79). Participants were recruited from an experimental cohort-sequential study including two age-overlapping longitudinal cohorts. Secure base script knowledge was assessed with the Middle Childhood Attachment Script Assessment (T. E. A. Waters et al., 2015, 2019). Zygosity of the twins was determined using DNA samples. In the younger cohort, cognitive development was assessed with the Dutch version of the Wechsler Preschool and Primary Scale of Intelligence (Wechsler, 1967). In the older cohort, the Dutch version of the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children was used (Wechsler, 1991). 

Results
A significant common environment component (40%) was found in the absence of additive heritability. This result aligns with findings in infancy and early childhood and diverges from results in early adolescence.

Conclusions
The gene-environment correlation hypothesis suggesting that older children more actively shape their experiences in social contexts may offer a plausible explanation for heritability of attachment in adolescence, but in middle childhood this mechanism did not yet tip the balance toward a larger role for additive genetics. Larger longitudinal twin studies are needed to investigate the (lack of) heritability of attachment after preschool age.

articles.title: The Effect of Variability in Appearance and Context on Face Learning
articles.authors: Kévin Nguy; tom lapadula; Christel Devue
articles.abstract: Exposure to multiple unique images of the same face enhances recognition. Using ambient pictures and videos, face-learning studies have shown improved generalization, suggesting more refined memory representations for faces exhibiting greater variability. Although ambient images provide more ecologically valid learning conditions, the relative contribution of different sources of variability remain largely unexplored. In two pre-registered experiments, we investigated the effect of variability in appearance (stable, variable hairstyle) and context (unique, multiple background scenes) during face learning, as these factors may influence the coarse-to-fine encoding of diagnostic facial features. In Experiment 1, eight identities were learned in a blocked manner, whereas they were randomised in Experiment 2. Recognition was assessed using images displaying only inner facial features. We hypothesized that conditions promoting finer encoding of inner facial features (variable appearance and multiple contexts in Experiment 1) or providing stable learning anchors (stable appearance and unique context in Experiment 2) would improve recognition. In Experiment 1, variable appearances enhanced recognition performance relative to stable appearances, despite diverting gaze from inner features during learning. However, Experiment 2, along with supplementary cross-experiment analyses, revealed that randomizing identities eliminated the recognition disadvantage observed for stable faces, possibly by encouraging participants to re-engage attention with each learning image. Contextual variability did not affect recognition performance in either experiment, suggesting that appearance variability and learning procedure are stronger determinants of efficient learning. These findings highlight the critical role of appearance variability and of learning procedure in shaping recognition outcomes.

articles.title: Emotion Socialization in the Spotlight: Early Parent-Infant Synchrony, Mind-Mindedness, and Children’s Later Expressions of Shyness
articles.authors: Christopher Riddell; Milica Nikolic; Agneta Fischer; Itir Onal Ertugrul; Moniek Zeegers; Susan Bögels; Cristina Colonnesi
articles.abstract: Owing to their burgeoning understanding of the social world, the early childhood years represent a critical developmental period in which children's first expressions of shyness take shape. Nonverbal expressions of shyness have been argued to take two forms: positive shyness, characterized by smiling with a gaze and/or head aversion, and negative shyness, characterized by negative affect with a gaze and/or head aversion. Although early parental socialization practices are known to shape children’s emotions, few studies have examined how these influence shyness expressions beyond infancy. In a sample of 120 Dutch children and their parents, we examined two potential socialization mechanisms of preschool children’s expressions of shyness: positive affective synchrony (measured at 4-months) and mind-mindedness (measured at 12- and 30-months). We assessed these facets separately with both mothers and fathers during free-play tasks, and children’s expressions shyness during a public performance. Mothers’, but not fathers’, positive affective synchrony was associated with lower levels of negative shyness in childhood, consistent with a potentially protective role of early synchrony. Fathers' appropriate mind-mindedness, however, was unexpectedly associated with less positive and more negative shyness, while mothers' non-attuned mind-mindedness was associated with more positive shyness. Together, our results highlight the importance of early parent-child socialization practices in infancy for later expressions of shyness in childhood but suggest that these operate in context dependent ways.

articles.title: Validation of the English Social Attunement Questionnaire (SAQ): associations with substance use, impulsivity and sensation seeking across the lifespan
articles.authors: Emese Kroon; Karis Colyer-Patel; Christophe Romein; Janna Cousijn
articles.abstract: Our social environment plays a crucial role in our behavior, including substance use and other impulsive and sensation seeking behaviors. While most research in these fields focus on the role of group pressure and conformity, the importance of social attunement - the tendency to harmonize with and adapt to the social environment to find social reward in the absence of explicit group pressure – has been overlooked. In this study, we assessed the psychometric properties of the English translation of the social attunement questionnaire (SAQ) and assessed how social attunement is associated with age, alcohol use, cannabis use, impulsivity, and sensation seeking (N = 447, 54.5% female). Confirmatory factor analysis showed an acceptable to good fit to the original two-factor structure (RMSEA = .068; CFI = .959; TLI = .947). Internal consistency of the full scale ( = .82), cognition subscale ( = .75) and behavior subscale ( = .78) were sufficient to good, and the SAQ displayed good measurement invariance to sex. Younger individuals displayed higher social attunement, and SAQ scores – guided by the behavior subscale – were positively associated with alcohol and cannabis use, as well as impulsivity. Furthermore, age moderated the association between SAQ scores and alcohol consumption as well as the association between SAQ scores and sensation seeking; while the associations were positive in younger individuals, the associations became more negative with increasing age. These results show that high social attunement might be both a risk and protective factor for the engagement in risk-taking behaviors depending on age.

articles.title: Can the neurodiversity approach apply to ADHD?  Exploring the lived experience of ADHD as neurodivergence
articles.authors: Saskia van der Oord; Gert-Jan Vanaken; Blandine French
articles.abstract:
Introduction: Contrary to medical and social constructionist approaches, in the neurodiversity approach, ADHD is considered a value-neutral neurodivergence, rather than a deficit to-be-cured or a societal problem affecting identity, fostering stigma and overmedicalization. This paper explores, guided by lived-experiences, the applicability of the neurodiversity approach to ADHD in comparison to the other approaches.
Methods: We examine key claims of the medical and social constructionist approaches to ADHD, using an “inside-out” perspective, based on studies documenting first-person perspectives of ADHD adolescents and adults with experiencing ADHD, disability, getting or having a diagnosis and with medication. 
Results: In these lived experiences (1) ADHD characteristics are generally not experienced as universal deficits (2) disability primarily stems from a highly invalidating mismatch between the ADHD person and their neuronormative environment (3) the ADHD diagnostic label brings both beneficial and disadvantageous consequences (4) medication is often a balancing act between costs on people’s bodies and identity and benefits as medication is necessary to meet important life-goals and for navigating unaccommodating environments.
Discussion: Considering ADHD a difference with disability mainly occurring in mismatch with environment and not a unified deficit, nor a purely social problem, reflects people's lived experiences, makes an ADHD diagnosis easier to reconcile with oneself and can make the balancing act of medication easier. The neurodiversity approach may be a promising and less stigmatizing alternative to current approaches. It empowers people by valuing lived experience as expert evidence and provides a clinical, societal and research framework of ADHD that integrates both individual and environmental aspects of disability.

articles.title: Does reading for pleasure support vocabulary learning? A naturalistic study to test the link between print exposure and vocabulary gain
articles.authors: Sanne W van der Kleij; Alice Grønhøj; Janet Vousden; Adrian Burgess; Laura Shapiro; Jessie Ricketts
articles.abstract: Purpose: This study adapted a well-established behavior-change intervention to investigate reading and vocabulary learning in a naturalistic context, investigating: (i) if this approach provides an effective way to encourage 11-14-year-old adolescents to read more; and (ii) wether the frequency of exposures to target words predicts vocabulary learning from commercially available fiction. Method: The intervention involved completing a reading diary, including goal-setting, feedback on this goal via text-messages, and rewards. Participants (n = 254, 50% girls) from the South of England were provided with commercially available fiction books to read in their own time and we tracked the number of exposures to particular words found in those books. Results: Our approach did not increase reading amount: the intervention group did not read more than the control group. However, the number of exposures to the tracked words was associated with vocabulary knowledge for those words. This is in line with theories supporting a crucial role of leisure reading for vocabulary learning. Conclusion: Future research should work more collaboratively with teachers and young people to design interventions that are effective, engaging and acceptable for adolescents.

articles.title: Students are willing to invest personal resources in a writing assignment to avoid environmental costs that are associated with using generative AI
articles.authors: Sarah Kusch; Ruth Krebs
articles.abstract: Every day, students have to decide whether to use generative artificial intelligence (genAI) for their coursework, requiring them to tradeoff the costs and benefits of using that technology. Our preregistered study (n = 164) tested the specific tradeoff between personal (i.e., cognitive effort and time) and environmental consequences in a Belgian student sample. Participants made nine choices regarding whether to use genAI to write an essay based on varying essay lengths and environmental impact of the genAI models. Depending on their choices, participants then wrote an essay with or without genAI. We found that participants were less likely to choose genAI with decreasing essay lengths and higher environmental impact of the genAI models. Thus, participants considered not just personal but also environmental costs in their decisions to use genAI. This supports the call for more transparency regarding the environmental impact of genAI companies to enable users to make informed decisions.

articles.title: Towards a community driven framework for standardized open data in psychoneuroendocrinology: A Delphi consensus study
articles.authors: Maria Meier; Milou S. C. Sep; Thomaz Bastiaanssen; Emma Clephas; Conny W.E.M. Quaedflieg; Jakub Rajcani; Robert Richer; Bence Szaszkó; Susanne Vogel; Stella Voulgaropoulou
articles.abstract: In psychoneuroendocrinology (PNE), data is valuable due to its high clinical and health-related relevance and resource-intensive data collection yet sharing it openly alongside publications remains uncommon. If shared, data remain highly fragmented and non-standardized, which hinders data reuse and integration across studies. To address this, we formed a consortium and, as a first step, conducted this study using a modified Delphi approach across two survey rounds to inform about important considerations regarding data structure, metadata, subject-level variables, and implementation requirements before a data standard for PNE may be developed. The panel in the final round consisted of 50 responders from the field of PNE. In two online survey rounds, respondents rated their agreement with 84 statements anonymously on a 5-point Likert scale. Consensus (> 70% [dis]agreement) was achieved for 80 items. The results highlight a broad agreement within the PNE community on the need for, and key components of, a standardized data structure, including a balance between flexibility and standardization to allow for adaptability across many use cases as well as comparability. Developing and establishing a standard for PNE data will be a central next step to foster transparency, sustainable use of resources, and the translational potential of our work.

articles.title: The genetic structure of creativity: A comprehensive investigation into the shared nature of different proxies for creativity
articles.authors: Penghao Xia; Laura Wesseldijk; Lu Yi; Miriam A. Mosing; Fredrik Ullén
articles.abstract: In recent years, there has been a surge of interest in creativity and its underlying biology and mechanisms. However, an unsolved fundamental question concerns the degree to which various instruments really measure the same construct. To investigate the structure of creativity, we analyzed seven creativity measures collected in around 9,000 twin individuals, from both a phenotypic and genetic perspective. Phenotypically, overall low to modest correlations suggested considerable heterogeneity between the individual measures. An exploratory graph analysis classified creativity measures as artistic or scientific. Genetically, multivariate twin modeling revealed one set of additive genetic influences common to all creativity measures, reflecting general creativity, and an additional set shared only between artistic creativity measures. While different creativity measures capture partly distinct constructs, they also reveal a shared underlying structure across all measures, and particularly strong commonality among measures of artistic creativity. Findings encourage future gene-discovery studies to incorporate multiple creativity measures.

articles.title: Surprise-Minimizing Contextualizing Control: A Theory of Adaptive Cognitive Control Without a Homunculus
articles.authors: Maximilian Mittenbühler; Sarah Schwöbel; David Dignath; Stefan J. Kiebel; Martin V. Butz
articles.abstract: Theories of cognitive control typically presuppose dedicated control structures that monitor and regulate information processing in predefined ways. Although such accounts explain many empirical findings, they risk reintroducing a homunculus: an internal controller whose own structure, inputs, and regulatory targets remain unexplained. We propose the Surprise-minimizing Contextualizing Control Framework (SCCF) as a normative account of how control structures can emerge through interaction with an environment. Rather than treating cognitive control as a separate process, SCCF conceptualizes control as emerging from how an agent organizes its internal model of the environment. It combines decision making and latent structure inference under a joint objective: minimizing anticipated surprise. Formally, we show that minimizing anticipated surprise yields an optimal trade-off between reducing expected error and limiting deviations from prior beliefs. Simulations demonstrate that statistical regularities in the environment give rise to latent contextual structures that determine what is monitored and what is regulated. We further show how classic behavioral phenomena, including proportion-congruency and task-switch effects, arise in SCCF as a consequence of rational adaptation to environmental statistics. SCCF thereby links structure learning, predictive coding, and resource-rational theories of control. Together, these results suggest that the apparent machinery of cognitive control—monitoring, regulation, control allocation, and control costs—can emerge from the same inferential principles that govern prediction, learning, and action selection, without the need for a dedicated controller.

articles.title: Structure and diversity in children's line drawings of object categories across four international contexts
articles.authors: Haoyu Du; Tarun Sepuri; Urvi Maheshwari; Rebecca Zhu; Joab Ochieng Arieda; Bria Long
articles.abstract: Line drawings are one of our oldest cultural technologies for depicting what we know about the visual world and, in many contexts, are produced prolifically by young children throughout early and middle childhood. We examine how children's line drawings of common object categories differ across development in four international contexts (San Jose, USA; Beijing, China; Kisumu, Kenya; Delhi, India). We embedded 4,387 drawings of 12 categories produced by children ages 4--9 into the feature space of a large multimodal model and used representational similarity analysis to compare category-level structure across contexts. All four contexts shared similar category geometry, yet context permutation tests revealed reliable context-specific differences in five of six site pairs that are mostly driven by differences in drawing recognizability. Overall, cross-site similarity generally increased from younger to older children, particularly for pairs involving contexts with lower baseline recognizability. These findings suggest that children's visual conventions are broadly shared across diverse contexts, with cross-site variability largely attributable to differences in recognizability rather than site-specific representational structure.

articles.title: The value of generative AI in creative teams depends on when it enters collective search
articles.authors: Difang Huang; Shumin Yu; Fadong Chen; Weihua Zhou
articles.abstract: Generative AI is fast becoming a fixture in creative teams, yet its value may hinge less on access than on when it enters the collective search process. We address this through a randomized experiment with 444 participants across 148 three-person teams completing a 40-minute product-design task. Teams were assigned to different AI-access regimes, receiving the assistant during divergent search only, convergent selection only, the full process, or not at all. AI during divergent search raised human-rated creativity, as did full-process access, whereas access confined to convergent selection did not; timing models attribute this gain to early availability rather than continuous use. Process logs, proposal texts, human ratings and AI-based diagnostics expose the underlying mechanism: early AI broadened semantic exploration and enriched substantive discussion, whereas convergent-stage AI only inflated proposal volume, a pathway associated with diminished creativity. Among AI-using teams, semantic proximity between final proposals and AI outputs predicted higher creativity, yet generic warnings eroded that proximity. AI raters, though internally reliable, rewarded quantity over quality. Together, these findings reveal that AI’s creative payoff hinges not on access alone but on its placement within collective search, and that both deployment and evaluation choices can quietly distort that value.

articles.title: Distinct yet coupled hemispheric codes for foveal visual processing
articles.authors: Amanda K Robinson; Tijl Grootswagers; Sophia M. Shatek; Marlene Behrmann; Thomas A. Carlson
articles.abstract: Seeing the world feels effortless, yet it is accomplished by two hemispheres that do not process visual information in the same way. Whether this involves distinct processing cascades, tight hemispheric coupling, or something in between has fundamental implications for understanding the emergence of coherent perception from divided processes. Images presented to the fovea project to both hemispheres, offering an opportunity to isolate intrinsic hemispheric differences in visual coding. Here, we used electroencephalography and neural decoding methods to investigate the dynamics of foveal visual processing in the left and right hemispheres. Human participants (N = 20; 15 females, 5 males) viewed images of objects, faces and words in rapid sequences while performing an orthogonal task. We found different trajectories of visual coding within the left and right hemispheres, and these differences were characterised by distinct featural biases in each hemisphere, with a particular left bias for rectilinearity and right bias for colour. Yet, despite encoding visual information differently, the left and right hemispheres appear to converge on a shared representation. The results provide new insights into hemispheric dynamics underlying visual perception and the complementary roles of the left and right hemispheres in processing visual information.

articles.title: Testing Linear and Non-Linear Associations Between Climate Anxiety and Pro-Environmental Behaviour: An Individual Participant Data Meta-Analysis
articles.authors: Tomás Gago; Helen Fischer; Taciano L Milfont
articles.abstract:
Climate change can impact people in a variety of ways. One such way is via climate change anxiety, the anticipatory anxious emotions and potential impairment in day-to-day functioning associated with people’s perceptions of climate change as a threat. Climate anxiety has been discussed as a practical anxiety that can motivate pro-environmental actions (suggesting a positive linear association), but at very high levels it may become overwhelming and lead to paralysis (implying a non-linear association). We conducted a pre-registered individual participant data meta-analysis across 12 independent samples comprising more than 6,000 participants to examine the association pattern between climate anxiety impairment (as indexed by the Climate Change Anxiety Scale) and pro-environmental behaviour (PEB). Across distinct analytical strategies, results confirmed a robust positive association between climate anxiety impairment and PEB.
Although quadratic models indicated a statistically significant negative curvature, more conservative two-lines analyses revealed that this pattern reflected a strong positive association at lower levels of climate anxiety that attenuated at higher levels rather than reversing direction. Contrary to eco-paralysis accounts and our predicted inverted U-shaped pattern, higher climate anxiety impairment was not associated with lower PEB; instead, there is a saturation point at which higher levels of climate anxiety impairment only led to marginal gains in PEB. We discuss the implications for conceptualizing climate anxiety as a practical anxiety and an initial catalyst for individual and collective pro-environmental action, as well as potential mechanisms that sustain PEB even at higher levels of climate anxiety impairment.

articles.title: Framed Listening: Can accompanying contextual cues shape Music-Influenced Mental Content?
articles.authors: Hazel Aileen van der Walle; Elizabeth Hellmuth Margulis; Kelly Jakubowski
articles.abstract: When we listen to music, what we think about is rarely shaped by sound alone. This study tested whether contextual framing cues—phrases locating music within a familiar social or cultural situation—systematically align listeners’ music-influenced mental content (MIMC). Using a between-participants design, 160 participants heard 16 unfamiliar instrumental clips (Electronic, Jazz, Metal, and Eighties-pop) each accompanied by one of four context cues (bar, concert, movie, video game). Semantic and lexical similarity of participants’ MIMCs described after each clip were quantified using complementary natural language processing pipelines:  sentence-embedding model, MiniLM (semantic; 1,919,820 individual-level pairwise comparisons), and TF-IDF (lexical; 2,016 aggregated document-level comparisons). Listeners who received the same context cue produced more semantically similar MIMC than those given both a different clip and context cue—a finding replicated at the lexical level and confirmed in linear mixed models controlling for genre, clip, and individual response tendencies. Music remained the stronger driver of convergence: MIMC were significantly more similar with same-clip pairs than same-context pairs. Both factors contributed additively, with combined clip-and-context alignment producing the greatest convergence overall. Genre moderated this balance: Metal consistently showed the greatest context framing susceptibility across both pipelines and was the only genre where contextual framing exceeded music as a driver of within-genre similarity; Jazz showed the smallest framing susceptibility. Believability of the clip–context pairing significantly predicted lexical (TF-IDF) but not semantic (MiniLM) similarity, suggesting framing operated through associative priming rather than conscious plausibility judgement. These findings provide an empirical test of source sensitivity in MIMC, demonstrating that even newly introduced contextual frames are sufficient to meaningfully constrain the distributed subjectivity of MIMC in music listening experiences.

articles.title: Individual Differences in Stereomotion Yield Functional Differences in Reaching Dynamics
articles.authors: Pin Yang; Geoffrey P Bingham
articles.abstract: Studies have shown that human motion-in-depth (MID) perception is supported by two stereomotion mechanisms, change in disparity over time (CDOT) and interocular velocity differences (IOVD), yet their roles in visually guided action remain unclear especially given that they also exhibit individual differences.  We tested whether reliance on these channels predicts visuomotor control during reaching. 244 participants completed relative-distance discrimination tasks and were classified as CDOT-dominant, IOVD-dominant, or Combined observers. A subset then performed dynamic reaching tasks in which MID signal reliability was manipulated via dot-lifetime. We found that visuomotor behavior was systematically different as a function of the stereomotion channel reliance. CDOT-dominant observers exhibited slower velocities and longer movement times, consistent with a temporally sluggish but spatially precise control strategy. IOVD-dominant observers moved faster but with reduced spatial accuracy, reflecting a velocity-based strategy. Combined observers showed a hybrid profile, matching the IOVD group in speed and the CDOT group in accuracy. Dot-lifetime manipulations further supported this dissociation: accuracy in the CDOT group was unaffected by dot-lifetime, whereas the IOVD group showed a sharp improvement from 1- to 2-frame dot-lifetime, with no further gains. The Combined group exhibited a more complex pattern, reflecting the integration of both disparity- and velocity-based information. These findings provide converging evidence that specialization of stereomotion channels systematically influences both MID perception and online visuomotor control. The results underscore a tight coupling between perceptual capabilities and action dynamics, and highlight the critical role of continuous online guidance and individual differences in contemporary models of visually guided action.

articles.title: Screen Time and Developmental Outcomes in the First Digitally Native Generation: Co-viewing Does Not Buffer Harm in a Mixed-Methods Study From Urban Iran
articles.authors: Toktam Farhang; Seyed Saeid Sajjadi Anari; Saeid Hassanzadeh
articles.abstract:
Background. Parental co-viewing is widely recommended to mitigate the negative effects of early childhood screen time. Evidence for its protective role remains limited in non-Western populations and rarely distinguishes between passive co-presence and active verbal mediation.
Methods. A two-phase mixed-methods cross-sectional study was conducted among urban Iranian families. Phase 1 used an online questionnaire (n = 250 mother-child pairs) to assess child screen time, content type, device use, technoference, and co-viewing. Phase 2 (n = 50) included in-person developmental assessment with the Ages and Stages Questionnaire, Third Edition (ASQ-3), a structured questionnaire, and a semi-structured qualitative interview. Spearman correlations, multiple regression, moderation analysis, and thematic analysis examined associations between screen exposure variables and developmental outcomes.
Results. Child screen time was negatively associated with all five ASQ-3 domains (Spearman r = -0.542 to -0.704, all p < 0.01) and total developmental score (r = -0.891, p < 0.001). In regression analysis controlling for child age and maternal education, child screen time explained 75.6% of variance in total developmental score (β = -0.869, p < 0.001). Maternal screen time was selectively associated with fine motor (r = -0.323, p = 0.022), personal-social skills (r = -0.304, p = 0.032), and total score (r = -0.411, p = 0.003). Co-viewing did not moderate the screen time-development relationship (interaction β = 0.047, p = 0.520). Three qualitative themes emerged: pseudo-cognitive development paradox, behavioral and physiological strain, and passive co-viewing as a parental coping mechanism.
Conclusions. Screen time exposure was strongly and negatively associated with developmental outcomes across all measured domains. Parental co-viewing did not buffer these effects because, in this cultural context, it functioned as physical co-presence rather than active mediation. These findings support screen time reduction as the primary target of early childhood media interventions, with active mediation as a complement rather than a substitute.
Keywords: screen time; child development; co-viewing; technoference; Ages and Stages Questionnaire; early childhood; mixed methods; Iran.

articles.title: Prevalence of epilepsy and young-onset dementia in adults with intellectual disability:  Contrasting Down syndrome and autism diagnoses
articles.authors: Nancy Raitano Lee; Sha Tao; Samuelle Voltaire; Lauren Bishop; Anna Esbensen; Brian Lee; Lindsay Shea; Gregory Wallace
articles.abstract:
Objective: Epilepsy and young-onset Alzheimer’s disease and related dementias [ADRD] greatly impact health and service needs. Despite their elevated prevalence in intellectual disability (ID), large scale studies of both conditions, which are known to co-occur, among subgroups of ID (i.e., Down syndrome [DS] and/or autism spectrum disorder [ASD]) are lacking. The objective of this research was to characterize the prevalence of epilepsy, young-onset ADRD, and their comorbidity in ID relative to the general population and to examine how co-occurring DS and/or ASD impacts prevalence.
Methods: In this cross-sectional study of data from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS; 2008-2019) from all 50 US States/District of Columbia, prevalence and odds ratios of epilepsy and young-onset ADRD were examined for all enrollees with ID between the ages of 30 and 64 years (n=847,430) and a random sample of age-matched adults from the general population without diagnoses of ID, ASD, or DS (n=1,065,530) representing both sexes and all racial/ethnic groups. Among those with ID, prevalence of these conditions was contrasted when individuals held diagnoses of DS and/or ASD.
Results: Higher prevalence was observed in the ID group relative to the general population for epilepsy, young-onset ADRD, and their comorbidity. Among participants with ID, ASD was associated with the greatest epilepsy risk whereas DS was associated with the greatest risk for ADRD and epilepsy+ADRD. When DS and ASD diagnoses were considered together, interactions were observed, indicating subtle differences in the risk for these conditions among participants with both DS and ASD.
Significance: These results support the importance of screening adults with ID for epilepsy and young-onset dementia. They also draw attention to subgroups of people with ID who may be at greater risk for these conditions, and the importance of evaluating the co-occurrence of these conditions among adults with ID.
