articles.title: Moral Virtues Are Often Relevant in Daily Life, but “Morality” Is Not
articles.authors: Benjamin Hardin; Qilin Zhang; Jessie Sun
articles.abstract:
Recent work suggests that morality may be surprisingly absent from everyday life (Atari
et al., 2023; Hofmann et al., 2014). Across four daily life studies (Total N = 1,663 people from
North America, Germany, and China; 90,002 observations), we revisit the everyday relevance of
morality by examining how often people perceive opportunities to express 12 moral virtues (e.g.,
honesty, kindness), 12 corresponding vices (e.g., dishonesty, unkindness), and (im)morality.
Participants perceived opportunities to express at least one of the 12 virtues > 49% of the time.
However, they rarely perceived these opportunities to express virtues as opportunities to be
“(im)moral” (< 8% in North America and Germany; 26% in China). Patience, responsibility, and
respectfulness were most often relevant, whereas forgiveness, courage, and loyalty were least
often relevant. We conclude that conceptualizing morality in more concrete terms reveals a
plethora of morally relevant situations that have gone unnoticed in previous studies.

articles.title: Visualização de dados com R
articles.authors: Felipe Flores Kupske
articles.abstract:
Este tutorial é um guia introdutório sobre visualização de dados no R, desenvolvido como material de apoio para estudantes das áreas de linguística, psicologia e educação. O foco é prático e voltado a pesquisadores que estão dando os primeiros passos
na criação de gráficos com o pacote ggplot2. O tutorial cobre: (1) carregamento de pacotes e dados, com os datasets do pacote languageR; (2) gráficos de barras para variáveis categóricas, incluindo barras agrupadas, empilhadas e personalização com RColorBrewer; (3) histogramas para variáveis quantitativas, com ajuste de bins, marcação de média e curva de densidade; (4) gráficos de caixas (boxplot), com marcação de média, notches, jitter e facetas; (5) gráficos de violino combinados com boxplot para maior detalhe da distribuição; (6) gráficos de dispersão (scatterplot) com linhas de tendência, rótulos e facetas por grupo; e (7) visualização de dados ordinais com o pacote likert, incluindo integração com ggplot2 para personalização. Ao longo do tutorial, cada tipo de gráfico é construído progressivamente, linha por linha, com foco na compreensão de cada argumento. Não é exigido conhecimento avançado de R, mas familiaridade básica com o RStudio — como abrir arquivos, executar código e instalar pacotes — é necessária.

articles.title: Efficacy of Arts-based Social Prescribing for Mental Health in Adults with Psychiatric Diagnoses: two subsequent Randomized Controlled Trials
articles.authors: Nikos Stefanis; Antonis Tsionis; Theodoros Sergentanis; Andreas Katsimpris; Eleni Giannouli; Lambros Yotis; Sophia Martinaki; Katerina Mavromichali; Marlen Mouliou; Aphrodite Pantagoutsou
articles.abstract:
Objective: Arts engagement has been proposed as a scalable adjunct to psychiatric care, but high-quality randomized evidence amongst psychiatric populations remains limited. This study evaluated the efficacy of arts on prescription (AoP) added to care as usual (CAU) on mental health outcomes amongst adults receiving community psychiatric care.
Methods: We conducted two sequential 12-week parallel-group randomized controlled trials in Greece: a pilot (N=380) and a confirmatory trial (N=541). Adults receiving psychiatric care were referred by mental health professionals and randomized to AoP plus CAU or CAU alone. The intervention involved participation in structured arts activities (music, dance, cinema, storytelling, theatre, environmental and visual arts). Depression symptom score (Patient Health Questionnaire-9 [PHQ-9]) at 12 weeks was a primary outcome across both trials. Secondary outcomes included anxiety (GAD-7), wellbeing (WEMWBS), and loneliness. Analyses used intention-to-treat linear mixed-effects models.
Results: Across both trials (N=921), AoP was associated with greater reductions in depression symptoms compared with CAU. In the pilot and the main trial, the between-group difference at 12 weeks were -1.91 PHQ-9 points (95% CI=-3.09 to -0.72; p= 0.001) and -0.97 (95% CI= -1.89 to -0.06; p= 0.001), respectively. Significant improvements were also observed for anxiety and wellbeing, but not loneliness. Attrition was modest (15.7% - 18.7%), and no serious adverse events attributable to the intervention were reported. Conclusions: AoP added to CAU produced consistent improvements in depression, anxiety, and wellbeing across two randomized trials. These findings support AoP as a scalable, person-centered adjunct within community mental health services.

articles.title: Clarifying the concept of psychological richness: Is it a dimension of subjective well-being?
articles.authors: Mark Fabian; Vithor Franco
articles.abstract: This paper queries the claim of a series of recent publications that “psychological richness” (PR) is a coherent concept and a dimension of subjective well-being. Our argument is that psychological richness has merit, but a great deal more theoretical exposition is required to effectively conceptualise, measure, and study it. In particular, the value-laden relationship between psychological richness and wellbeing needs to be thoroughly theorised. Cognitive interviewing or similar qualitative methods are also required to understand what respondents understand by PR when responding to psychometric surveys designed to measure it. Until this is done, data from the psychologically rich life questionnaire is hard to interpret and thus has little evidentiary merit. We critique the designs of existing studies that are supposed to validate psychological richness as a dimension of a good life. We also provide a statistical reanalysis of existing data from the psychological rich life questionnaire. Our results suggest that psychological richness is neither a unified construct nor a dimension of subjective well-being. We conclude that we do not presently understand psychological richness and should be cautious about popularising the concept.

articles.title: Have you really seen this illusory face before? Illusory faces bias recognition memory reports
articles.authors: Olga Kreichman; Limor Brook; Susan G Wardle; Sharon Gilaie-Dotan
articles.abstract: Visual memory is known to be influenced by objective image properties, including image size and contrast as well as visual category. However, the influence of illusory perceptual experience on memory remains less understood. Here we investigated whether face pareidolia, a robust illusory phenomenon where certain stimuli elicit a face-like percept despite not being real faces, influences memory. While we assumed illusory faces would be remembered better than matched control images since they are surprising, interesting, draw more attention, and may be associated with longer observation times, oldnew recognition memory sensitivity was not consistently influenced by the face illusion. Surprisingly, decision criterion was more liberal for illusory faces than for other categories, and this effect scaled with illusory strength such that participants were more likely to report having seen illusory faces for stronger illusory face percepts. In a 2AFC recognition memory paradigm that minimizes criterion influence we found that the tendency to report having seen illusory faces is present even for unseen images. Further analyses involving confidence ratings indicated our findings likely reflect heightened sense of familiarity for illusory faces and not genuine false memory. Our results suggest that during retrieval illusory faces influence decisions independently of the actual memory trace.

articles.title: How (not) to Correct a “Mistake”: A True Tale from the Journal of Applied Psychology
articles.authors: Joachim Hüffmeier; Guido Hertel
articles.abstract: With this commentary, we pursue the following four goals: (1) Publicly acknowledging that we—of course—care about the correctness and integrity of the scientific record and therefore also support the correction of clear mistakes in our own manuscripts. We further also acknowledge the important function that anonymous readers play in this context who identify possible mistakes in the scientific record. (2) Providing transparency concerning the correction of our article (Breuer et al., 2016) that was issued by the Journal of Applied Psychology (JAP) on the 6th of April 2026. To do so, we provide a timeline with all details that are necessary to comprehend the whole process preceding the issuing of the correction, starting from an anonymous reader’s message to the then Editor-in-Chief of JAP in late 2024 until the publication of the correction in April 2026. (3) Pointing out the problems, which occurred during the exchange with the then Editor-in-Chief of JAP and which still remain. (4) Raising questions and providing suggestions on (i) how the process of correcting mistakes should unfold, (ii) how corrections should be formulated, and (iii) where possible limits of corrections of published scientific papers are. We thereby want to contribute to the needed transparency in the case of the correction of our article, but also to the question of how the process and results of corrections should—and perhaps also should not—be designed.

articles.title: Navigating Safety in Reproductive Health Self-Care Interventions in Ghana: A Qualitative Study
articles.authors: Kwasi Adu Manu; Sarah E. Stutterheim; Charles Adjei; Francine Schneider
articles.abstract:
Introduction
Reproductive health self-care interventions are growing rapidly in Ghana, but there is limited evidence on the quality-of-care provisions that ensure safety for end-users and health professionals. This study explored how quality of care and safety are experienced and maintained within reproductive health self-care interventions in Ghana, using a self-care-specific conceptual framework.

Materials and Methods
Using an exploratory qualitative approach, underpinned by the Quality-of-Care Framework for Self-Care, we interviewed 29 end-users and 19 professionals, comprising health providers, program managers, digital referral agents, and community facilitators across Accra, Tamale, Cape Coast, and Sunyani. The study spanned six sexual and reproductive health and rights (SRHR) domains: family planning, abortion, HIV/STI, maternal and child health, general SRHR, and cervical cancer. Data was analysed using thematic analysis techniques in Dedoose, grounded in both deductive and inductive principles.

Results
Key outcomes include the following: Technical Competency – Despite barriers, end-users felt sufficiently empowered to access self-care services. although professionals required continuous capacity strengthening to keep pace with the evolving ecosystem; Client Safety – End-users preferred access to medical eligibility information, but professionals were while professionals expressed concerns about potential misuse; Information Exchange – Information on family planning self-care services was more openly available than across other SRHR domains; Interpersonal Connection and Choice – End-users reported more positive experiences with remote services than facility-based care, although professionals faced operational constraints that affected consistent delivery of quality care; Continuity of Care – Follow-up care was better integrated across health facilities than alternate service delivery points, with periodic stockouts disrupting continuity efforts.

Conclusion
In exploring how quality of care and safety are experienced and maintained within reproductive health self-care interventions in Ghana, this study found that end-users and professionals have adopted interim measures to maintain some level of safety amid policy and institutional inadequacies. However, the sustainability of these measures is uncertain, posing a serious risk to public health if not prioritised in the short term.

articles.title: On the Necessity of Conscious Perception to Attention: Revisiting Jiang et al. (2006) with Gaze-Contingent Crowding
articles.authors: Yung-Jung Chen; Ryan Bradley Scott; Zoltan Dienes
articles.abstract: This was a conceptual replication of Jiang et al. (2006) and Chen et al. (2026), who found nude images suppressed by continuous flash suppression (CFS) drew heterosexual viewers' spatial attention without awareness. The present study exploited gaze-contingent crowding, in which nude images and neutral blobs were displayed bilaterally in peripheral vision, crowded by neutral flankers, and replaced when gaze departed central fixation. Sixty-six participants completed a modified dot-probe task with a 1°-tilted Gabor probe; perceptual awareness was indexed by a perceptual awareness scale. No attentional priming was found in trials without reported visual experience, contrary to previous results obtained under CFS. Heterosexual females showed moderate evidence of gaze shifts toward male images in trials without reported visual experience, suggesting early oculomotor capture before crowding consolidated. A follow-up manipulation check confirmed that peripheral visual suppression was an artefact of visual crowding rather than eccentricity. The findings indicate that unconscious attentional priming is sensitive to the mechanism by which subliminality is induced, informing the broader debate on the dissociation between consciousness and attention.

articles.title: When does curiosity lead to proactive and attentive information seeking? The moderating roles of auxiliary skills in classroom learning settings in Nepal
articles.authors: Kenji Kitamura; Dana McCoy; Elizabeth Bonawitz
articles.abstract: Curiosity is widely regarded as a fundamental driver of learning, with information-seeking behavior serving as one key mechanism linking the two. To date, research has largely focused on overt, proactive information seeking, although in many non-Western classroom contexts, curiosity may instead be expressed through socially permissible attentive behaviors (e.g., focused listening). Moreover, little is known about the cognitive and socioemotional mechanisms that shape how curiosity translates into different forms of information seeking. Based on data from 701 Grade 1 students in Nepal (45% female; Mage = 6.46 years), we found that children’s curiosity, measured by willingness-to-pay and breadth-of-exploration scores, predicts proactive information seeking (e.g., question asking), with stronger associations among students higher in social confidence and executive function. Curiosity, measured by willingness-to-pay and depth-of-exploration scores, also predicts attentive information seeking, with these associations being stronger among children with higher executive function. Together, these results indicate that curiosity operates as a motivational factor that interacts with auxiliary socioemotional and cognitive skills to facilitate proactive and attentive information-seeking behaviors in real-world classroom settings. Accordingly, fostering effective learning requires supporting not only curiosity itself but also the skills that enable students to translate their internal drive into contextually appropriate information-seeking.

articles.title: Can a Brief Informational Intervention Facilitate Culture-Add Hiring? An Experimental Vignette Study
articles.authors: George Ko; Lukas Wallrich
articles.abstract: Culture-add, hiring for what a candidate brings beyond what a team already has, has gained traction in practitioner discourse but has remained largely disconnected from organizational theory. We argue that culture-add is best understood as complementary person-group (P-G) fit applied to behavioural attributes, extending a literature that has largely confined complementary fit to knowledge and skills. Realizing culture-add in practice, however, depends on hiring managers actively recognizing complementary value rather than defaulting to similarity. In a vignette experiment (N = 264; 521 hiring decisions), working professionals with hiring experience evaluated pairs of culture-fit and culture-add candidates for two product roles after reading three short hiring-related articles; the intervention group received one article introducing culture-add hiring. Relative to controls, intervention participants showed a significantly smaller culture-fit advantage in hireability ratings and were significantly more likely to hire culture-add candidates (47% instead of 35% of decisions). Effects held across levels of hiring experience. These findings provide initial experimental evidence that complementary P-G fit can be operationalized at the behavioural level, that culture-add is manipulable in hiring evaluations, and that a brief informational intervention can shift both ratings and selection decisions.

articles.title: Too Distracted to Remember: Does Attentional Disengagement Underlie the Photo-Taking Impairment Effect?
articles.authors: Marc Croes; Benedetta Kreuwels; Tanja van der Marel; Guido Faber; Steven Verheyen
articles.abstract: The photo-taking impairment effect refers to the finding that photographing an object can impair subsequent memory for that object. Two explanations have been proposed: the offloading account suggests that people rely on the camera as an external memory store instead of encoding information themselves, while the attentional disengagement account argues that attention is diverted from the object toward the act of photographing, resulting in shallower encoding. The attentional disengagement account was tested in two preregistered experiments. Participants viewed paintings under three within-subject conditions: observation only, simple photo-taking, and difficult photo-taking. In the difficult photo-taking condition, participants optimized photographs using camera settings, increasing the attentional demands of photo-taking. To rule out memory offloading, participants were informed that they would not have access to their photographs during the memory test. Increasing attentional demands did not produce greater memory impairment, contrary to the attentional disengagement hypothesis. The longer exposure time in the difficult photo-taking condition likely compensated for any memory impairments due to increased attentional disengagement. Moreover, when participants were given additional viewing time after photographing the paintings, the photo-taking impairment effect disappeared. Together, these results indicate that taking sufficient time to thoroughly encode photographed items can offset the photo-taking impairment effect.

articles.title: NeuroGym: An open resource for developing and sharing neuroscience tasks
articles.authors: Giulia Crocioni; Alexander Hadjiivanov; Nathan Cloos; Caterina Barezzi; Sara Fuentes; Joao Barbosa; Dani L. Bodor; Parva Alavian; Jordi Pastor-Ciurana; Marta Fradera
articles.abstract: Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) trained on specific cognitive tasks have established themselves as useful tools to study the brain, although the absence of standardized platforms and implementations still constitute important challenges. Towards this goal, we present NeuroGym, an open-source Python package that provides a large collection of carefully curated and customizable neuroscience tasks to test and compare network models. Building upon the Gymnasium platform, NeuroGym tasks (1) are implemented in a high-level flexible Python framework; (2) possess a shared interface tailored to common needs of neuroscience tasks that facilitates their design and usage; (3) support the training of ANNs using Reinforcement Learning and Supervised Learning techniques. We demonstrate its versatility here by using NeuroGym to train networks and compare their performance with real behavioral data from neuroscience tasks, including decision-making, context-dependent categorization, oculomotor delayed response, and a novel mice foraging task newly introduced here. Likewise, we show its usefulness to train networks across functions via simultaneous multi-task protocols. NeuroGym is a community-driven effort that contributes to a rapidly evolving open ecosystem of neural network development, data analysis, and model-data comparison, and it allows easy assembly of new tasks by modifying existing ones in a hierarchical and modular fashion.

articles.title: Educator Distress Post-COVID: Associations with Educator Characteristics, Family Income, Work Intentions, and Job Satisfaction
articles.authors: Matthew James Hirshberg
articles.abstract:
Purpose: Linkages between school system employee (SSE; including teachers) wellbeing and educational system outcomes suggest that addressing SSE distress is essential. The purpose of this research was to estimate the prevalence of anxiety, depression and occupational burnout among SSEs post-pandemic, and identify correlates of distress symptoms post-COVID.
Research Methods/Approach: In this observational study, baseline data from a randomized controlled trial of digital wellbeing trainings was used to estimate SSE average and clinically meaningful levels of anxiety and depression, occupational burnout, perceptions of administrator support, and job satisfaction. Multivariable linear and logistic regressions were used.
Findings: After schools returned to pre-pandemic routines, SSEs (n=827; teacher n=449) reported significantly higher average (0.90 and 0.61 SD) and clinically meaningful levels (Odds Ratios 1.63-2.01) of anxiety and depression than the general population. Perceptions of administrator appreciation and satisfaction with students but not COVID anxiety explained unique variance in occupational burnout, controlling for anxiety and depression. Lower earning, female and White SSEs reported greater distress, but White SSEs were 2.2 times more likely than Black SSEs to intend to return. Teachers reported higher levels of occupational burnout, but not anxiety or depression. 
Implications: Study results suggest that implementing programs and systems designed to improve SSE wellbeing, increasing salaries for lower earning SSEs, and encouraging cultures of occupational wellbeing are promising avenues for future research and practice. Investigating the reasons for Black SSEs disproportionately lower intentions to return to their jobs is also an important area for future research.

articles.title: Color naming needs a non-uniform perceptual prior: Individual differences in hue scaling and the vision–language interface
articles.authors: David Henry Peterzell
articles.abstract: How a language divides the continuous spectrum of color into named categories is increasingly explained as efficient compression: a color vocabulary is treated as a code that identifies colors as accurately as possible using as few names as possible. Every such model needs a description of how similar colors look, and nearly all use the same one: a standard, evenly spaced color space (CIE L*a*b*, or CIELAB) in which each color carries a fixed, symmetric “blur” of perceptual uncertainty. Here I argue that this assumption about perception is wrong in a way that matters. When people with normal color vision judge hues, they disagree in systematic ways. The pattern of that disagreement shows that hue perception is carved into roughly eight narrow, largely independent regions that tile the color circle and align with the boundaries between color categories rather than their best examples. I show, first in plain terms and then formally, how to build this measured structure into the standard model, by stretching the color circle at the category boundaries, and report a first computational test. A fuller test substitutes the reshaped space into the information-bottleneck model and rescores all 110 languages. On the equiluminant hue circle the structure describes, the warp neither clearly improves nor harms how efficiently languages name color, a near-wash that is itself informative about where perceptual structure matters. The proposal yields a prediction that can be tested directly with public data and code, and is informative whether it succeeds or fails.

articles.title: Stress biases alcohol choice across timescales in daily life, but context gates its expression
articles.authors: Jonas Dora; Caspar J. Van Lissa; Maxwell Shinn; Adam M. Kuczynski; Gavin Kelly; Aleksandra Kaurin; Kevin Michael King
articles.abstract: Laboratory studies consistently support the hypothesis that people drink alcohol to regulate stress and negative affect, yet ecological momentary assessment (EMA) studies have repeatedly failed to detect the predicted within-person association between stress or negative affect and drinking. We applied a value-based decision-making (VBDM) framework to test where in the chain from affective state to behavior this disconnect arises. We recruited 250 at-risk adult drinkers (median AUDIT=9) via Prolific. Participants completed a 14-day EMA protocol with two daily scheduled prompts plus optional event-contingent self-initiation following stressors (5,978 completed EMA assessments; 99.5% of choice trials completed). At each prompt, they self-reported affective and contextual states and completed 160 trials of a cognitive choice task pairing personalized alcoholic and non-alcoholic drinks. Drift diffusion modeling decomposed choice and response time data into decision parameters; mixed-effects LASSO and random forest models identified moderators of affect-choice associations. Stressful events increased alcohol choice by ~10 percentage points and selectively biased evidence accumulation toward alcohol without affecting decision carefulness or value sensitivity, replicating prior laboratory findings. Raw associations attenuated when covariates were included; contextual moderators (presence of strangers, alcohol expectancies, time-of-day, alcohol cue visibility) consistently shifted choice in directions predicted by VBDM. Affect regulation processes are detectable at the level of choice tendency in daily life, but their behavioral expression depends on context, suggesting the theory may require reformulation as a context-dependent mechanism. Our sample consisted of at-risk, non-treatment-seeking adult US drinkers recruited online; our findings may not generalize to treatment-seeking, adolescent, or non-Western samples.

articles.title: The Link Between Parental Education and Teenagers’ School Grades is Largely Genetic – Evidence from a Children-of-Twins Study
articles.authors: J.B. Bernardo; Hans Fredrik Sunde; Fartein Ask Torvik; Elsje van Bergen
articles.abstract: Why do children of more highly educated parents obtain better school grades? We addressed whether this reflects environmental effects of parents or familial factors shared across generations, using Norwegian population-register data (1.6 million individuals from 200k extended families) and genetically informative family designs. At age 16, teacher evaluations and national written exams in Norwegian (L1), English (L2), and mathematics were highly similar, with strong phenotypic correlations (r = .68-.84) and near-unit genetic correlations (r = .87-1.00). Associations between parental educational attainment and offspring grades were comparable across teacher evaluations, national exams, and grade point averages (r = .28-.36). For grade point averages, children-of-twins-and-siblings modelling indicated that most parent-offspring resemblance reflected genetic transmission (66%), alongside significant contributions from extended-family (25%) and parental transmission (10%). These findings suggest that intergenerational stability of education largely reflects genetic and extended-family transmission rather than teacher bias or direct effects of parental education alone.

articles.title: Gradient Phonetic Structure in Bilingual Speech: A Multidimensional Analysis of L1 and L2 Production
articles.authors: Christine Shea; Ciara Tapanes
articles.abstract: This exploratory study examines the production of Spanish palatal segments and English [j] by Spanish-English bilinguals. Guided by the revised Speech Learning Model (SLM-r), we assume that similar L1 and L2 sounds are represented within an integrated phonetic system, where categories emerge through gradient patterns of redistribution. Productions from 24 female Mexican Spanish speakers were analyzed using the unsupervised learning techniques of Principal Components Analysis (PCA), Hierarchical Cluster Analysis (HCA) to characterize the structure and grouping of the bilingual phonetic space. Complementing these approaches, we use Mahalanobis Distance (MD) to characterize the compactness of participants’ productions. PCA showed that Spanish and English productions were organized along overlapping acoustic dimensions, particularly frication and intensity/temporal structure. HCA revealed weakly bounded clustering, with stronger internal differentiation for Spanish and greater overlap for English, consistent with gradient rather than discrete category organization. MD indicated that position-specific comparisons were more informative than global language-level measures. Together, the findings support an SLM-r account in which bilingual phonetic categories are gradient, dynamically linked, and shaped by both acoustic similarity and contextual organization within a shared bilingual phonetic space.

articles.title: Dickman’s Impulsivity Inventory: Confirmatory factor analyses of existing models and development of a shortened scale (8-DII)
articles.authors: George Horne; Adrian Furnham
articles.abstract: Dickman’s Impulsivity Inventory is a popular measure of impulsivity which differentiates between when impulsivity is functional and when it is functional. Despite its continued use over the 36 years since its original publication, there are considerable concerns regarding the factor structure of the measure. At the time writing, the scale has been translated into many different languages, all needing to be revised by removing different items for varying factor structures and item inclusions. In each case, all item removals were done in absence of theoretical or semantic rationale. In Study 1, we first assess current item models with a series of confirmatory analyses, and use an exploratory factor analysis to propose an 8-item model. In Study 2, we test this 8-item model alongside the best fitting existing models. Results showed that our 8-item had better fit indices while maintaining adequate internal reliability. We propose this reduced model as a more practical measure for both research and practice.

articles.title: Motions Speak Louder Than Words: Designing Human-like AV Speed Profiles at Crosswalks with a Bayesian Optimisation–Driven Adaptive Experiment
articles.authors: Yuwei Wang; Gustav Markkula; Yee Mun Lee
articles.abstract: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) must communicate yielding intent to pedestrians in mixed traffic to ensure safe interaction, and vehicle decelerating motion is a promising implicit communication channel at crosswalks. Designing AV deceleration motion remains difficult because it is continuous with interdependent kinematics (e.g., braking onset, deceleration rate), making fixed‑design experiments inefficient. Therefore, we parameterised deceleration behaviour using a minimum‑jerk model (MJM), which can generate smooth speed profiles from tuneable parameters (stopping distance and duration). We will then formulate profile design as an optimisation problem that maximises pedestrian‑rated safety and efficiency. We will conduct an adaptive experiment in an immersive pedestrian simulator in which Bayesian optimisation sequentially proposes profiles that are most likely to improve safety and efficiency ratings based on pedestrians’ evaluations of previous profiles. Preliminary results from fitting MJM to 62 naturalistic human profiles indicate that the MJM reproduces the overall human profile shape (median RMSE = 0.88 m/s) with only 2 parameters (i.e., stopping distance, duration), supporting its use as a profile generator for the experiment. This work can contribute a human-in-the-loop framework for designing AV motion as an implicit interface.

articles.title: FalseResMem: A Neural Network to Predict False Memories in Visual Recognition Tasks
articles.authors: Anastasiia Mikhailova; Wilma A. Bainbridge
articles.abstract: Predicting false alarms, i.e., when novel images are mistakenly identified as familiar, is crucial for understanding visual memory bias. Prior studies show that some images consistently elicit more false alarms than others, so to model these patterns, we introduce FalseResMem, the first neural network designed to predict image-level false alarm rate (FAR). The model combines ImageNet-pretrained ResNet50 features with a retrained AlexNet-like architecture to capture image properties that drive false recognitions. Trained on a large-scale object memory dataset, FalseResMem achieves consistent performance (Spearman’s rank correlation = 0.48 ± 0.13; max = 0.58, 10-fold cross-validation) and FalseResMem’s predictions successfully generalize to other image categories, including scenes, art, faces, and symbols, demonstrating its robustness. This contribution highlights the potential of integrating error-based metrics into visual memory modeling through a simple FalseResMem package, offering researchers a practical way to predict false alarms and investigate the image features that systematically shape false memories.

articles.title: Critical review of Artificial Intelligence for qualitative analysis: not just whether to use it but how and when
articles.authors: Paulina Bondaronek; Emma Ward; Susie Weller; Laura Dennison; Thomas Gültzow; Lisa Marie Ballard; Ben Ainsworth; Daniella Watson; Aimee E Pink; Aliya Amirova
articles.abstract:
Introduction: Artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming increasingly visible in qualitative research, with growing interest in its potential to support or accelerate analytic work. However, debates in this area are largely polarised. Few papers offer a balanced, critical consideration of the role of AI for qualitative analysis. This paper addresses this gap.
Methods: This critical review was developed by an interdisciplinary researcher community (the [redacted] network) through a series of discussions and investigations, synthesised into a set of core domains.
Findings: Seven domains were identified as central to AI-assisted qualitative analysis: epistemology; increasing analytic capabilities; research integrity; trust; equity and bias; consent and confidentiality; and evolving researcher expertise. Across domains, AI offers potential benefits, including enabling “Big Qual” analysis and supporting hybrid human–AI approaches. Limitations and risks were deliberated, including misalignment between AI and interpretive traditions, challenges to transparency and veracity, bias concerns, and implications for training and sustaining qualitative competence. 
Conclusions: Recommendations on how and when to use AI for qualitative analysis without compromising rigour, equity and competence are proposed. Current responsible use requires the development of transparent, robust, and critically informed methodologies that address differences between AI approaches, power and sustainability, while retaining human interpretation.

articles.title: The Perceived Utility and Scope of Ecological Momentary Assessment and Interventions for Non-Suicidal Self-Injury: A Needs-Based Dynamic Risk Perspective
articles.authors: Mirthe Luijsmans; Laurence Claes; Ruth Tatnell; Marlies Houben; Thomas Vaessen; Tom Smeets; Inez Myin-Germeys; Glenn Kiekens
articles.abstract:
Background: Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) is a serious mental health problem characterized by rapid within-person fluctuations in thoughts and urges. Yet, treatments rely on retrospective and static approaches for assessments and interventions. Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) and Ecological Momentary Interventions (EMIs) may enable more dynamic support in real time. This study examined the perceived utility and scope of these new digital modalities from the perspectives of treatment-seeking individuals and mental health professionals.
Methods: Semi-structured interviews were conducted with 30 treatment-seeking individuals engaging in NSSI, after completing a four-week EMA period, and with 14 professionals who used EMA in treatment. Interviews explored experiences with EMA and perspectives on EMIs and were analyzed using reflexive thematic analysis.  
Results: Three themes were identified. First, EMA and EMIs were perceived as extending therapeutic 
support by enhancing awareness, reflection, and skill use in daily life. Second, participants emphasized that EMIs must be tailored to changing needs in function of changing risk. This informed the development of a Needs-Based Dynamic Risk and Intervention Model for NSSI, distinguishing needs and interventions through a traffic-light analogy across low (i.e., mental health promotion), moderate (i.e., vulnerability regulation), and high (i.e., behavioral interruption) risk states. Third, EMIs were viewed as valuable when embedded in blended care and implemented through shared decision-making, balancing system- and user-initiated support.
Conclusion: Integrating EMA and EMIs into treatment has potential to provide needs-tailored support for individuals who self-injure. Future work should focus on empirically validating the proposed dynamic model and co-designing EMI components that address fluctuating needs in daily life.

articles.title: The effects of animals’ touch on soldiers’ stress during war
articles.authors: Neta Ehrlich; Leehe Peled-Avron
articles.abstract: Human-animal interactions can have beneficial effects on well-being. This study examined the effects of animals’ touch and interaction on soldiers' stress during war.  Soldiers during war are at a high-risk for developing mental health difficulties. In a sample of 31 soldiers on active duty during wartime, participants completed online questionnaires, rating their comfort from an animal companion, their human-animal interactions, and their levels of trait and state overall anxiety.  We hypothesized that positive human-animal interaction will result in lower stress among soldiers during war. Contrary to the initial hypothesis, soldiers who reported higher levels of trait anxiety (as opposed to state anxiety) experienced greater comfort from animal interactions. A clear divide emerged between different groups of soldiers. Those who grew up with pets, female soldiers, and non-combat soldiers all reported similar tendencies: they engaged in more frequent human-animal interactions, derived greater comfort from companion animals, and exhibited higher levels of both trait and state anxiety. In contrast, combat soldiers and male soldiers showed markedly different results. These groups reported lower frequencies of positive human-animal interactions, less perceived comfort from companion animals, and lower anxiety levels overall. This study is the first to provide real-time (and not retrospective) empirical data on human-animal interactions during war, a phenomenon that was documented but not studied empirically before in a non-laboratory or therapeutic setting. It seems human-animal interaction does not reduce stress levels and that those who engage in these interactions have higher stress levels in general. We suggest further research on the relationship between human-animal interaction and stress, its processes, and possible effects, especially in the context of military service and war.

articles.title: The Sophie Sugar Addiction Scale: Conceptualisation, Development and Validation
articles.authors: Sophie Hascher; Ori Ossmy; Halley M. Pontes
articles.abstract: Despite growing evidence for food addiction, sugar addiction remains underexplored. This study developed and validated the Sophie Sugar Addiction Scale (SSAS), a psychometric tool assessing sugar addiction as a distinct behavioural addiction. Based on Griffiths’ (2005) components model, an initial 18-item scale covering six dimensions (salience, mood modification, tolerance, withdrawal, conflict, relapse) was administered to 1,017 adults (M_age = 28.7, SD = 8.8; 41% female). Confirmatory Factor Analysis led to a refined six-item version, which demonstrated high internal consistency (α = .87, Ω = .86) and strong convergent validity with the Yale Food Addiction Scale 2.0. The SSAS also showed meaningful associations with ADHD, OCD, depression, anxiety, and stress. Notably, 28.9% of participants met criteria for sugar addiction without meeting food addiction thresholds, supporting its uniqueness. The SSAS provides a reliable, valid tool for identifying sugar addictive behaviours in research and clinical contexts.

articles.title: Agenda for Culturally Competent Neuropsychological Research and Testing (ACCENT): A Brief Checklist for the Responsible Conduct and Reporting of Cross-Cultural Neuropsychological Research
articles.authors: Kaylee S. van Wyhe; Arish Mudra Rakshasa-Loots; Kevin G. F. Thomas
articles.abstract: Cross-cultural neuropsychological research is essential for understanding how and why cognitive performance might vary substantially across different sociodemographic, socioeconomic, and linguistic contexts. The responsible conduct of such research requires careful attention to ethical, methodological, and interpretive challenges that are not fully addressed by existing guidelines. We describe the development of the ACCENT (Agenda for Culturally Competent Neuropsychological Research and Testing) Checklist, a concise, practical tool designed to guide ethical, culturally sensitive, and methodologically rigorous cross-cultural neuropsychological research, from design through dissemination. The checklist was developed using a stepwise synthesis of comprehensive literature review and the authors' combined experience conducting neuropsychological research in diverse Global South contexts. ACCENT is organised into six domains: (a) research team composition, (b) community and participant engagement, (c) study design, (d) test selection and adaptation, (e) cultural competency training, and (f) transparent reporting. The checklist contains 19 items: 15 classed as required, two as conditional, one as aspirational, and one as conditional-aspirational. By complementing and extending existing frameworks, ACCENT addresses the full research lifecycle, including dimensions of epistemic justice and structural equity that prior guidelines leave unaddressed. We present ACCENT as a proposed framework and describe a planned multi-method validation programme comprising Delphi consensus, content validity, cognitive interviewing, and simulation studies.

articles.title: When One Measure Hides Multiple Mechanisms: Interpreting Accuracy, Similarity, and Assimilation in Psychological Data
articles.authors: Dan Schley
articles.abstract: Many psychological findings are claims about closeness: judgments become more accurate, attitudes more similar, or estimates closer to an anchor. These claims often rely on folded scores, such as mean absolute deviation and mean squared error, which remove direction before summarizing distance. If the correct answer is 50, estimates of 40 and 60 are both scored as 10 units away. That summary is useful, but it can make different psychological patterns look identical. A folded score can change through a position channel, when responses move toward or away from a reference point, a dispersion channel, when responses become more or less spread out, or both. This manuscript shows how decomposing these components give researchers a richer picture of the psychological mechanisms behind folded-score effects. Across reanalyses of wisdom-of-the-inner-crowd, ideological judgment, and anchoring studies, we show that folded scores can clarify, obscure, or reverse theoretical conclusions. Researchers can keep folded scores as summaries, but should report the position and dispersion components that explain what changed.

articles.title: Large language models capture universal relationship knowledge but compress cultural variation
articles.authors: Yijie Zhang; Siyi Li; Xi Cheng; Chen Guoqiu; JingKai Li; Yujie Wu; Yinyin Zang; Siyang Luo; Yipeng Kang; Renfen Hu
articles.abstract: Can large language models (LLMs) represent how people understand relationships across cultures? We tested four prominent LLMs against human benchmark data from more than 20,000 participants across 19 global regions using the FAVEE framework, which represents 159 interpersonal relationships along 33 relational features. LLMs closely matched human ratings at the aggregate level and reproduced the broad multidimensional structure of relationship knowledge. However, this apparent human-likeness masked poor cross-cultural discrimination. LLMs compressed differences between regions, pulled culturally distinctive regions toward more generic profiles, and reversed some documented cultural patterns. Additional analyses showed that human variation was associated with sociocultural and macroenvironmental factors such as religion and modernization, whereas LLM variation was organized more strongly by language similarity and widely used cultural dimension frameworks. These results show that LLMs can reproduce shared structure in human social knowledge but underrepresent culturally distinctive relational meanings. LLMs data should therefore be used cautiously in cross-cultural psychology studies, especially when the goal is to explain cultural variation rather than approximate broad human averages.

articles.title: Improving autobiographical episodic memory, quality of life, and sense of self with a smartphone intervention in early dementia: A case study
articles.authors: Miranda Chang; Bryan Hong; Morgan Barense
articles.abstract: In memory disorders such as Alzheimer’s disease, recent autobiographical memories are disproportionately vulnerable to loss, yet most traditional reminiscence therapies focus on remote past events. We present a case study examining whether a digital reminiscence intervention designed to support memory for recent experiences can improve episodic recall and psychosocial outcomes in neurodegenerative memory impairment. G.F., a 79-year-old man with early-stage dementia, completed an 11-week personalized intervention using HippoCamera, a neuroscience-based smartphone application that helps users generate and review multimodal memory cues from everyday events. Events that G.F. reviewed using HippoCamera were recalled with greater episodic detail than events that were recorded but not reviewed. Post-intervention, G.F. reported improvements in quality of life, life satisfaction, self-concept, and perceived episodic and spatial memory abilities, along with reduced depressive symptoms. Qualitative feedback revealed that the intervention helped G.F. regain confidence, re-engage socially, and feel more optimistic about the future. These findings suggest that digital interventions targeting memory for recent experiences—a domain often overlooked in traditional reminiscence therapy—may provide benefits to cognition and well-being in the early stages of dementia. This work highlights the promise of HippoCamera as an accessible, neuroscience-informed tool to support memory and well-being in those experiencing memory loss.

articles.title: Experience Shapes Early Noun Comprehension from 8-18 Months: The Roles of Word Frequency and Referent Familiarity
articles.authors: Lily Zihui Zhu; Andrei Amatuni; Shannon Egan-Dailey; Hallie Garrison; Evgenii Kalenkovich; Sharath Koorathota; Lillianna Righter; Shaelise Tor; Elika Bergelson
articles.abstract: We investigated how experience with particular nouns and referents influences infant word comprehension by combining home recordings with in-lab eyetracking. In Study 1, infants (n=44) participated in a yearlong longitudinal study which included monthly home recordings (daylong audio and hourlong video, 6-17mo.) and hand-tailored eyetracking experiments (every two months, 8-18mo.). Word comprehension was operationalized as how much more infants looked at named images than distractors. Each session tested 16 words: 8 held constant across all infants at each timepoint ("generic" common nouns, e.g., "shoe"), and 8 hand-picked for each session from each child's recent home recordings. Of the hand-picked items, half appeared as images familiar to children from home (e.g., their own ball); the other half and all generic items appeared as prototypical child-friendly (but not individually-tailored) images. Results showed expected improvements with age, and an effect of word frequency (particularly at 12 and 14mo. timepoints) but not referent familiarity. In Study 2 (n=247), cross-sectional controls completed the same eyetracking experiments (i.e., those hand-tailored for Study 1 children) as a stronger test of the roles of frequency and referent familiarity. Here, we did find an effect of referent familiarity. Study 2 infants understood words less well than those in Study 1, particularly when tested with images of referents selected from Study 1 children's homes. Together, these findings highlight the role of experience in shaping early noun comprehension and suggest that once infants understand words, they readily generalize them to new instances beyond those they are familiar with.

articles.title: Elevated suicidal thoughts and behaviors, proximal suicide risk factors, and non-suicidal self-injury among adults reporting symptoms of Cannabinoid Hyperemesis Syndrome: Results from a national survey of US adults
articles.authors: Brian M. Hicks; Blair J. Whittington; Amanda Price; Paula Goldman; Mark A. Ilgen
articles.abstract:
Background: Cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome (CHS), a disorder characterized by severe nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain among heavy cannabis users. We previously showed that CHS symptoms are associated with several behavioral and psychological characteristics linked to psychosocial impairment. We examined links between CHS symptoms and suicidal thoughts, behaviors, and proximal suicide risk factors.   
Methods: We used data from the National Firearms, Alcohol, Cannabis, and Suicide survey, a nationally representative survey of 7034 US adults. Items assessed symptoms of CHS and suicidal thoughts and behaviors, proximal suicide risk factors, and non-suicidal self-injury. Comparisons focused on: those with daily cannabis use and CHS symptoms (n = 191), those with daily cannabis use without CHS symptoms (n = 882), those with past year cannabis use but not daily use (n = 1288), and those without past year cannabis use (n = 4673).
Results: Those with CHS symptoms reported the highest lifetime and past 12 months prevalence of suicidal thoughts and behaviors with nearly all being significantly higher than those with daily cannabis use without CHS symptoms. Those with CHS symptoms also reported higher mean-levels of proximal risk factors for suicide (i.e., perceived burdensomeness, thwarted belongingness, defeat, entrapment) than all the other groups. 
Conclusions: Those with CHS symptoms reported especially high rates of suicidal thoughts, behaviors, and attempts even when compared to others with daily cannabis use. People with CHS symptoms appear to be at high risk of suicide, possibly related to distress from their gastrointestinal symptoms and psychiatric, substance use, and medical comorbidities.

articles.title: When Attentional Training May Leave Self-Grasping Intact: A Yogācāra Account of the Manas Layer with Implications for Theorizing Mindfulness-Based Interventions
articles.authors: Zhenguo Lai
articles.abstract:
Objectives. Contemporary psychotherapy and mindfulness-based interventions (MBIs) show well-documented benefits for symptom reduction, emotion regulation, and attentional control. Yet they are most often framed as refining an already given self rather than as modifying the deeper structures through which experience is centered as “mine,” a level that has received comparatively little explicit modeling. A related finding—that practitioners can advance meaningfully in decentering and meta-awareness without equivalent shifts in background self-grasping—has not been fully explained at the level of mechanism (Lindahl et al., 2017; Vago & Silbersweig, 2012). The present paper addresses this gap by drawing on the Yogācāra (vijñānavāda) tradition of Indian Buddhist philosophy.
Methods. The paper employs textual reconstruction and comparative conceptual analysis. The classical eight-consciousness doctrine is reconstructed as a five-layer mental architecture (L1: sensation-perception; L2: conceptual-reasoning; L3: background self-grasping; L4: latent-structural; L5: post-transformation non-grasping) and then brought into systematic dialogue with contemporary self models, mindfulness mechanism research, and cognitive neuroscience.
Results. Three theoretical contributions are developed. First, the seventh consciousness (kliṣṭa-manas) is proposed as a distinct background layer of self-grasping, offering a candidate structural account of the self-appropriation process that is left implicit in many existing self models—and suggesting one possible reason why L2-level interventions may carry an inherent depth limit (termed here level-blindness). Second, the eighth consciousness (ālaya-vijñāna) is reconceived as a dynamic generative field rather than a static store, reframing how deep dispositional patterns may maintain or transform over time. Third, āśraya-parāvṛtti (transformation of the basis) is sketched as a possible “third horizon” for psychological change, complementing symptom management and acceptance-based approaches by directing attention to gradual shifts in background self-grasping.
Conclusions. The Yogācāra framework does not aim to replace existing therapies; it offers a structural supplement that may help clarify why mindfulness gains in attentional training and decentering can coexist with persistent background self-grasping. Testable implications are outlined for nonattachment research, MBI mechanism studies, and future measurement development.

articles.title: A Six-Layer Yogācāra Model of Emotion Generation: Implications for Constructionist Emotion Theory
articles.authors: Zhenguo Lai
articles.abstract: Contemporary emotion science lacks a Yogācāra‐grounded model capable of engaging constructionism’s central tension: operational reliance on category-like units despite rejecting natural-kind categories. Drawing on Asaṅga’s Yogācārabhūmi and the five omnipresent mental factors (sarvatraga-caittas), this article develops a six-layer model of emotion generation—from phenomenal awareness through categorical apperception to architectural recognition—that preserves the constructionist insight that emotions are constructed rather than kinds while supplying analytic depth below the categorical layer constructionism has not theorized. The article offers (i) a systematic Yogācāra emotion model for English-language affective science, (ii) a falsifiable granularity prediction via the Granularity-Awareness Scale (GRAS), and (iii) a non-polemical bridge between contemplative and affective science.

articles.title: When Awareness Becomes the Obstacle: A Yogācāra Diagnosis of the Mindfulness Ceiling in Mindfulness-Based Interventions
articles.authors: Zhenguo Lai
articles.abstract: [Problem] Mindfulness-based interventions (MBIs) produce well-documented clinical benefits, yet a possible dissociation between attentional skill and deeper self-structural transformation remains undertheorized: some experienced practitioners attain high scores on standard mindfulness measures while showing limited corresponding change in self-referential processing—a pattern this paper terms the mindfulness ceiling. [Gap] Existing critical lines—political-economic, philological, and phenomenological—address the limitations of contemporary mindfulness at the levels of context, definition, and symptomatology, but none identifies the consciousness-structural mechanism that generates the ceiling. [Approach] Drawing on the Yogācāra eight-consciousness architecture, this paper locates the ceiling at the stratum of kliṣṭa-manas—a persistent background process of self-appropriation beneath ordinary introspective access—and diagnoses the awareness trap: a three-layer progressive entrapment in which identification shifts from mental content (Layer 1), to the observer-position (Layer 2, the primary ceiling), to awareness of awareness itself (Layer 3, commonly mistaken for liberation), each corresponding to a distinct strategy of manas co-optation. [Claim] The ceiling is best understood not as a dosage problem but as a structural-mechanism problem, and moving beyond it may require a shift from dose-escalation to mechanism-switching within MBI design: the introduction of complementary interruption mechanisms—drawing on processes distinct from decentering and self-as-context—once observational refinement reaches its natural upper bound. [Contributions] The paper offers (i) a Yogācāra structural diagnosis of the mindfulness ceiling, (ii) a preliminary nine-mechanism typology of self-grasping interruption organized around a common four-stage architecture (accumulation–peak–interruption–disclosure), (iii) a testable behavioral prediction—the awareness-trap profile—together with a proposed companion measure, the Observer Identification Scale (OIS).

articles.title: Contextually Attuned Understanding of Epistemic Curiosity in Early Childhood: An Emic Exploration in Nepal
articles.authors: Kenji Kitamura; My H. Do; Dipu Shakya
articles.abstract: Child-centered and play-based education interventions, increasingly adopted worldwide, often aim to foster epistemic curiosity – the desire for new information – to enhance children’s learning. Dominant Western frameworks conceptualize curiosity as expressed through independent exploration and proactive questioning, treating these behaviors as prototypical indicators of curiosity. However, this characterization is largely derived from research in high-income Western contexts, raising concerns about its cross-cultural validity in non-Western low- and middle-income countries. This qualitative study examines how epistemic curiosity is expressed and interpreted in early childhood classrooms in Nepal. We analyzed data from focus groups with parents and child development experts, interviews with teachers, and classroom observations. Findings revealed expressions of curiosity that extend beyond Western prototypes, particularly attentive information-seeking, such as focused listening. These attentive behaviors were socially valued because they aligned with relational norms emphasizing respect and harmony. By conceptualizing curiosity as a universal construct with context-adaptive manifestations shaped by cultural and sociodemographic dynamics, this study advances a more inclusive framework for studying curiosity across diverse contexts. It challenges indicators that privilege independence and psychological autonomy as the primary expressions of curiosity. This study also highlights auxiliary skills that operate alongside curiosity and variation in adults’ perceptions across demographic contexts.

articles.authors: Zhenguo Lai
articles.abstract:

articles.title: A Six-Layer Yogācāra Model of Emotion Generation: Implications for Constructionist Emotion Theory
articles.authors: Zhenguo Lai
articles.abstract:
Contemporary emotion science lacks a Yogācāra‐grounded model capable of engaging constructionism’s central tension: operational reliance on category-like units despite rejecting natural-kind categories. Drawing on Asaṅga’s Yogācārabhūmi and the five omnipresent mental factors (sarvatraga-caittas), this article develops a six-layer model of emotion generation—from phenomenal awareness through categorical apperception to architectural recognition—that preserves the constructionist insight that emotions are constructed rather than kinds while supplying analytic depth below the categorical layer constructionism has not theorized. articles.authors: Zhenguo Lai
articles.abstract:

articles.title: Beyond speed and beyond prediction: The multi-faceted nature of speech processing in preschoolers and how it relates to language development
articles.authors: Meg Cychosz; Sonia Patrizi; Amy Martinez
articles.abstract: Efficient language processing requires both the ability to predict as well as flexibly revise. Numerous studies have demonstrated that young children can make predictions during online language processing tasks, but less is known about their ability to quickly revise interpretations when initial predictions are infelicitous. Adults readily revise initial interpretations during language processing across linguistic domains—recovering from garden paths, adjusting to acoustic variation, and resolving lexical ambiguities. However, it is relatively unknown how revision mechanisms develop in early childhood, and, even more critically, how revision ability relates to the acquisition of language. We examined four- and five-year-olds’ (N=60) phonological revision abilities using an eye-tracking paradigm that captured children’s initial phonological interpretation of a word and subsequent revision and recovery. We characterized children’s processing along 11 distinct dimensions, and employed unsupervised clustering analyses to identify three distinct speech processing types among the children. Results showed substantial individual differences in processing strategies with different patterns of initial interpretation, as well as subsequent revision, underlying similar language outcomes. Processing type membership significantly predicted children’s vocabulary and pre-literacy skills, suggesting that phonological revision mechanisms may underpin multiple domains of language development. Overall, our findings align with both Bayesian inference and error-driven learning accounts where the probabilistic prediction and resolution of mismatches may drive language development across multiple linguistic domains.

articles.title: Smart and authentic: How anthropomorphic features of AI chatbots drive reactance among Indian university students
articles.authors: Bashir Ansari; Ahmed Sameer; Deepika Sharma
articles.abstract: This study integrates privacy calculus and psychological reactance theories to examine the mechanism by which the anthropomorphic characteristics of AI chatbots, such as perceived authenticity and competence, drive reactance through perceived privacy concerns and identity threat among Indian university students. Survey data from 449 students were analyzed using covariance-based structural equation modelling (CB-SEM). The results show that perceived authenticity is significantly associated with reactance in a complementary mediated relationship, alongside a direct positive association with reactance. Perceived competence operated through a competitive mediated relationship; while it indirectly increased reactance via perceived privacy concerns and identity threat, it also showed a significant, direct negative association with reactance. Findings suggest that students’ reactance to AI chatbots arises in response to anthropomorphic AI personas, triggered directly by the perceived invasiveness of authentic features and indirectly by the surveillance risks associated with competent AI chatbots. Finally, we argue for transparent, machine-like intelligence expansion, rather than deceptive human-like features, as key targets for future AI design and deployment.

articles.title: Shared neural representations of barriers to agents’ actions and objects’ motions
articles.authors: Minjae J. Kim; Samuel Maione; Shari Liu
articles.abstract: In the human brain, distinct regions preferentially respond to the actions and interactions of animate agents, and the motions and interactions of inanimate objects. Yet, evidence across the cognitive sciences suggests that we process animate agents fundamentally as physical bodies (Liu et al., 2025). This suggestion predicts that processing of agents and objects depend in part on shared neural representations of physically-relevant features. Here, we used functional brain imaging to test this prediction. Our case study concerned a fundamental, developmentally early-emerging intuition that because agents and objects are solid bodies, their motions are constrained by physical barriers (Gergely & Csibra, 2003; Saxe et al., 2006; Spelke et al., 1992). Participants (N=28) were scanned using functional magnetic resonance imaging while they watched videos of agents navigating or objects moving in environments containing physical barriers, and videos containing similar physical features that did not block agents’ or objects’ paths. We report a domain-invariant code for barriers to agents’ actions and objects’ motions in regions associated with physical reasoning and domain-general processing (superior parietal lobule), object processing (lateral occipital complex), and scene processing (occipital place area, parahippocampal place area, and retrosplenial cortex). Our results generalized across two distinct tasks, and are not explained by differences in luminance, motion energy, or the number of objects in the stimuli. These results suggest that brain regions specialized for reasoning about physical dynamics and spatial layouts support our capacity to make sense of agents’ actions, raising questions about interactions more broadly between social, physical and spatial cognition.

articles.title: Open-Source Embedding Strategies for Recovering Spanish Scale Structure
articles.authors: Lindsey W. Vilca; Javier Suárez-Álvarez
articles.abstract: Text embeddings from large language models are increasingly able to reproduce how items cluster in real response data, offering a fast, low-cost way to screen items and spot structural problems early. But most evidence comes from English tests and closed commercial models, so it remains unclear whether these benefits extend fairly to other languages. The goal of this study is to test whether open-source multilingual text-embedding models can recover the empirically observed structure of Spanish-language questionnaires across instruments with different internal structure, and to provide guidance for responsible use of these models beyond English-language testing. We analyzed responses from 7,379 Peruvian adolescents on a one-factor scale (11-item Critical Thinking Disposition Scale) and a five-factor scale (20-item EQ-i:M20). For each test, we compared item-to-item semantic similarity from text embeddings with empirically estimated item networks from exploratory graph analysis using multiple metrics (correlations, top‑k accuracy, clustering, and the adjusted Rand index). All models showed positive alignment between semantic and empirical item relations, with multilingual‑e5‑large performing most consistently. Instruction-tuned prompting nearly recovered the multidimensional EQ‑i:M20 structure (adjusted Rand index = .91) but offered little benefit for the unidimensional CTDS. Local and global metrics sometimes diverged: one model captured nearest neighbors yet missed the overall dimensional structure, supporting multi-metric evaluation. Reverse-worded items were systematic semantic outliers across models. Overall, models with stronger Spanish training signals were more reliable than English-focused alternatives, and instruction-style prompting was most beneficial for multidimensional instruments. The results also suggest that semantic–psychometric evaluations should explicitly account for reverse-worded items and should rely on multiple complementary metrics rather than a single summary score. In conclusion, these findings speak to validity and fairness concerns by exposing training-data limitations, providing guidance for responsible AI use beyond English-language testing, and supporting a more transparent and reproducible AI agenda.

articles.title: From homework questions to emotional confessions: the psychological implications of children confiding in AI, "relational drift", and developmental vulnerability
articles.authors: Nomisha Chandran Kurian
articles.abstract: Rising evidence shows children and adolescents using AI to confide personal worries and seek advice. However, while empirical data on this phenomenon is growing, there has not yet been sustained conceptual work on the psychological and ethical implications of children - as developing beings still forming their identities and views of the world - confiding in artificial systems. This article aims to help address this gap. It unpacks why the process of confiding matters for children developmentally. It introduces the concept of “relational drift” to show how children may begin chatting with AI in strictly task-focused ways (for example, homework help), yet the emotionally responsive nature of contemporary LLMs can result in some child-users, particularly those who are vulnerable or socially isolated, seeking more personal and emotional support. The article examines the psycho-ethical implications of these liminal, ambiguous spaces of child-AI interaction. In doing so, the article also helps address the lack of child-centred, developmentally informed theorisations in the field of AI ethics. In a time when child-AI interactions are becoming more emotionally complex, child-safe AI design, policy and governance must move beyond narrow discussions of content moderation and take psychological and relational dynamics seriously.

articles.title: Are There Really Pervasive COIs in the ABA Literature?: A Response to Bottema-Beutel et al. (2026)
articles.authors: Matthew Thornton Brodhead; Shawn P. Quigley; Abraham Graber
articles.abstract: Bottema-Beutel et al. (2026) claim that, in the applied behavior analysis (ABA) intervention research with autistic participants they considered, “93% of author statements regarding no COIs [conflicts of interest] were false” (p. 1). However, the understanding of conflicts of interest endorsed by Bottema-Beutel et al. (2026) does not align with how conflicts of interest are understood by recognized authorities on the topic. In this letter, we propose it is unclear if Bottema-Beutel et al.’s (2026) conclusions remain intact when conflicts of interest are understood in line with the recommendations of the Committee on Publication Ethics (COPE) and the International Committee of Medical Journal Editors (ICMJE). We also raise concerns regarding the methods of Bottema-Beutel et al. (2026). We conclude that collaboration with subject matter experts (e.g., bioethicists and research compliance officers) is recommended. While adherence to best practices in research ethics is non-negotiable, both research ethics and related regulations can be byzantine. As such, autism researchers may want to consider engaging in ongoing dialogue with their colleagues with expertise on these topics.

articles.title: Developmental changes in children’s production of line drawings in a picture-sparse environment: Evidence from Kisumu, Kenya
articles.authors: Rebecca Zhu; Joab Ochieng Arieda; Tabitha Nduku; Zachary Watson; Tarun Sepuri; Bria Long; Judith Fan; Michael C. Frank
articles.abstract: Children growing up in environments with many drawing opportunities improve dramatically at producing recognizable drawings over the course of childhood. Does this same developmental trajectory hold for children growing up in environments with fewer drawing opportunities? We measured children’s drawing recognizability cross-sectionally in a drawing-sparse context, specifically Kisumu, Kenya. By leveraging within-context variation in children’s drawing experiences, we investigated whether, at an individual level, drawing recognizability increases with children’s drawing experiences. A preregistered sample of 120 4- to 9-year-olds drew 12 object categories in random order. Children produced more recognizable drawings with age, even after controlling for tracing ability. Moreover, younger children with more drawing experience produced more recognizable drawings, even after controlling for tracing ability and home environment variables. These results suggest commonalities in drawing development across cultures and document the experience-dependence of drawing development in drawing-sparse contexts.

articles.title: The Perception-Expression Cycle Framework: A Dynamic Model of Peer Processes and Achievement Goals
articles.authors: Alla Hemi
articles.abstract: Peer relationships fundamentally shape the development of achievement goals during adolescence, yet research has largely overlooked an important distinction: the difference between (a) the achievement goals peers actually pursue and (b) the goals students perceive their peers to pursue. The proposed Perception–Expression Cycle Model clarifies how students’ subjective perceptions of peer goals, their own goal adjustments, and behavioral expressions of goals create self–reinforcing cycles through which peer motivation is constructed and sustained at the classroom level. The model comprises three interconnected phases. In the Perception phase, students observe peer behaviors and infer underlying goals, a process shaped by classroom norms, peer reputations, and assumed similarity biases. In the Adjustment phase, students reorganize their goal hierarchies in response to perceived (rather than actual) peer goals through informational, normative, and identification–based influence processes. In the Expression phase, students communicate adjusted goals through observable behaviors that peers interpret as evidence confirming their initial perceptions, potentially creating self–fulfilling prophecies. Individual differences, contextual factors, and cultural contexts moderate the links and processes within the cycle. This framework reconceptualizes motivation as socially constructed and classroom motivational cultures as emergent from peer perception–expression dynamics, rather than the sum of individual student dispositions.

articles.title: The Body Appreciation Scale-2 for Autistic Adults (BAS-2A): Psychometric Properties in Autistic Gender Diverse and Transgender Adults from Four English-Speaking, Western Countries
articles.authors: Phaedra Longhurst; Luka White; Viren Swami
articles.abstract: The Body Appreciation Scale-2A (BAS-2A) is a measure for capturing a core facet of positive body image in the adult Autistic population. Preliminary evidence suggests body appreciation may foster physical and psychological well-being among Autistic people who are also transgender or gender diverse (TGD); however, to date, the scale has not been validated within this group. To address this, we evaluated the factor structure and psychometric properties of the BAS-2A in Autistic TGD people. A total of 191 Autistic TGD people (aged 18 to 85 years) from Western countries (i.e., Australia, Canada, the United Kingdom, and the United States) completed the BAS-2A along with additional measures of physical well-being and eating disorder symptomatology. Confirmatory factor analysis supported a 12-item, unidimensional structure of the BAS-2A in the total sample. The BAS-2A also demonstrated excellent composite reliability. Evidence of convergent, concurrent, discriminant, and incremental validity was strong when using both observed and latent scores. Body appreciation was significantly associated with higher self-compassion, self-esteem, and quality of life, and lower eating disorder symptomatology. Overall, our results suggest that the BAS-2A is a valid and reliable measure for capturing body appreciation among autistic TGD people. The present study offers a valuable contribution toward a better understanding of positive body image in the wider autistic population.

articles.title: POWERED to Recover: Preliminary Evaluation of a Semi-structured Interview to Support Individualized Expected Body Weight Determination in Eating Disorder Treatment
articles.authors: Agatha Laboe; Mahathi Gavuji; Lilian Palmer; Ava Heyrman; Anna Kreynin; Katherine Schaumberg
articles.abstract:
Objective: Determination of expected body weights (EBWs) is common in eating disorder (ED) treatment, yet no reproducible, individualized method currently exists to guide this process. The Personalized Optimization of Weight Recovery in Eating Disorders (POWERED) interview was developed to address this gap by providing a standardized method for capturing medical, psychiatric, and ED behavior history for use alongside automated growth chart-based tools that support individualized EBW determination. This study evaluated the preliminary feasibility and acceptability of the POWERED interview.
Method: Seventeen youth and young adults ages 14-20 years with restrictive-spectrum EDs completed the POWERED interview and provided quantitative and qualitative feedback on its feasibility and acceptability, as well as their preferences regarding EBW disclosure. 
Results: Participants reported high confidence in their ability to provide accurate responses across interview domains, supporting feasibility. However, participants agreed that the interview was somewhat tiring and difficult. Most participants preferred being informed of their own EBWs (76.5%) and having their treatment providers informed (82.4%), yet only 47.1% wanted parents or guardians informed. Higher EBWs elicited greater distress and fear regardless of how the EBW was determined.
Discussion: Findings support the preliminary feasibility and acceptability of the POWERED interview while identifying clear targets for refinement. Results highlight the importance of collaborative decisions about parental involvement in EBW discussions. The association between higher EBWs and elevated distress underscores the entrenchment of fear of weight gain and suggests EBW discussions may function as therapeutic exposures in ED treatment.

articles.title: Beyond the Average: Hierarchical Bayesian Models Recover Attenuated Effect Sizes and Reliability in ERP Research
articles.authors: Jan; Christoph Löffler; Anna-Lena Schubert
articles.abstract: Event-related potential (ERP) measurement faces a psychometric problem: subject-level estimates differ substantially in precision, yet trial averaging treats them as equally precise. The standardized measurement error (SME) framework has made this heterogeneity diagnosable but does not change how individual estimates are used downstream. Hierarchical Bayesian models (HBMs) integrate per-subject noise directly into estimation through partial pooling, a solution well-established in behavioral research but rarely applied to ERPs. Using the publicly available ERP CORE dataset (N = 40), we compared HBM to trial averaging across five canonical components (MMN, N400, P3, N170, ERN). Signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) ranged from γ = 0.07 to 0.51, overlapping with values reported for behavioral tasks. Hierarchical modeling recovered attenuated standardized effect sizes (Cohen’s d_z) by 5 % to 99 % and split-half reliability across components (e.g., P3 from r = .28 to .81; N170 from .75 to .85), with the largest recoveries for low-SNR paradigms. For the P3 this raised the maximum achievable brain-behavior correlation from .53 to .90, illustrating the practical stakes for individual-difference research. Effect size recovery persisted after excluding noisy subjects, while reliability recovery diminished, indicating distinct mechanisms underlying the two improvements. We provide an accessible brms implementation and tutorial notebook (https://osf.io/6rewm/).
