HBMs subsume the diagnostic logic of SME and the corrective logic of manual outlier exclusion into a single parameterized estimation step, simultaneously reducing researcher degrees of freedom.

articles.title: Can mindfulness in virtual reality modify food cravings?
articles.authors: Sophie Hascher; Anoushka Parylo; Mike Kendig; Poppy Watson; Ori Ossmy; Halley M. Pontes
articles.abstract: Mindfulness-based treatments have demonstrated efficacy in reducing addictive behaviours, including food addiction. Yet, accessibility to in-person programmes remains a barrier, with limited capacity issues and accessibility for individuals suffering from mobility impairment. Virtual reality (VR) offers a promising platform to deliver mindfulness interventions in immersive nature-like environments. This study investigated the immediate effects of a single 10-minute mindfulness intervention on food cravings in adults (N = 56) from the United Kingdom and Australia, comparing a VR group with a control group viewing the same nature-like scene on a computer screen. Cravings were assessed using a food rating picture task, and the Food Cravings Questionnaire-State, alongside state mindfulness measures. A series of mixed ANOVAs indicated that the mindfulness intervention significantly reduced food cravings and increased mindfulness scores across both groups, with no significant differences observed between VR and control conditions. Results suggest that the mindfulness practice and nature-based context, rather than the immersive VR technology itself, drove effects. Additional sub-analyses comparing food images showed significant reductions in cravings over time for both healthy and unhealthy foods. Self-reported immersion scores did not moderate changes in food cravings, regardless of VR condition. Finally, self-reported sugar addiction scores were positively associated with sweet food cravings at baseline, but not with changes in craving following the mindfulness intervention. Taken together, these findings highlight that a brief mindfulness intervention can modulate craving states, potentially via enhanced interoceptive awareness and emotion regulation.

articles.title: Development and Behavioral Validation of the Dark Creativity Deception Battery (DCDB)
articles.authors: Hansika Kapoor; Sarah Rezaei; Apoorva Thakur; Tithi Roy; Corinna Perchtold-Stefan; James C. Kaufman
articles.abstract: Although creativity is commonly associated with positive outcomes, emerging research has highlighted its darker side, particularly in facilitating deceptive and unethical behavior. The present study introduces the Dark Creativity Deception Battery (DCDB), a novel task-based assessment designed to measure creative deception across prosocial, self-serving, and antisocial contexts. Participants (N = 755) were recruited from India, USA, and Austria. They completed several self-report measures and a series of deception tasks in which they generated novel and deceptive ways to address a series of vignettes. Responses were assessed across seven parameters: creativity, moral valence, goal-directedness, deception, virtue caveat, fluency, and flexibility.  Results indicated that younger individuals, lower moral disengagement, and higher negative affect were associated with creative responses in prosocial contexts. In contrast, higher trait deceptiveness and lower income were associated with self-serving and antisocial lies. Moral valence varied across tasks, with prosocial deception rated most positively. Interestingly, high psychopathy was associated with moral justifiability in self-serving deception. Deception itself was highest in antisocial and self-serving contexts, coupled with high individualizing morality, suggesting moral licensing effects. Our findings highlight the interplay between morality, personality, and creativity in deceptive behavior. The DCDB offers a novel way to study creative deception beyond traditional measures. Limitations and future scope are discussed.

articles.title: Altered States, Enhanced Potential: Psychedelics and Physical Performance
articles.authors: Zane Qarni; Jeremie Richard
articles.abstract: Interest in psychedelics has expanded beyond clinical treatment contexts, yet little empirical work has examined how users describe psychedelic use in relation to sport and physical activity. This study conducted a qualitative content analysis of Reddit discussions involving psychedelics and physical performance. A Reddit search for “sports psychedelics” returned 253 threads. After screening, 39 threads and 290 comments met inclusion criteria. Analysis identified eight content categories: physical performance enhancement; flow state/automaticity; perceptual and cognitive enhancement; risks, limitations, and safety concerns; reduced fatigue/pain perception; microdosing as strategy; psychological and emotional benefits; and fairness, ethics, and governance. Psilocybin mushrooms and LSD were the most frequently mentioned substances. Across accounts, users most often described psychedelics as enhancing performance indirectly through altered attention, increased mind-body connection, flow-like absorption, and reduced pain or fatigue. Some users reported greater perceived strength, speed, endurance, coordination, or overall capability during activity, while others described effortless movement, reduced self-consciousness, sharper perception, and improved focus. Reports were predominantly positive or mixed, with concerns about overexertion, injury risk, impaired judgment, and fairness in competitive settings. These findings suggest that psychedelics may be understood less as traditional performance-enhancing drugs and more as potential-enhancing substances that alter the subjective conditions under which performance occurs. However, because all accounts were self-reported, it remains unclear whether perceived performance gains correspond to measurable physiological or behavioral improvement. Future controlled research using objective performance outcomes is needed to clarify the relationship between psychedelic use, subjective experience, and physical performance.

articles.title: Collective Effervescence as a Mediator between Emotion and Enjoyment in Live Dance
articles.authors: Ondine Simonot-Bérenger; Haeeun Lee; Laura Rai; Matthias Sperling; Julie Grezes; Guido Orgs
articles.abstract: Live performance provides a paradigmatic setting for collective aesthetic experiences, where the social dynamics shape how audiences perceive and evaluate performances. Building on prior research demonstrating that collective effervescence mediates the relationship between spectators' affective responses and enjoyment in live theatre, the present paper investigates the role of collective effervescence in two contemporary dance choreographies. In Study 1 (Detective Work, N=121), we examined both live (N=65) and screened (N=56) performances, distinguishing between connectedness to audience members and connectedness to performers. Unlike findings in live theatre, valence rather than arousal predicted enjoyment indirectly through connectedness, with performers-connectedness emerging as the stronger mediator in both live and screened modalities. Study 2 (Readings of what was never written, N=73) employed a reduced collective effervescence scale including both connectedness and sacredness facets; sacredness emerged as the dominant mediator of enjoyment, despite strong covariance between facets. Across both studies, valence, not arousal, consistently drove the affect-to-enjoyment pathway, suggesting dance-specific collective affective signatures that differ from narrative theatre. These findings extend theoretical models of aesthetic appreciation, indicating that while the core mediation pathway might generalise across performing arts, the most salient facet of collective effervescence and the dominant social tie can vary with artistic form, scale, and dramaturgical design.

articles.title: Validation of a German Version of the Barcelona Music  Reward Questionnaire (BMRQ)
articles.authors: Kim Meyer; Charlotte Jolmes; Madita Jodes; Willi Moxter; Lena Rölz; Merle Roth; Lars Rogenmoser
articles.abstract:
The Barcelona Music Reward Questionnaire (BMRQ) is a widely used self-report measure of 
individual differences in music reward sensitivity. Although the instrument has been adapted 
into several languages, no validated German version has previously been available. The 
present study translated and psychometrically evaluated a German adaptation of the BMRQ 
in a sample of 471 German-speaking adults. 
A split-sample approach was used to conduct confirmatory and exploratory factor analyses. 
Confirmatory factor analysis supported the original five-factor structure comprising Musical 
Seeking, Emotional Evocation, Mood Regulation, Sensory-Motor, and Social Reward. 
Exploratory analyses broadly reproduced this structure, although overlap emerged between 
Musical Seeking and Social Reward. Internal consistency estimates for the BMRQ dimension 
scores and overall score ranged from acceptable to good. Associations with reward-related 
motivational tendencies provided evidence for convergent validity. 
Scores were distributed across a broad range of the scale and showed moderate negative 
skewness, reflecting a tendency toward higher levels of music reward. 
Overall, the present findings support the German BMRQ as a psychometrically sound 
instrument for assessing individual differences in music reward sensitivity in German
speaking populations.

articles.title: From Thought to Senses: Assessing the Relationship Between the Generation Effect and Multisensory Facilitation
articles.authors: Michaela Ritchie; Jonathan Wilbiks
articles.abstract: The current study investigated whether the generation effect, a memory advantage for self-generated verbal information, is enhanced in multisensory conditions. Such a finding would be consistent with the multisensory facilitation effect, a phenomenon wherein multiple sensory inputs may reduce the cognitive load required to process and respond to co-occurring stimuli from multiple senses. Although extensive research has explored the generation effect, the multisensory aspect of this task has been overlooked. In the current study, we aimed to determine if the generation effect is modulated by the multisensory facilitation effect. Multisensory facilitation involves the brain’s ability to efficiently process information from diverse sensory sources simultaneously, enhancing cognitive performance. We employed a 2 (Task Type: generate, read) X 3 (Sensory Modality: auditory, visual, multisensory) factorial design, utilizing word pair lists and a cued recall test. A generation effect emerged for the auditory and audiovisual condition, but not for the visual condition. The hypothesis that the magnitude of the generation effect would be greater for the multisensory encoding condition was not supported. Overall, the hypothesis that generation effect will be enhanced for multisensory learning was not supported. The current study addresses a gap in existing literature by examining the multisensory component of generation tasks and extends the current understanding of multisensory learning by incorporating verbal stimuli as the visual component.

articles.title: (Tracked) From Thought to Senses: Assessing the Relationship Between the Generation Effect and Multisensory Facilitation
articles.authors: Michaela Ritchie; Jonathan Wilbiks
articles.abstract: The current study investigated whether the generation effect, a memory advantage for self-generated verbal information, is enhanced in multisensory conditions. Such a finding would be consistent with the multisensory facilitation effect, a phenomenon wherein multiple sensory inputs may reduce the cognitive load required to process and respond to co-occurring stimuli from multiple senses. Although extensive research has explored the generation effect, the multisensory aspect of this task has been overlooked. In the current study, we aimed to determine if the generation effect is modulated by the multisensory facilitation effect. Multisensory facilitation involves the brain’s ability to efficiently process information from diverse sensory sources simultaneously, enhancing cognitive performance. We employed a 2 (Task Type: generate, read) X 3 (Sensory Modality: auditory, visual, multisensory) factorial design, utilizing word pair lists and a cued recall test. A generation effect emerged for the auditory and audiovisual condition, but not for the visual condition. The hypothesis that the magnitude of the generation effect would be greater for the multisensory encoding condition was not supported. Overall, the hypothesis that generation effect will be enhanced for multisensory learning was not supported. The current study addresses a gap in existing literature by examining the multisensory component of generation tasks and extends the current understanding of multisensory learning by incorporating verbal stimuli as the visual component.

articles.title: Active and Passive Social Media Use, Momentary Thought Patterns, and Emotion in  Young Adults
articles.authors: Xiangyu Deng; Thuy-vy Thi Nguyen
articles.abstract: Using ecological momentary assessment, this study examined how active and passive social media use were associated with momentary thought patterns, affect, and examined the role of individual differences in 146 young adults aged 18–23 years. Participants completed a baseline survey and up to four daily prompts over 14 days, yielding 4,325 reports, including 1,171 reports of recent social media use. Exploratory factor analysis of Multidimensional Experience Sampling (MDES)-based thought items identified three cognitive dimensions during social media use: Episodic, social, memory-based thought; Intentional, detailed, task-focused thought; and Intrusive or negative thought. Intrusive or negative thought showed the strongest associations with distress, including higher high-arousal negative affect, boredom, sadness, fatigue, and lower positive affect. Compared with passive use, active use was associated with higher Episodic, social, memory-based thought, higher Intentional, detailed, task-focused thought, slightly higher high-arousal positive affect, and lower boredom, but did not differ in Intrusive or negative thought or sadness. Moderation analyses suggested that social reward sensitivity amplified active-use differences in engaged thought, whereas active use was linked to lower Intrusive or negative thought only among young adults lower in social anxiety, envy, social comparison, self-expression, and rumination. Social anxiety and envy also strengthened the association between active use and sadness. These findings suggest that the active–passive distinction is useful but insufficient: momentary thought patterns, distinct emotional states, and individual differences help clarify young adults’ momentary social media experiences.

articles.title: Origin and Control of Persistent Mental Content
articles.authors: Gabriel Kressin Palacios; Tianshu Ouyang; Buddhika Bellana; Christopher John Honey
articles.abstract:
Some thoughts and experiences persist in our minds on the scale of minutes. For example, topics discussed earlier in a conversation remain fresh in mind and may persist in our thinking after the conversation is over. What cognitive mechanisms underlie this persistent mental content and how can it be controlled? To answer these questions, we induced persistent mental content using immersive narrative stimuli and measured the rate and influence of persistent thoughts.
First, we found that persistent mental content was spontaneous and not merely a consequence of intentional memory retrieval. Second, we found that persistent content was robust  to interference from multiple types of similar and dissimilar distractor tasks. Finally, we found that when new information was construed as related to the preceding experience, the persistence of the preceding experience was prolonged. These data indicate that memory models need to be augmented with a slowly changing "deep context" representation which is robust to stimulus-driven interference and which biases both retrieval and the generation of the next thought.

articles.title: Developmental variation from rational choice
articles.authors: Samuel David Jones; Dara Tan; Paul Rauwolf
articles.abstract: Rational choice theory offers a normative account of the allocation of bounded cognitive resources under constraints. This work has focused largely on static solutions, leaving the developmental implications of rational choice relatively under-researched. Conversely, developmental science has in general overlooked the role of an individual's choices, actions, and habits in shaping rather than merely reflecting developmental variation, from delay to psychopathology to expertise. The aim of this paper is to characterise the developmental implications of rational inattention, a leading framework within rational choice theory, and in doing so develop a normative account of how adaptive behaviour reciprocally shapes the emergence of diversity in intelligent behaviour. The thesis we develop is that rational choice – behaviour that is adaptive not only to the structure of the environment but to the constraints of the agent – shapes neurodevelopmental variation beyond the neurogenetic factors that promote that behaviour. We begin by presenting static solutions to rationally inattentive choice problems, before turning to adaptivity and learning over time. We demonstrate that adaptive behaviour contributes unique variance to developmental outcomes and predicts inertia in changing environments. Finally, we distil the key features of rationally inattentive behaviour and corresponding empirical support from the developmental science literature. The strength of this approach is to demonstrate that some of the signatures of diversity in intelligent behaviour across the lifespan can in principle fall out of a minimal set of rational assumptions. This has implications for how we think about and respond to individual differences in human development.

articles.title: Technology-Natives and Technology-Adopters: Age Differences in Remote Cognitive Assessment in Alzheimer’s Disease
articles.authors: Samhita Govindan Katteri; Hannah Wilks; Matt Welhaf; Andrew J. Aschenbrenner; Colleen C Frank; Clara Vila-Castelar; Sarah H. Stout; Martin Sliwinski; Tammie Benzinger; Florencia Clarens
articles.abstract:
INTRODUCTION: Cognitive changes in the asymptomatic stages of Alzheimer’s disease (AD) are subtle and difficult to detect using conventional cognitive tests. High-frequency smartphone-based testing may improve sensitivity to these changes, but generational differences in technology use could influence performance. We examined generational differences in cognition using remote, high-frequency smartphone assessments.
METHODS: Older adults (N = 489) from the Knight Alzheimer Disease Research Center and young and middle-aged adults (N = 130) from the Dominantly Inherited Alzheimer Network (DIAN) completed an in-clinic cognitive battery and brief smartphone tests up to four times per daily for seven days. We compared adherence, reliability, practice effects, and correlations with in-clinic measures. 
RESULTS: Older adults showed higher adherence than younger participants (79% vs 57%). Younger adults achieved higher reliability with fewer sessions and exhibited much smaller practice effects. Smartphone to in-clinic test correlations were stronger in younger adults.
DISCUSSION: Generational differences have important implications for the design and implementation of smartphone-based cognitive studies.

articles.title: The Alienated Encounter: Calm but Disconnected Subjects and the Case for Prosthetic Capability
articles.authors: Vince Jacques de Blauwe
articles.abstract: Contemporary mental-health and wellbeing discourse increasingly converges on the calm, self-managing subject as an unquestioned ideal. Yet practitioners and participants report a recurring pattern: people who become less anxious and more composed while remaining disconnected from others. This article names that pattern the Masking Paradox and argues that it cannot be resolved within frameworks that locate the problem inside the individual. Drawing on critical psychology, capability theory, and recognition theory, the article develops two linked conceptual moves. First, it reframes non-transactional connection as a contested social capability rather than an internal disposition or skill. Second, it introduces the concept of prosthetic capability: institutional and relational scaffolding that makes the capability achievable for subjects whose formation under capitalist conditions has rendered it precarious. Nonviolent Communication is examined as a worked example, including Rosenberg's in-tradition critique of "jackal" appropriation, to show how a practice tradition can function as prosthetic infrastructure when its institutional conditions are met, and how it fails when they are not. The article closes by arguing that critical psychology must take relation itself, rather than only the internal state of the relating subject, as a primary object of theory, intervention, and institutional design.

articles.title: A Network Analysis of Disordered Eating and Emotion Regulation Among Individuals with a High Body Mass Index
articles.authors: Chantal P Delaquis; Dean Spirou; Yasmin Brown; Katie Wood; Evelyn Smith; Jayanthi Raman; Sylvie Berthoz
articles.abstract:
Overweight and obesity are a growing public health concern with few effective long-term treatments. While excess weight has been associated with disordered eating behaviours (DEB) and emotional difficulties, their interplay has not been thoroughly explored. This study examines the network structure of emotion regulation (ER), anxious and depressive symptomatology and DEB among individuals with a high body mass index (BMI).
The sample consisted of 126 individuals aged 40.29 (SD = 8.91) years with a mean BMI of 36.95 (SD = 8.08). We estimated a regularized partial correlation network and examined centrality and bridge symptoms. Bootstrapping was used to assess network stability with the correlation stability (CS) coefficient. 
The three symptoms with the highest Expected Influence (EI) were Lack of ER Strategies (EI = 1.22), Eating Concerns (EI = 1.05) and Impulse/NonGoals (EI = 0.95). The most central bridge node was Depression (BEI = 0.42). The network had excellent stability for EI and BEI (CS = 0.6 and 0.52 respectively).
Our results point to the centrality of lack of adaptive ER strategies, in addition to eating concerns  and impulsivity/difficulty with goal directed behaviour among individuals with a high BMI. Depressive symptoms bridged ER difficulties, BMI and eating pathology, highlighting the potential relevance of depressive symptoms as an illness pathway between these symptom clusters.

articles.title: AI and Pediatric Mental Health: Joint AI Engagement for fostering Safe and Supportive AI Use
articles.authors: Emre Sezgin; William Leever; Ian mckay
articles.abstract: Generative artificial intelligence is rapidly entering the developmental ecologies of children and adolescents through conversational assistants, tutoring systems, companion chatbots, creative tools, and mental health or wellness applications. Emerging evidence indicates substantial youth exposure, while evidence regarding developmental, educational, and clinical outcomes remains limited. Existing responses often oscillate between prohibition and technological optimism. We propose a developmentally grounded middle position: Joint AI Engagement, adapted from the Joint Media Engagement framework. Unlike earlier digital media, generative AI is interactive, personalized, and capable of simulating understanding, authority, and care. For children and adolescents, its meaning therefore depends not only on content, but on context, interpretation, relational use, and adult scaffolding. We map Joint AI Engagement across three settings central to child and adolescent mental health: the home, the school, and the clinic. At home, it supports digital literacy, privacy awareness, and relational availability. In schools, it preserves authorship, productive struggle, peer collaboration, and critical thinking. In clinical settings, it becomes part of assessment, psychoeducation, safety planning, and family guidance. We conclude that AI may augment learning, access, and support, but for young people its developmental value and risk depend on whether trusted adults remain actively present as guides.

articles.title: Italian Adaptation and Psychometric Validation of the Cardiac Distress Inventory in Patients With Recent Cardiac Events
articles.authors: Graziano Gigante; Edward Callus; Sara Gostoli; Enrico Giuseppe Bertoldo; Valentina Fiolo; Giulia Lorefice; Sofia De Stefano; GIULIA CASU; Marco Monti; Stefano Urbinati
articles.abstract:
Background: Acute cardiac events are accompanied by multifaceted psychological responses that affect individuals' coping capacity. The construct of cardiac distress captures these responses. Although the Cardiac Distress Inventory (CDI) assesses this construct, no Italian version is available. This study aimed to translate the CDI and assess its psychometric properties.
Methods: After translation and back-translation, face validity was evaluated with five patients and content validity with healthcare professionals. The CDI was administered to 293 cardiac patients who experienced an event within 12 months, recruited from two Italian centers. Missing data were handled using multiple imputation. Exploratory factor analysis (EFA; principal axis factoring and oblimin rotation) assessed factorial structure, followed by reliability analysis. Criterion validity was evaluated through correlations with Emotional Thermometers and Kessler-6 (K6). ROC analysis assessed discriminative accuracy and identified the optimal cut-off score using K6 as criterion.
Results: The questionnaire demonstrated good face validity and suboptimal content validity (CVI-S/Ave = .72). EFA revealed a five-factor structure explaining 37.05% of the variance, comprising "Role Change and Adaptation to Disease", "Social Isolation", "Cognitive Challenges", "Emotional Overwhelm", and "Doctor-Patient Communication". The final 42-item version (11 items removed for insufficient loadings, 2 for reliability issues) showed good internal consistency (ω = .746-.879) and excellent discriminative accuracy (AUC = .833) with optimal cut-off of 26 (sensitivity = 81%, specificity = 74%).
Conclusions: The Italian CDI represents a psychometrically sound instrument addressing a significant gap in cardiovascular psychology assessment. Its five-factor structure provides a comprehensive framework for evaluating cardiac distress and informing targeted clinical interventions.

articles.title: Preprint "Extraversion, positive affect, and social attention in small human groups"
articles.authors: Sabine Ostermann; Julia Stern; Tobias L. Kordsmeyer; Lars Penke
articles.abstract: Why extraverted individuals tend to be happier in the context of social interactions is a longstanding question. This study investigates the role of receiving social attention as an interpersonal behaviour that explains qualitatively different social experiences. For this purpose, we used data from a large observational laboratory group study (N = 449) and coded gazing behaviour of participants during a group discussion. We replicated the finding that more extraverted individuals report higher positive affect after social interactions compared to less introverted individuals. Social attention partially accounted for this association, independent of speech ratio and physical attractiveness. However, there was no evidence that more extraverted individuals respond to social attention with greater positive affect than less introverted individuals. Additionally, the social attention an individual received was differentially associated with social status but not liking. Social recognition through social attention might explain why extraverted individuals are happier after social interaction.

articles.title: Training the power: Physical size guides the processing of exponential expressions
articles.authors: Bar Salman; Ami Feder; Serena Mingolo; Nadav Neumann; Mariya Lozin; Michal Pinhas
articles.abstract: This study examined how training and the physical size of base and power components affect the processing of exponential expressions. Research has shown that exponential expressions are cognitively challenging and that physical attributes, such as component size, can influence their processing. In the present study, we aimed to improve the comprehension of exponential expressions by training participants (N = 32) to learn the mathematical roles of bases and powers across standard and non-standard displays, where the latter featured a physically larger power than the base. Over five training sessions, participants practiced matching exponential expressions with their multiplication decompositions. In a subsequent test phase, we assessed both automatic processing (via a physical comparison task) and intentional processing (via a numerical comparison task) of the trained exponential expressions, as well as untrained expressions. Training improved response speed for familiar expressions, although automatic processing was limited; participants continued to focus on the physically larger component rather than the mathematically more important one. In the numerical comparison task, training improved the understanding of the power’s role for familiar expressions, but this understanding did not generalize to new expressions. Additionally, performance on the exponent knowledge test improved significantly following training, with reduced underestimation errors. The findings suggest that while training can enhance the intentional processing of exponential expressions, automatic processing of exponential syntax remains limited, highlighting the need for refined instructional approaches to exponential expressions.

articles.title: Exploring How to Improve Experimenter Training in Experimental Psychopathology: Lessons from and for the Trauma Film Paradigm
articles.authors: Tommy Mcconnell; Elisabeth Leehr; Matt Garner; Deborah Talmi; Emily A Holmes
articles.abstract: Experimental psychopathology (EPP) historically relied on extensive experimenter training, but this has dwindled in recent years. Insufficient training may lead to increased ‘experimenter noise’ and ‘protocol drift’, potentially hindering the fidelity and replicability of EPP research. This may ultimately impact translation of findings from the laboratory to the clinic, decelerating the development of novel psychological interventions. In an effort to address this, we made a first attempt to develop experimenter training guidance for EPP researchers, using the example of the trauma film paradigm (TFP). In a pragmatic review of articles on methodological guidance for laboratory-based EPP paradigms, we identified only seven articles, just one of which provided an explicit framework for experimenter training. Incorporating insights from the reviewed articles, together with approaches employed in adjacent clinical trials, we suggest a possible framework for experimenter training in EPP. The framework includes overarching principles of experimenter training, steps in a training process, an approach to evaluating training outcomes, and offers examples from the TFP. Further work is needed, and future directions include incentivizing, improving, disseminating and experimentally testing guidance for experimenter training in EPP. Critically, as we have seen in previous decades, this may even ultimately aid the turbulent translational pipeline of new techniques from the laboratory to evidence-based psychological therapies grounded in scientific principles.

articles.title: EVALUATING THE BIOLOGICAL RELEVANCE OF THE RELATIVISTIC THEORY OF CONSCIOUSNESS
articles.authors: Wolfgang Kromer
articles.abstract: According to the Relativistic Theory of Consciousness by Lahav and Neemeh, consciousness is not an absolute feature but a relativistic one that depends on the reference system of the observer. The theory offers a new, thought-provoking view on consciousness, but also raises critical issues. First: What is the fundamental precondition of consciousness which the Relativistic Theory may be based upon? Although this aspect does not question the relativistic principle as such, it still remains open. Therefore, a Comparator Model of consciousness will be discussed in relation to the Relativistic Theory of Consciousness. Second: What is the Relativistic Theory’s biological relevance? This solely depends on the theory’s real-world applicability because this is it what the authors fundamentally envisage by their statement that we just need to move to the appropriate frame of reference in order to measure phenomenal properties. Although the mathematical formulation of changing from a third-person to a first-person frame of reference is possible when based on suitable premises, and may well be internally consistent, this does not per se prove any corresponding biological reality. Thus, the technological requirements for such a transition are a most critical issue. The obstacles in real-life will be addressed.

articles.title: Predicting individual differences in LITMUS Sentence Repetition performance using the Q-BEx questionnaire
articles.authors: Philippe Prevost; Cecile De Cat; Arief Gusnanto; Draško Kašćelan; Ludovica Serratrice; Laurie Tuller; Sharon Unsworth
articles.abstract: We present the first comprehensive investigation of language experience and cognitive predictors of LITMUS (Language Impairment Testing in Multilingual Settings) sentence repetition (SR) performance in 136 typically developing bilingual children aged 5 to 9 years across three societal languages (French, Dutch, English). Language experience predictors, informed by the Q-BEx questionnaire, included quantity of exposure (age of onset, cumulative and current measures, language entropy) and language use (dominance), quality of exposure (activities in the language, diversity and proficiency of interlocutors), language attitudes (child’s willingness to speak the language), and risk of language impairment (early milestones and parental concerns). Cognitive predictors included short-term memory, working memory, and non-verbal intelligence. Results were analyzed using predictive regression models and partition models. Different scoring methods were found to reflect different aspects of performance, as they were sensitive to different combinations of predictors. Predictive models identified age of onset, memory (mainly short-term but also working memory), and quality of language exposure as the most encompassing predictors. Partition analyses provided a more nuanced picture based on interactions between predictors, suggesting different profiles among typically developing bilingual children. These profiles can inform identification of language impairment in bilingual children, which LITMUS-SR was designed to detect.

articles.title: Can Aerobic Exercise Really Improve Decision-Making in Aging? What Has Been Measured, What Has Been Assumed, and What Needs to Change.
articles.authors: Jonathan Alexis Cortés Silva; Christina A. Davis; Diego Lievano Parra; Valeria V. González
articles.abstract:
As populations age globally, cognitive decline has become one of the most pressing
health challenges of our century. Among the most affected domains is decision making, a core
executive function (EF) that is particularly vulnerable to aging. In this context, aerobic exercise
has emerged as a promising strategy for preserving and improving brain health. However, a
critical question remains underexplored: whether improvements in executive function tasks truly
reflect enhanced decision-making capacity, or whether they index more circumscribed changes
in neural efficiency and network specificity. The heterogeneity of protocols has limited efforts to
standardize interventions and evaluate their long-term impact. This review examines studies
from the last decade that investigated aerobic and combined physical-cognitive training
programs in healthy older adults, focusing on their effects on decision-making and associated
neural changes. Evidence shows that chronic interventions (6 months to 2 years) improve EF
closely related to decision-making, including cognitive flexibility, task switching, and attentional
control. These behavioral gains correlate with neural adaptations in the PFC, including improved
cerebral oxygenation and preserved white matter microstructure when exercise is sustained
over time. At the molecular level, sustained aerobic activity has been associated with increased
VEGFR2 and Cathepsin B, and decreased BDNF expression, suggesting systemic plasticity
processes that may contribute to cortical integrity. These findings support the potential of long-
term, multimodal interventions that integrate physical, cognitive and social components to
promote durable neurobiological adaptations and enhance decision-making. Yet, across the
reviewed studies, none directly assessed decision-making under ecologically valid conditions,
and the improved performance in real-world decision-making remains assumed. We argue that
the field must move beyond descriptive summaries of cognitive gains and toward a more
rigorous framework that distinguishes between what has been measured, what has been
inferred, and what remains to be proven, particularly regarding the generalizability of exercise-
induced neural changes to everyday decision-making.

articles.title: Imagining the Future: Modality Effects of Mental Imagery, Verbal Reasoning, and Virtual Reality on Behavioural Engagement Predictions in Dysphoria
articles.authors: Eleanor Burton; Matthew Coxon; Jennifer Shevchenko
articles.abstract: Difficulties anticipating and engaging in future activities are characteristic of dysphoria and depression. This research investigated whether simulating future activities could improve prospective evaluations of behavioural engagement, and whether increasing immersion enhanced these effects. Across two studies, participants completed mental imagery, verbal reasoning, or (in Study 2) virtual reality tasks while evaluating future activities. In Study 1, both mental imagery and verbal reasoning improved perceived likelihood of engagement, importance, vividness, anticipatory pleasure, and mood. Mental imagery showed some selective advantages for vividness and anticipatory pleasure, although verbal reasoning produced broadly comparable effects. Study 2 introduced a virtual reality condition to examine whether greater immersion would strengthen these outcomes. Contrary to predictions, virtual reality did not produce additional benefits over mental imagery or verbal reasoning. All three tasks improved prospective evaluations across most measures, while mental imagery and verbal reasoning were more effective than virtual reality in increasing motivation. Participants in the verbal reasoning condition also reported similar levels of imagery use to those in the imagery condition, suggesting spontaneous imagery engagement. Overall, findings suggest that engaging in structured future-oriented thinking, regardless of modality, can positively influence expectations about future activities. The results suggest that simply engaging in the structured, deliberate simulation of future events, regardless of the specific technique used, may be sufficient to produce change. These findings have implications for low-cost cognitive interventions aimed at improving motivation and behavioural engagement in dysphoria.

articles.title: Harmonizing in Tune: Age of Acquisition in Melodic Recognition
articles.authors: Mahmoud Medhat Elsherif; Jon Catling
articles.abstract: Across cultures and languages, early-learned words are processed more quickly than late-learned words, an effect known as the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect. This advantage is argued to arise from the early development of semantic representations and the nature of the links between them. Specifically, the AoA effect is most pronounced in tasks requiring deep semantic processing or when the mapping between representations is arbitrary. However, the role of the AoA in music, where lexical-semantic processing may be less prominent, remains unclear. To investigate this, we used melody-melody, melody-title, and title-melody verification tasks. We found that the AoA were limited across most tasks. While melody-melody verification showed no AoA effect, a significant reverse AoA effect emerged in the melody-title and title-melody verification task. Late-acquired melodies were recognized more quickly and more accurately than early-acquired ones. This effect only occurred when imageability and naming was included in the model, suggesting that imageability and naming acts as a suppressor variable in the relationship between AoA and melody recognition. Our findings support the Representation Account of AoA, indicating that the musical lexicon is organised by the semantic distinctiveness of representations as opposed to the mapping between representations.

articles.title: Blurring of self-other boundaries is associated with awe and social bonding in an art ritual performance
articles.authors: Valerie van Mulukom; Robert Clark; Lexi Eikelboom; David Newheiser; Jyotsna Joseph; Miguel Farias
articles.abstract: Self‑transcendence in rituals and awe experiences tends to promote social bonding, particularly if these events involve synchronous movement. Here we suggest that the blurring of self-other boundaries, as a cognitive-perceptual mechanism underlying self-transcendence, may increase perceived self-other overlap, which in turn may lead to greater social connection, together with awe. Building on the body ownership illusion literature in which self-boundaries are routinely manipulated, a choreographed art‑ritual performance study was developed to examine whether synchronous movement and touch could change bodily self-other boundaries, and whether this would affect social bonding between dyads of strangers. One hundred and nineteen previously unacquainted participants took part in a performance with two dancers, who moved either in synchrony (main condition) or in canon (control condition) behind and next to the dyads of participants. Participants showed significant increases in social bonding across conditions, but the blurring of bodily self-other boundaries was stronger in the synchronous than in the in-canon condition. Increases in social bonding were most strongly predicted by self-other boundary blurring via increased perceived self-other overlap, whereas awe made only a modest additional contribution. These findings suggest that embodied blurring of self-other boundaries may be a key proximal mechanism linking self‑transcendent experiences to social connection.

articles.title: Design Psychology: Treating Digital Design as a Modifiable Factor in Mental Health
articles.authors: Nejra van Zalk; Joseph Firth; Lion Shahab; Jamie Brown; Yvonne Rogers; Jake Linardon; John Torous
articles.abstract: Digital technologies profoundly shape psychological experiences, yet research has predominantly focused on coarse exposure measures like screen time, yielding inconsistent associations with mental health. Here we propose Design Psychology, an interdisciplinary framework that conceptualises digital design features and affordances as modifiable influences on core psychological processes such as attention, reinforcement learning, social comparison, and perceived control. By integrating insights from human-computer interaction, behavioural science, and digital mental health, this framework elucidates causal pathways linking design to mental health outcomes. This perspective shifts focus from limiting technology use to optimising design elements to promote wellbeing. Our approach supports targeted, scalable interventions and informs the development of evidence-based design standards. Ultimately, Design Psychology offers a mechanistic and actionable foundation for creating digital environments that better support mental health across diverse populations.

articles.title: Large fluid - crystallised discrepancy profiles as a pragmatic cross-sectional proxy for an atypical cognitive trajectory in bipolar disorder: a multi-cohort reproducibility and meta-analytic study from the ICONIC‐BD.
articles.authors: Tamsyn Van Rheenen; Elysha Ringin; Christos Pantelis; Allan H Young; Anastasia Yocum; Lakshmi Yatham; Nefize Yalin; Timothy Wong; Eduard Vieta; Dimosthenis Tsapekos
articles.abstract: A large within-person discrepancy between fluid and crystallised cognition may represent a sensitive cross-sectional proxy for an atypical cognitive trajectory. However, this proxy has not been widely studied in bipolar disorder (BD), and replication data regarding the prevalence and predictors of large discrepancy profiles in BD are therefore lacking. Using a shared analytic pipeline, we analysed cross-sectional cognitive data from 16 independent BD cohorts across 10 countries (n= 3339). Fluid-crystallised discrepancy profiles (FCDPs) were defined as the within-cohort standardised difference between fluid and crystallised cognition. A large (>0.5SD difference) FCDP was taken as a proxy for an atypical cognitive trajectory. We estimated the prevalence of large FCDPs in each cohort and examined sociodemographic and clinical predictors using regression, then compared, synthesised, and meta-analysed the results. In two cohorts with prospective data, we calculated FCDPs in follow up data as well as longitudinal short-term (~1-2 years) fluid cognitive change scores. The pooled prevalence of large FCDPs was 34%, with similar proportions across cohorts despite differences in age, illness duration and clinical features. Clinical course variables showed little or no association with large FCDPs. In the prospective cohorts, large FCDPs were highly stable over ~2 years but largely unrelated to short-term longitudinal cognitive change. Nonetheless, ~20% of participants showed short-term decline, suggesting heterogeneity in how longer-term cognitive deviations and immediate change relate within BD. The pattern of results suggest that a sizeable subgroup of BD shows a meaningful discrepancy between fluid and crystallised cognition that is not readily explained by aging or illness chronicity indicators. The pattern is consistent with either a long-standing neurodevelopmental vulnerability or an early-onset decrement in fluid cognition that then remains relatively stable over time. However, another BD subgroup may still exhibit genuinely progressive cognitive deterioration that is not well captured by the discrepancy-based trajectory proxy.

articles.title: Parental socialization of child lying: How parental messages about lying, parenting-by-lying, and parenting styles are associated with child lying
articles.authors: Lisanne Schroer; Rianne Kok
articles.abstract: Children’s lying behaviour is thought to develop through both domain-specific socialization, such as parental modelling of lying and parental moral messages about lying or honesty, and broader domain-general socialization such as parenting styles. To date, no studies have simultaneously investigated these socialization domains in child lying. The present study used cross-sectional parent-report data from 217 parents of 4- to 8-year-olds to examine associations between parental messages about lying, parenting-by-lying (modelling of lying), parenting styles, and child lying. All three aspects of socialization were independently associated with child lying. First, more parenting-by-lying was associated with more child lying. Second, stronger parental emphasis on honesty was linked to less child lying. Third, higher levels of authoritarian parenting were associated with more child lying. Overall, this study illustrates how different aspects of socialization contribute independently to children’s moral development.

articles.title: Heterogeneous Variance Models with Gaussian Processes
articles.authors: Yvette Baurne; Frédéric Delmar; Jonas Wallin
articles.abstract: Understanding variability is a key focus in many areas of psychological research, with growing interest in modeling individual- and group-level variability. Although multilevel models such as heterogeneous variance models (HVMs) and Mixed-Effects Location-Scale (MELS) models have been used to capture these dynamics, they typically rely on linear assumptions and restrict temporal changes in variability to a single level. Recent calls for nonlinear approaches in psychology highlight the need for more flexible models that can better account for complex, dynamic processes. This paper introduces the use of Gaussian Processes (GPs) within the framework of HVMs to address these limitations. By incorporating GPs in HVMs, we allow for the modeling of nonlinear variability across multiple levels, including temporal dynamics at both the individual and group levels. We demonstrate the benefits of this approach in two empirical applications. Our findings show that using GPs provides an improved model fit compared to traditional linear methods and highlight the utility of GPs in variance modeling, offering new possibilities for studying dynamic and emergent processes in psychological and social science research.

articles.title: Expectation Development in Analytical Procedures: A Bayesian State Space Approach
articles.authors: Lotte Mensink; Henrik R. Godmann; jacques de swart; Koen Derks; ruud wetzels
articles.abstract: Analytical procedures involve comparing recorded amounts with expectations developed by the auditor. These expectations can be generated using methods that capture underlying relationships in the data. However, the methods currently used in analytical procedures do not fully exploit recent advances in statistical modeling. This article proposes Bayesian state space modeling as a promising alternative, evaluating its conceptual strengths and limitations relative to established methods and illustrating its applications in audit settings. The results suggest that Bayesian state space models offer both theoretical and practical advantages, and can therefore enhance expectation development in analytical procedures.

articles.title: Cognitive Enhancement through Interventions:  Evidence and Challenges
articles.authors: David Moreau
articles.abstract: Efforts to enhance human cognition have generated a vast and interdisciplinary body of research, spanning cognitive training, physical exercise, lifestyle and multidomain interventions, technological innovations, pharmacological enhancers, and education. This chapter critically reviews the evidence for these approaches, emphasizing the mechanisms of neuroplasticity, cognitive reserve, and transfer as foundational theoretical constructs. Although near-transfer effects are robust across training paradigms, evidence for far transfer remains scarce, underscoring the importance of aligning interventions with measurement tools that capture construct-level change rather than task-specific gains. Physical exercise and multidomain lifestyle programs currently provide the strongest and most reliable benefits, though education remains a powerful determinant of lifelong cognitive capacity through selection effects rather than reduced decline. The chapter concludes by highlighting methodological challenges—including construct validity, measurement sensitivity, and the epistemic limits of psychological constructs—while calling for integrated, multimodal frameworks that better reflect the dynamic and context-dependent nature of human cognition.

articles.title: How Prompting and Editing Shape Psychological Ownership in AI-Assisted Creative Writing
articles.authors: Niki Pennanen; Pyry Kanerva; Christian Guckelsberger
articles.abstract: Large language models are increasingly used as writing partners, but their use complicates whether writers experience the resulting text as genuinely their own. Such psychological ownership is especially relevant in AI-assisted writing because it contributes to the immediate and extended experience of AI use, and users may sign, share, revise, or be held accountable for texts whose content was partly generated by AI. Prior work shows that greater human input can increase ownership, but it remains unclear how ownership is shaped by two common forms of involvement: steering generation through prompting, directly revising generated text, and their combination. We report a preregistered mixed factorial experiment (N=273) in which online participants wrote short stories either without AI assistance or with AI assistance under one of two prompting styles (single-prompt vs. iterative), crossed with editing or not editing the AI-generated text. Editing increased ownership, especially for single-prompt generation. Iterative prompting increased ownership when editing was unavailable, but not when editing was available. These findings suggest that iterative prompting and post-generation editing are partially substitutable routes to psychological ownership, each providing opportunities for control and self-investment at different phases of the writing process. Fully manual writing nevertheless produced substantially higher ownership than all AI-assisted conditions, a gap that persisted in exploratory covariate analyses adjusting for effort, perceived story quality, and intrinsic motivation. The results suggest that AI writing tools should avoid one-shot delegation workflows when ownership matters, and instead support users in both steering and directly revising co-created text.

articles.title: Breaking Common Ground through Reciprocal Misalignment
articles.authors: Siham Abu Ali; Tatiana Philippova; Isaac Fradkin
articles.abstract: Establishing shared meaning is widely considered the normative, almost ubiquitous trajectory of linguistic interaction, while moments of misalignment are typically viewed as temporary interruptions that individuals attempt to repair. However, it is not uncommon to experience communication breakdowns that actively escalate rather than resolve. This raises a fundamental question: does interaction inherently lead people toward shared meaning, or is semantic alignment a reciprocal, socially contingent process? We conducted four experiments using a novel multiplayer word association task, where participants generated semantic associations while interacting with either human or simulated partners varying in semantic typicality. Our results show that, in general, social interaction motivates individuals to produce more conventional associations to support alignment. However, this tendency depended on partner’s behavior: when interacting with an atypical or difficult-to-align-with partner, individuals actively reduced their own typicality and tendency to match partner’s associations, effectively sacrificing semantic alignment. Such ‘reciprocal misalignment’ persisted even when participants were incentivized to maximize alignment. Our findings suggest that semantic misalignment may reflect a reciprocal, socially-motivated modulation of identity and differentiation, rather than solely a failure of coordination. This work sheds light on the interactional mechanisms underlying interpersonal distancing, communication breakdowns in neurodivergence, and potentially broader patterns of social polarization.

articles.title: Age-related changes in eye movements during pictorial recall in older adults
articles.authors: pauline Berthouzoz; Sandra Chiquet; Corinna Martarelli
articles.abstract: During the recall of pictorial information, individuals typically exhibit eye movements directed toward the locations they focused on during perceptual encoding. This behavior is thought to support memory retrieval by linking spatial information to cognitive processes. Given the known memory decline associated with healthy aging, investigating age-related changes in eye movements during recall is crucial. We compared the eye movement patterns of older (N = 38, 65–75 years) and younger adults (N = 37, 20–35 years) during different pictorial recall tasks. Both groups tended to look back at the original stimulus locations; however, this effect was less pronounced in older adults. They displayed less systematic and more dispersed fixations across the screen. Moreover, eye position information generalized to novel objects from previously seen categories in both groups. In terms of performance, older adults performed worse than younger adults on an old/new recognition task but not on a task assessing visual detail recall. Older adults rated their mental images as more vivid than younger adults did, which may reflect differences in the perception or interpretation of vividness, or subjective bias. The observed pattern of eye behavior, together with the relatively preserved recall performance, suggests that older adults rely less on embodied strategies, consistent with prior findings indicating an age-related shift toward gist-based representations. Overall, our results demonstrate that eye movement behavior during pictorial recall changes with age, highlighting the need for further investigation into the underlying mechanisms and possible consequences of this change.

articles.title: Evaluating the Imaginative Capability Scale:  Factor Structure, Refinement, and Measurement Invariance
articles.authors: Lindsay Ellis Lee; Kristen Lamb
articles.abstract: This study examined the factor structure and measurement invariance of the Imaginative Capability Scale (ICS) across sex within a sample of university students in the United States. Using confirmatory factor analysis and multigroup confirmatory factor analysis, results supported a three-factor structure consisting of initiating, conceiving, and transforming imagination. Although the original model demonstrated acceptable fit, model refinement resulted in a revised 24-item version with improved fit and stronger psychometric properties. Measurement invariance analyses supported configural, metric, and partial scalar invariance across males and females, suggesting that the latent structure of imaginative capability was invariant across sexes. The findings and the following discussion raise conceptual and construct validity considerations regarding the operationalization of imaginative capability. Implications for psychometric refinement, fairness in assessment, and future imagination research are discussed.

articles.title: Public insensitivity to Paris temperature targets
articles.authors: Ziqian Xia; Rachit Dubey; Sara Madureira Constantino
articles.abstract: The Paris Agreement committed nations to limiting warming to 1.5°C. As global average temperatures approach this threshold, these targets are increasingly used in public communication to communicate urgency. In a pre-registered survey of Americans shown warming scenarios of 1.5°C to 6.0°C (N = 986), we find that risk perceptions only weakly correlate with temperature rise (R2 = 0.02), with policy-critical comparisons like 1.5°C vs. 2.0°C showing no difference. Temperature targets, while important for policy, hold limited psychological meaning for lay audiences.

articles.title: What 5000 Babies Can Tell Us About Developing Minds and How to Study Them
articles.authors: Brianna McMillan; Heidi A Baumgartner; Christina Bergmann; Michael C. Frank; Kiley Hamlin; Dora Kampis; Melissa Kline Struhl; Eon-Suk Ko; Jessica Elizabeth Kosie; Casey Lew-Williams
articles.abstract: A decade of ManyBabies research, testing thousands of babies across hundreds of labs, has shown that some, but not all findings in infant research replicate well. Collectively, these projects have shown us that our methods carry limitations that larger samples alone cannot resolve. Here we present three lessons that point toward a more reliable, inclusive developmental science.

articles.title: Predictability Effects in Natural Reading are Logarithmic: Evidence from an Eye-Movement Replication of Brothers and Kuperberg (2021)
articles.authors: Ryan Buggy; Stephanie Cho; Cory Shain; Adrian Staub
articles.abstract:
The question of whether the relationship between a word’s predictability and its processing time is linear or logarithmic is of substantial theoretical importance, as it arbitrates between theories of the predictability effect (pre-activation vs. Surprisal) and has implications for sentence processing generally. While several previous corpus studies have obtained evidence for a logarithmic relationship, Brothers and Kuperberg (2021) obtained evidence for a linear relationship in a large self-paced reading study with well-controlled experimental materials. Here, we use Brothers and Kuperberg’s materials in an eyetracking-during-reading experiment. We find clear evidence for a logarithmic relationship between predictability and the eye movement measures of first fixation duration and gaze duration; this relationship is clearest when using predictability estimates from the large language model GPT-2, which can distinguish small differences in predictability at the low end of the scale. We find that this conclusion is robust to log transformation of the reading time measures, and we find that the relationship between predictability and the log odds of word skipping may also be logarithmic. These results support Surprisal as an account of predictability effects in natural reading.
Keywords: eye movements, lexical predictability effects, surprisal, preactivation, reading

articles.title: Does self-projection have a dominant temporal direction? In nine countries, people feel more similar to their future than past selves
articles.authors: Jareef Martuza; Simen Bø; Helge Thorbjørnsen; Hallgeir Sjåstad
articles.abstract: Do you project your current self—the person you are today—equally across time? Compared to the present, 12 studies (total N = 11,416) reveal that people feel more similar to their future selves than their equidistant past selves. This future-oriented asymmetry in self-projection is directionally consistent across nine countries, with variation in effect size, and holds across different time horizons. Time-asymmetric self-projection covaries with higher levels of emotional connection between present and future selves and lower levels of perceived future self-change. We also provide preliminary causal-chain evidence for perceived self-change as a process by experimentally manipulating self-change beliefs. The future-oriented asymmetry appears to be self-focused, as it does not extend to others. Finally, future-oriented self-projection can lead to miscalibrated expectations: People expect their future selves to be more satisfied with hypothetical decisions made in the present than they currently would be with the same decisions in the past.

articles.title: Salivary cortisol responses to academic stress in university students following a dog-assisted intervention
articles.authors: Jose María Peinado Herreros; María Jesús Lirola; Meriem Khaled Gijón; Emilio Jesús Lizarte Simón; José Gijón Puerta
articles.abstract:
Background
Academic stress represents a major challenge for university students, particularly during examination periods, triggering both psychological and physiological responses. In addition to circadian cortisol secretion, the cortisol awakening response (CAR) and the area under the curve (AUC) are widely used indicators of hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis activity. Animal-assisted interventions have shown potential for modulating stress responses, although evidence based on diurnal cortisol dynamics remains limited.
Objective
This study aimed to examine the effects of a dog-assisted intervention on physiological stress responses in university students, using diurnal salivary cortisol secretion, CAR, and AUC as primary indicators.
Methods
A quasi-experimental study was conducted with 95 university students from two Spanish universities assigned to experimental (dog-assisted intervention) and control groups. Salivary cortisol samples were collected at six time points throughout the day under two conditions: a regular academic day and the day preceding an examination. Psychological variables were assessed using the Depression Anxiety Stress Scales-21 (DASS-21). Non-parametric analyses were performed to evaluate within- and between-group differences.
Results
Both groups showed a physiological circadian cortisol secretion pattern, characterized by peak levels occurring 30 minutes after awakening and the lowest levels before bedtime. Significant increases in CAR were observed in both groups on the day preceding an examination (p < .001), supporting the anticipatory effect of academic stress. However, the control group exhibited a greater increase in CAR, whereas the experimental group showed a broader distribution of positive ΔAUC responses (p = .003). These findings suggest a differential pattern of HPA axis activation between groups.
Conclusions
The dog-assisted intervention was not associated with a reduction in physiological stress responses, but rather with a differential pattern of cortisol activity characterized by lower anticipatory reactivity and greater variability in cumulative cortisol responses across the day. This pattern may reflect a more heterogeneous physiological response to academic stress. Further randomized studies are needed to confirm these findings and clarify the underlying mechanisms.

articles.title: A Novel Cognitive Framework to Enhance the Design and Evaluation of Music-Based Interventions
articles.authors: Cloie Dobias; Marusa Laure; Frederick J. Gallun; Susanne M. Jaeggi; Aaron R. Seitz
articles.abstract: We introduce a novel Cognitive Framework to enhance the design and evaluation of Music-Based Interventions (MBIs) targeting cognition. MBIs are increasingly recognized as meaningful contributors to health and well-being across the lifespan, and recent initiatives are advancing MBI standardization and reproducibility. However, existing variability (including heterogeneous intervention designs, inconsistent mechanistic reasoning, and minimal specificity regarding hypothesized outcomes) impedes evidence-based interpretability and impact. To address this problem, the proposed Cognitive Framework provides a theoretically-based model to guide MBI design, thus clarifying the mechanisms through which music influences cognition by explicitly linking musical features to the cognitive processes they are hypothesized to engage. The present paper focuses first on characterizing this novel conceptual framework in the context of existing literature, and then on operationalizing the framework methodologically by providing clear next steps for application and implementation. Rather than broadly examining unitary constructs, the Cognitive Framework distinguishes between granular features and subcomponents of both music and cognition, emphasizing their distinct contributions to intervention engagement. Integrating these elements into a structured model, the framework provides a clearer foundation for designing and evaluating mechanism-informed MBIs targeting cognition. Utilizing this Cognitive Framework in future research can improve effectiveness and interpretability of MBIs by precisely assessing how musical engagement can be harnessed to support cognitive health.

articles.title: Simplicity is Used to Promote Accuracy, More Than Efficiency, in Explanation Evaluations
articles.authors: Thalia Vrantsidis
articles.abstract: People often prefer simpler, single-cause explanations over more complex, multi-cause explanations, yet the function of this preference remains unclear. Does simplicity serve to increase the accuracy of explanation evaluations, improve efficiency, or both? The current research investigates this question in the context of evaluating which of two competing explanations is more likely—one simple (single-cause) and one complex (two-cause)—when base rate probabilities are either provided or inferred from background knowledge. Across three studies (N = 1494), participants were asked to evaluate explanations under different goal conditions (accuracy vs. efficiency), to report the extent to which they relied on different types of information (simplicity and probabilities), and to report what purpose they thought simplicity served. Results showed that simplicity was used more under accuracy than efficiency goals, was thought to promote accuracy more than efficiency, and, in line with potentially accuracy-enhancing strategies, it was generally combined with probability information rather than used as an alternative to it. Moreover, simplicity use was associated with less efficient (slower) judgments, though these were only sometimes more accurate, depending on the other biases present in a given case. These findings suggest that simplicity’s use in explanation evaluation is primarily intended to promote accuracy, more so than efficiency, though, in reality, it may only do so imperfectly. These findings offer new insights into the functions of preferences for simpler explanations, and into the strategies people use to evaluate competing explanations.

articles.title: Stereotypes of Voluntary Simplifiers: More Sociable, Moral, and Pro-Environmentally Driven, yet Equally Capable Compared to Other Lifestyles
articles.authors: Eva Moreno-Bella; Lope Tejero-Peregrina; Laura del Carmen Torres-Vega; Guillermo B. Willis
articles.abstract: In this research, we explored the stereotype content of those who follow a voluntary simplicity lifestyle, a reduction consumption lifestyle associated with pro-environmental values, social justice, and personal motives. Through three experimental studies (NTotal = 1,116), we manipulated different consumption styles and evaluated stereotype content dimensions, such as sociability, morality, assertiveness, and ability. In Study 1, we compared profiles of voluntary simplicity, high-consumption, and a control group, and found that the voluntary simplifier profile was perceived as more sociable and moral but less assertive than the high-consumption profile. There were no differences in the ability dimension. In Study 2, we replicated these findings and further observed motives for behaviour based on environmental commitment were especially characteristic of the voluntary simplifier profile. In Study 3, we compared two reduced consumption lifestyles: voluntary simplicity and frugalism. Voluntary simplifier profile was seen as more sociable, moral, and assertive than the frugalist profile, with both profiles rated equally in ability. These findings represent a first step in studying how voluntary simplifiers are perceived.

articles.title: Using non-binding experiments to estimate causal effects with instrumental variables regression: A tutorial
articles.authors: Sanford R Student
articles.abstract: Often, causal effects of interest in psychological research involve causes that cannot be randomly assigned. Instrumental variables (IV) regression, a cornerstone of the econometric literature on causal inference with nonrandom causes, allows for unbiased causal inference in the face of several threats to the validity of causal inference using ordinary least squares regression. IV is enabled by an instrument – a third variable that affects the outcome only through its influence on the hypothesized cause. One way to study causal effects in psychology is thus the random assignment of an intervention that drives change in the cause of interest but is otherwise unrelated to the outcome, followed by IV analysis to produce a valid causal effect. Moreover, IV regression models can be estimated straightforwardly as structural equation models (SEMs). Based on open data from a recently published study in the economics literature, this tutorial outlines the use of SEM to estimate the causal effect of a behavior of interest, exercise, on an outcome of interest, academic performance, enabled by an instrument –  a randomly-distributed free gym card – that constitutes a random, non-binding manipulation of exercise. The role of covariates in IV is discussed, and R code is provided for all analyses.

articles.title: Robustness of Regularized Regression Methods Under Compound Model Misspecification: A Simulation Benchmarking Study
articles.authors: Onur Ramazan; Yiu Wa Lui
articles.abstract: Regularized regressions are widely used in psychological research where the fitted model is assumed to be correctly specified. Yet, psychological data routinely violates assumptions of linearity, homoscedasticity and additivity which raises questions about estimator robustness when models are misspecified. The present simulation study examined how regularized estimators—ridge, LASSO, elastic net, adaptive LASSO, SCAD and MCP—degrade under compound misspecification. Using high-dimensional data with block-wise correlation (p = 2,000) and three sample sizes (n = 500, 2,000 and 10,000), we generated outcomes under correct specification and compound misspecification incorporating nonlinearity, heteroscedasticity and omitted interactions. Performance was evaluated through predictive accuracy (out-of-sample R² and RMSE) and selection stability (mean Jaccard index across replicates). Results revealed that misspecification produced modest degradation in predictive accuracy with R² reductions of 0.006–0.017 across sample sizes. Selection stability was minimally affected by misspecification at smaller samples. At n = 10,000, non-convex methods achieved highest stability.

articles.title: How to Run Round Robin Social Interaction Studies: A Tutorial for the Efficient Capture of Natural Social Behaviour
articles.authors: Erin A Heerey; Samantha Mary Jones; Jeremias Campos; Amanda Friesen
articles.abstract: The study of natural social behaviour is a frontier discipline in psychology. Although such work is highly necessary for advancing and refining theories of social behaviour, social cognition, evolutionary psychology, relationships, and more, researchers rarely commit to such intensive research designs due to the anticipated effort involved. In particular, to understand social behaviour as it naturally unfolds, scientists must be able to collect and handle a high volume of rich social data efficiently, effectively, and reproducibly. Moreover, because social behaviour is highly individualistic and context dependent, it is critical to capture variation in people’s natural social behaviour across multiple interaction partners – i.e., in “round-robin” research designs. Significant issues can arise in the administration of such designs, disincentivizing the collection of naturalistic round-robin social data. Here, we provide a tutorial based on a recent study, with customizable resources designed to mitigate the inherent challenges associated with this type of research. These include the semi-automation of in-person data collection and the automation of data management.

articles.title: Continuous theta burst stimulation over bilateral occipitotemporal cortex does not modulate negative affective appraisal or memory: a randomised crossover trial
articles.authors: Christian Wollmann; Nathalie Schicktanz; Thomas Schlitt; Sonia Prieto Wunderlich; Amanda Aerni; Andreas Papassotiropoulos; Dominique de Quervain; Ehssan Amini
articles.abstract:
The bilateral occipitotemporal cortex (OTC) has been implicated in the successful encoding of aversive stimuli in fMRI studies, but its causal contribution to affective appraisal and memory formation has not been tested. We conducted a single-blind, active-control, counterbalanced crossover trial in which 27 healthy adults received bilateral 30 Hz continuous theta burst stimulation (cTBS) over the OTC or over an active control site, in two visits 7–21 days apart. Following stimulation, participants viewed 72 standardised emotional pictures (positive, neutral and negative), rated them for valence and arousal, performed a free-recall task, and re-rated the same pictures approximately 30 minutes later.
The pre-registered primary outcome, valence ratings of negative pictures immediately after stimulation, did not differ between conditions (R = 0.01; mixed-model t (23.69) = 0.53, p = 0.60). Secondary analyses showed no effect of stimulation on immediate arousal ratings of negative pictures (R = < 0.01, p = 0.86), free-recall memory for negative pictures (R = 0.01, p = 0.80), or on any corresponding outcome for neutral or positive pictures. Among the secondary outcomes, delayed valence ratings of negative pictures differed nominally between conditions, but this effect did not survive correction for multiple comparisons.
We found no evidence that the present bilateral 30 Hz cTBS protocol over group-defined OTC targets modulates immediate valence appraisal, arousal or subsequent memory for negative pictures.

articles.title: Individual differences in object localisation are associated with video game experience and attentional tracking capacity
articles.authors: Fiammetta Marini; Bahar Yaman; Artyom Zinchenko; Harriet Batty; Mauro Manassi; Julia Föcker
articles.abstract: Object localisation is a fundamental function of vision. Although localisation ability exhibits sub-stantial and stable individual differences, the factors underlying this variability remain poorly under-stood. One intriguing possibility is that these differences arise from extensive exposure to environments that demand high spatial precision. Here, we investigated whether experience in visually demanding en-vironments is associated with this variability by comparing habitual video game players (VGPs) and non-video game players (NVGPs) on a localisation task. To investigate the mechanisms underlying these differences, we also measured (1) performance on a Multiple Object Tracking task, as a proxy for attentional capacity, and (2) serial dependence strength, a perceptual bias reflecting temporal integration of spatial information. VGPs exhibited superior localisation performance relative to NVGPs, with local-isation accuracy increasing as a function of weekly gaming time. Importantly, localisation performance was strongly associated with attentional tracking ability, whereas serial dependence strength remained stable across groups. These findings reveal a robust association between prolonged gaming experience and localisation performance, suggesting that individual differences in object localisation might be shaped by experience-dependent plasticity mediated by attentional mechanisms rather than changes in temporal integration.

articles.title: Intellectual humility and learning outcomes in online health information-seeking
articles.authors: Xinyi Wang; Benjamin Muzekari; Thandi Lyew; Jessica Fishman; Mark Cinquegrani; Andy SL Tan; Melissa Mercincavage; David M. Lydon-Staley
articles.abstract: Online health information-seeking involves actively trying to resolve uncertainty by searching for, evaluating, and updating knowledge in response to information. The present study examined whether adults learn from brief online health information searches and whether learning outcomes are associated with intellectual humility and access to authoritative sources. In a laboratory study, 111 adults (mean age = 38.95 years, 44.1% women) completed a multi-trial health information-seeking task, answering factual questions before and after online search.
