Participants were randomly assigned to an experimental condition in which they received links to authoritative sources prior to search (n=53) or to a control condition in which no links were provided (n=58).  Participants showed improved accuracy (b=1.68, p<0.001) and confidence (b=1.17, p<0.001) following search, but access to authoritative sources did not significantly improve accuracy (b=-0.17, p=0.66) or confidence (b=-0.34, p=0.08) overall. Intellectual humility was positively associated with post-search answer accuracy (b=1.05, p=0.01). Intellectual humility also moderated the effect of authoritative source access on confidence (b=0.92, p=0.01), such that the association between intellectual humility and post-search confidence was stronger when an authoritative source was provided. Findings suggest that access to high-quality information alone may be insufficient for learning and that intellectual humility may help determine how effectively individuals learn from and respond to online health information.

articles.title: Statistical assessment of a combinatorial relationship algorithm: A comparative study of high-performance dyads and randomized controls
articles.authors: Phillip Thompson
articles.abstract: Carl Jung and many others have attempted to validate astrology with the scientific method. This research used an algorithm based on the ancient process of synastry to examine the combined birth charts for pairs of people and to calculate a number that measures astrological compatibility, the Starscore. A comparison of one million random Starscores to those for champion pairs in artistic swimming, figure skating and doubles tennis showed significantly different means. Correlations were also found for winners and runners-up of The Amazing Race, celebrity friends and self-identified pairs of close relationships, however the average Starscore for a database of divorced couples was not significantly different than the random group. False discovery rate and family-wise error rate analyses validated the statistical significance of the close relationships survey results. While not proof of causation, these correlations support Jung’s theory that the positions of the planets in our solar system could affect the development of human life by modulating emissions from the sun, and they support Jung’s belief that studying astrology can contribute to our understanding of human behavior. Once other researchers replicate the close relationships survey, the quantitative validation of astrology could justify its use as a tool for understanding human behavior.

articles.title: Client-Reported Prevalence of Repressed Memory Recovery in Therapy: A Representative U.K. Sample
articles.authors: Lawrence Patihis
articles.abstract: The prevalence of recovered memories of previously unknown abuse is important because of the risk of memory construction. Asking therapists about such prevalence comes with inherent biases, a problem bypassed by asking a representative sample of therapy clients directly. This study examined client-reported prevalence in a representative U.K. sample of 1,507 adults. Of the total sample, 40% reported receiving therapy. Among these, 4.9% reported that a therapist discussed repressed memories of abuse (12% of those who received therapy), with half indicating that the therapist raised it first (2.5% of total sample). Recovered memories of abuse during the course of therapy of previously unknown abuse were reported by 3.6% of the total sample (8.9% of therapy goers). Recovered memories were most associated with primal therapy, internal family systems, and psychodynamic therapy. Higher rates of memory recovery in therapy occurred when therapy started in the 1980s and recent decades (2006–2024: 8–12%). Qualitative comments provided context to the skepticism in some and belief in repressed memories in others. The findings suggest that recovered memory practices continue to likely affect millions in the UK—resembling U.S. patterns, with implications for disseminating the possibility of memory construction to the public, and in the training of therapists.

articles.title: Planning with Others in Mind
articles.authors: Nastaran Arfaei; Wei Ji Ma
articles.abstract: From playing team sports to large international projects, planning is rarely done in isolation, but instead in the presence of other agents who affect the environment and the future possible action space. This shared nature of environment, cost, and reward is particularly important when planning collaboratively, where reward maximization depends on the actions of all collaborating agents. How people incorporate the future actions of others in their planning process is largely unknown. To approach this question, we developed a collaborative dyadic turn-taking task with long decision sequences. We found that participants effectively collaborated in this context and that they incorporated future potential actions of their partner in their planning process. We constructed and tested computational models of people's choices. Among these, a collaborative heuristic search algorithm that simulates the actions of themselves and their partner in parallel, fit the data the best and accounted well for a range of properties of people's choices and response times. These findings lay a foundation for understanding the mechanisms that underlie intertwined planning and action prediction during collaboration.

articles.title: Trust drives preference for contractualist artificial agents across social contexts
articles.authors: Helena Gil-Buitrago; Luis Marcos Vidal; Clara Pretus
articles.abstract: As artificial agents are integrated into professional and social environments, fostering both human trust and alignment with Trustworthy AI principles such as human autonomy becomes increasingly relevant. Studies suggest that the moral frameworks underlying an agent’s decision-making – typically utilitarian and deontological reasoning – can influence how much trust humans place in it. Here, we examine whether contractualist reasoning – a third type of moral reasoning that prioritizes human autonomy – influences evaluations and partner preferences toward artificial and human agents. Across three experiments (N = 750), we found that contractualist agents are perceived as more trustworthy and reliable and elicit greater behavioral trust than utilitarian and deontological agents. Compared to these alternatives, contractualist reasoning more than doubled the likelihood of choosing an agent for social tasks across professional domains (from roughly 20% to 50%), an effect mediated by perceptions of trust and warmth. Incorporating forms of reasoning that prioritize human autonomy into artificial agents may have important implications for fostering trust and cooperation in real-world environments.

articles.title: The Verifiability of Recovered Memories of Abuse
articles.authors: Mirjam ter Beek; Henry Otgaar; Linsey Raymaekers; Corine de Ruiter; Paul Riesthuis; Tamara L. F. De Beuf; Anne Hoving; Meike Hogenkamp; Bruno Verschuere
articles.abstract: Different ways of remembering sexual abuse - continuous memories, spontaneously recovered memories, and recovered memories following an active search - have been shown to differ in the availability of case-level corroborative evidence. The present study examined whether memory types in delayed sexual abuse statements also differ in terms of verifiability, a statement feature previously associated with differences between truthful and deceptive accounts. Victim statements from archival police files (N = 140) were classified according to how the abuse was remembered. Confirmatory analyses showed that statements based on continuous memories contained more verifiable details than statements based on recovered memories following an active search. Exploratory analyses indicated that details reported following an active search were more often disconfirmed than those reported in continuous or spontaneously recovered memories. Verifiability may extend beyond deception detection paradigms to capture systematic differences associated with memory retrieval and reconstruction processes in delayed sexual abuse reports.

articles.title: Computational Modeling of Structured Memory Search
articles.authors: Wenjia Joyce Zhao; Sudeep Bhatia
articles.abstract: Semantic memory encodes both the individual features of concepts and the relations between them. We use computational memory models to study how people search through these knowledge structures. Participants in our paradigm are cued with a target word pair and are asked to retrieve other word pairs from memory. We find that both featural and relational knowledge guide memory search, but they are recruited differently depending on the task and exhibit distinct dynamics. Featural knowledge plays the strongest role in all cases, but is especially important in free association between word pairs. Relational knowledge has a robust effect on analogy generation but plays a weak role in free association. Additionally, instructions to be creative in an analogy generation task reduce reliance on featural knowledge but not relational knowledge. Finally, the use of featural knowledge decays strongly with successive responses whereas the use of relational knowledge is persistent across the trial. Importantly, we are able to quantitatively predict these effects, parameterize their associated mechanisms, fit parameters to retrieval sequences, and perform statistical tests on parameters, using a principled extension of established memory models. This allows us to formally integrate theoretical work on structured reasoning with computational models of memory search, laying the groundwork for parametric cognitive process models of high-level cognition.

articles.title: A spatiotemporal hierarchy for social interaction perception in the lateral visual stream
articles.authors: Emalie McMahon; Elizabeth Jiwon Im; Michael Bonner; Leyla Isik
articles.abstract: The lateral visual stream has been recently proposed as a third visual stream, in addition to the ventral and dorsal streams, specialized for processing dynamic social content. While prior work has suggested that the regions of this pathway form a hierarchy representing increasingly abstract information, the computations along this pathway are still largely unknown. High spatiotemporal resolution data are particularly informative for characterizing the information flow and thus neural computations across different brain regions. Using a novel regression approach, we combine data from EEG, fMRI, and behavior in response to the same videos to leverage the high temporal resolution of EEG and whole-brain spatial resolution of fMRI. We find that low-level visual features are represented in early visual cortex with a short temporal latency and are not represented in higher-level regions of the lateral stream. Further, we find that mid-level features are represented in mid-level lateral regions with a shorter latency than high-level features in more anterior regions of the lateral pathway. However, both mid- and high-level features were decodable in anterior regions of the lateral pathway with a similar latency. Together, these results provide evidence that features of social actions are processed rapidly in the lateral visual stream in a manner that is consistent with hierarchical processing, but the lateral stream does not exhibit a strict temporal sequence of representational transformations along the posterior-to-anterior axis.

articles.title: From homework questions to emotional confessions: the psychological implications of children confiding in AI, "relational drift", and developmental vulnerability
articles.authors: Nomisha Chandran Kurian
articles.abstract: Rising evidence shows children and adolescents using AI to confide personal worries and seek advice. However, while empirical data on this phenomenon is growing, there has not yet been sustained conceptual work on the psychological and ethical implications of children - as developing beings still forming their identities and views of the world - confiding in artificial systems. This article aims to help address this gap. It unpacks why the process of confiding matters for children developmentally. It introduces the concept of “relational drift” to show how children may begin chatting with AI in strictly task-focused ways (for example, homework help), yet the emotionally responsive nature of contemporary LLMs can result in some child-users, particularly those who are vulnerable or socially isolated, seeking more personal and emotional support. The article examines the psycho-ethical implications of these liminal, ambiguous spaces of child-AI interaction. In doing so, the article also helps address the lack of child-centred, developmentally informed theorisations in the field of AI ethics. In a time when child-AI interactions are becoming more emotionally complex, child-safe AI design, policy and governance must move beyond narrow discussions of content moderation and take psychological and relational dynamics seriously.

articles.title: Mind Wandering and Task-Unrelated Thoughts: off-task mental activity during rest, evoked by preferred music, does not impede short-term memory consolidation
articles.authors: Juan Felipe Ariza; Juan David Leongómez
articles.abstract: Research has shown that post-learning rest periods are beneficial for memory consolidation. These same periods of rest are also prone to evoke mind wandering (MW) episodes which, when prevalent during learning, have been observed to prevent consolidation. However, although research has focused on characterising the role of MW during encoding, less is known about its role on consolidation when occurring during post-learning rest periods. Using the caught probe paradigm, we measured the effect of task-unrelated thought (TUT) episodes (as a proxy for MW) occurring during two conditions of post-learning rest, in silence and with preferred music, over performance in a recognition and classification task. We found that TUT episodes during neither type of rest are predictive of performance in the classification task, even though participants are predicted to perform above chance. We additionally found that participants are predicted to perform above chance in the recognition task after rest with music, but no interaction between TUT episodes and performance after rest with music; and that the prevalence of TUT episodes interacts with rest in silence to predict performance in the recognition task, whereby a higher number of TUT episodes during rest in silence predicted higher performance.

articles.title: SeMiLLa: An R Package and Shiny App for LLM-Assisted Construction and Pre-Empirical Semantic Screening of Psychometric Scales
articles.authors: Jose Ventura-Leon
articles.abstract: SeMiLLa is an R package that brings together six phases of LLM-assisted psychometric scale development under a single interface (nine phases and 25 steps in its Shiny app), with 65 public functions and six extensions (version 1.1) grounded in 2022-2026 evidence on LLM use in psychometrics. An offline check on synthetic data showed the four new functions run stably (classification accuracy 100 %, ARI 1.00, explained variance 0.94 with sparsity 0.04 in regularized EFA, and r = 0.99 with lambda = 0.03 in predicted criterion validity), framed as a pre-empirical algorithmic step prior to validation against human responses. The empirical validation spanned three phases: (1) values from SeMiLLa were compared with those reported in nine Spanish validation papers (168 items, n = 4,085) extracted via extraer_psicometria_pdf(); (2) the semantic workflow was compared with uniform calculations over 10 raw OpenPsychometrics datasets (n of about 13,500), five reproduced in canonical Spanish; and (3) the workflow was applied to five Peruvian couple-relationships scales (n = 315 university students). SeMiLLa estimated aggregated reliability with a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.04 against raw data and 0.17 against the Peruvian Spanish scales, whereas the correlation between empirical factor loading and within-dimension semantic coherence reached r = 0.59 on raw data. This item-level concordance held in Phase 2 (English) but was lower and even negative in Phase 3 Spanish (Jealousy Scale, r = -0.73). The clustering-consensus item-factor assignment matched the empirical maximum-loading assignment on 74 % of items (mean ARI 0.37), and an adversarial item probing test on WLEIS reached a sensitivity of 1.00 up to four poisoned items. An ordinal robustness analysis supported these results: polychoric reliability was somewhat higher than Pearson (+0.019 to +0.122) without changing the ordering across scales, and a WLSMV CFA showed adequate fit in several scales (e.g., DASS-21, CFI = 0.971). SeMiLLa provides stable estimates of aggregated reliability and item-factor assignment across languages, whereas item-level concordance depends on the conceptual overlap among dimensions and the lexical diversity of items. Consequently, SeMiLLa works as a pre-empirical screening tool, not a substitute for response-based validation.

articles.title: Links between Caregiver–Child Engagement and Later Language in Toddlers with Autistic and Non-Autistic Older Siblings
articles.authors: Drina Agojci; Jennifer Markfeld; Kristen Bottema-Beutel; Ruoxi Guo; Caroline Braun; Shannon Crowley LaPoint; So Yoon Kim; Grace Pulliam; S. Madison Clark; Bahar Keceli-Kaysili
articles.abstract:
This study explored the degree to which time spent in higher- and lower-order caregiver–child supported joint engagement was associated with later language in toddlers at increased likelihood for autism because they have an autistic older sibling (Sibs-autism) and in toddlers at population-level likelihood for autism (i.e., toddlers with non-autistic older siblings; Sibs-NA).
Participants were 51 toddlers (27 Sibs-autism; 24 Sibs-NA) aged 21–27 months at the first time point (Time 1). Toddlers were seen again around 36 months (Time 2). Caregiver–child engagement states were measured via two, 15-minute caregiver–child free play sessions at Time 1. Child language was assessed via norm-referenced, standardized assessments and caregiver report questionnaires at Time 2. Regression models evaluated associations between engagement states and later receptive and expressive language.
Time spent in supported joint engagement was significantly associated with later receptive language only. Higher-order supported joint engagement had a significant positive association with later receptive language, whereas lower-order supported joint engagement did not. This association was stronger for Sibs-autism. 
These results provide insights into how caregiver–child engagement relates to later language in Sibs-autism and Sibs-NA. Further work is necessary to understand how to best support caregivers to optimize language learning environments for toddlers.

articles.title: Modeling Item Difficulty in Large-Scale Game-Based Cognitive Ability Assessment: An Illustration Using Wordle
articles.authors: Priscilla Achaa-Amankwaa; Jonas Walter; Valentin Hübner; Philipp Hübner; Ulrich Schroeders
articles.abstract: Building on the long and successful tradition of incorporating games into cognitive ability assessment, we investigated the popular word puzzle game Wordle, which integrates verbal abilities (lexical knowledge, orthographic processing), strategic word retrieval, and reasoning. Specifically, we examined the extent to which item difficulty in the German adaptation, GridWords, can be predicted using linguistic features, using a large online database that comprised 872 items collected from 76,095 players over two years. Item difficulty was operationalized via the number of attempts needed to solve an item, with scores adjusted for possible within-person practice effects across repeated gameplay. Moving beyond previous approaches that were limited to narrow sets of predictors, we integrated a broad set of features from psycholinguistic research on word recognition, such as word and letter frequencies, orthographic neighborhood size, and letter repetitions. Using regularized machine-learning methods in a nested cross-validation approach, we found out-of-sample predictive performances of R² = 52% (elastic net regression) and R² = 58% (gradient boosting machines) in item difficulty prediction. The cumulative consonant and unigram position frequencies, number of letter repetitions, and number of orthographic neighbors of the target words ranked among the most important features. We discuss feature importances as indicators of the underlying cognitive processes during gameplay, particularly with regard to the relevance of implicit knowledge of lexical regularities and the use of deliberate strategies. We also consider the strengths and limitations of using online games for cognitive ability assessment.

articles.title: Memories of Sexual Abuse in the Distant Past: Subtypes and Corroboration in a Legal Context
articles.authors: Mirjam ter Beek; Henry Otgaar; Linsey Raymaekers; Corine de Ruiter; Paul Riesthuis; Tamara L. F. De Beuf; Anne Hoving; Meike Hogenkamp; Bruno Verschuere
articles.abstract: Objective: Assessing the validity of testimony is critical in sexual abuse cases, particularly in case of recovered memories, whose authenticity has been considered controversial. The current study examined whether different types of memories (i.e., continuous, spontaneously recovered, recovered following an active search) could be reliably distinguished and whether they differed in the extent to which they are supported by evidence. Hypotheses: We predicted that three types of memories could be reliably differentiated: Continuous memories, spontaneously recovered memories, and recovered memories following an active search. We further predicted that continuous memories and spontaneously recovered memories would be more frequently corroborated by evidence than recovered memories following an active search. Method: We analyzed 140 police files on complex cases of sexual abuse in the distant past in which the prosecutor requested advice from an expert committee of behavioral experts. One pair of coders identified memory type based on continuity and retrieval, and another set of coders scored the degree of supporting evidence. Results: Memory types could be differentiated reliably. Statements from (alleged) victims in the continuous memory group were corroborated more often than those in the recovered memory-following-search group, which were rarely supported by strong evidence. The data were inconclusive regarding whether recovered memories following an active search and spontaneously recovered memories differ in the extent to which they were corroborated by evidence. Exploratory analyses showed that spontaneously recovered memories were more often related to single incidents, whereas recovered memories following an active search frequently involved sexual abuse over an extended period, including reports of multiple perpetrators. Conclusions: Reports of sexual abuse in the distant past can be reliably categorized into three types that help shed light on the likelihood of finding corroborating evidence in a criminal context. Recovered memories should not be treated as a homogeneous phenomenon because their phenomenology varies.

articles.title: Loneliness in the Human Brain: Structural and Functional MRI Correlates and Research Priorities
articles.authors: Christian K. Tamnes; Livia Tomova
articles.abstract: Loneliness, the subjective experience of a perceived discrepancy between desired and actual social connection, is robustly associated with poor mental and physical health across the lifespan. Although not uniformly increasing in prevalence, loneliness is rising among adolescents and young adults, making it an important public health concern. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) offers a means to examine the neural pathways through which loneliness emerges and affects health. A growing body of structural and functional MRI research implicates differences in regions and networks central to social cognition, internally directed thought, episodic memory, and executive control, including most consistently the default mode network, medial temporal structures, and prefrontal cortex. Here, we review this literature, critically assess its limitations, and outline priorities for future research, including the need for refined measurement, longitudinal developmental and experimental designs, and integration with genetic and contextual perspectives.

articles.title: How to tell whether AI is behaviourally human-like
articles.authors: Jamal Amani Rad; Stephane Hess; Thomas Hancock
articles.abstract: AI systems are increasingly described as behaving like humans. But passing as human in conversation, sharing answers or achieving similar benchmark scores is not enough to justify that specific label. Drawing on behavioural modelling, we propose five practical tests for deciding when claims of behavioural human-likeness have genuinely been earned.

articles.title: Prosody and Voice Quality in the Connected Speech of Individuals with Latent Aphasia
articles.authors: Cong Zhang; Tong Li; Gayle Dede; Chris Salis
articles.abstract:
Purpose: We explored the prosody and voice quality of people with latent aphasia, a mild form of aphasia, against a neurotypical control group. In contrast to people with moderate to severe aphasia, people with latent aphasia can have similar speech production compared to neurotypical controls. 

Method: We analysed three prosodic measures (fundamental frequency [f0], intensity, and duration) and three voice quality measures (local jitter, local shimmer, and harmonic-to-noise ratio) of utterance-initial and utterance-final words from ten speakers with latent aphasia and ten matched controls. Three relevant linguistic features (syllable number, part of speech, and word type [content or function words]) were also considered. Linear mixed-effects models assessed the effects of word position and speaker group on prosodic features.

Results: Both groups showed parallel patterns in f0 and intensity: Utterance-final words were produced with higher f0 and lower intensity than utterance-initial words. Significant group-by-position interactions indicated that these measures were reduced in the latent aphasia group, suggesting subtle but systematic prosodic distinctions. Word duration further differentiated the two groups, with the latent group consistently showing longer duration than controls. Voice quality measures showed minimal group differences, consistent with the latent group being clinically classified as “normal.” Nonetheless, these measures provide baseline values for spontaneous speech, which diverge from cut-offs based on sustained vowels.

Conclusions: Prosodic features are sensitive indicators of latent aphasia, offering an explanation for why predictive models using prosody alone can successfully distinguish affected individuals from controls. Voice quality measures, while less informative for group separation, contribute novel data for this population. Together, the results underscore the importance of prosodic research for identifying subtle pathological differences and support future clinical and computational approaches to detecting clinical language disorders.

articles.title: Reducing Cognitive Rigidity in Psychotherapy: Effects of an Inspiration Tool on Creative Cognition in Adults With Psychiatric Disorders
articles.authors: Maya Wenzel; Marco Filippi; Anja Vandersmissen; Paula Kattermann; Anja Rauthmann; Charlotte P. Malycha; Jens C Pruessner; Rainer Krähenmann; Patrick Fissler Plischke
articles.abstract: Objective: Cognitive rigidity is a transdiagnostic process observed across various psychopathologies and commonly targeted in psychotherapy. This study investigated whether a random input technique (RIT)-based inspiration tool enhances creative cognition in adults with psychiatric disorders.  Method: A total of 100 adults with a lifetime psychiatric disorder were randomized to either an inspiration tool (Brain2Business™ small) combined with brainstorming rules (B2B + BS) or brainstorming rules alone (BS) in a participant-blinded randomized controlled trial. Both groups completed their respective condition during two face-to-face psychotherapeutic interventions, followed by home-based sessions over 5 days. General, subjective, and objective creative cognition—including fluency, flexibility, and originality—were assessed. Results: Compared with BS, the B2B + BS condition increased objective creative cognition (SMD = 0.37, p = .049), including fluency (SMD = 0.43, p = .024) and flexibility (SMD = 0.41, p = .034). No significant effects were found for other outcomes. Additional analyses showed that the effect on fluency of B2B + BS vs. BS increased over time during the two face-to-face sessions. Conclusion: Findings suggest that an RIT-based inspiration tool supports psychotherapeutic processes by reducing rigid thinking patterns, particularly at later stages of idea generation.

articles.title: At what sample size do correlations stabilize with unicorn and other improbable creatures?
articles.authors: Anthony Durand; Clément Guichet
articles.abstract: To ensure sufficient statistical power, researchers are encouraged to justify their sample size through power analysis (Lakens, 2022; Cohen, 1988). One complementary approach estimates the required sample size based on the stability of the anticipated effect δ (Schönbrodt & Perugini, 2013). However, this approach was originally developed assuming normal distributions, and no simulation studies have examined its robustness under non-normal data. We therefore conducted simulations following Schönbrodt and Perugini's (2013) procedure to estimate the point of stability across various non-normal distributions. Results indicated that small-to-moderate normality violations only slightly affected the point of stability, whereas strong violations produced substantial changes. Implications are discussed.

articles.title: Prediction in action: toward an empirical science of active inference
articles.authors: Jet Lageman; Johannes Jacobus Fahrenfort; Heleen A Slagter
articles.abstract: Active inference has emerged as an influential theoretical framework in cognitive neuroscience, offering a unifying account of perception, action, and cognition under the single principle of surprise minimization. Despite its growing prominence, the framework is often criticized for the difficulty of extracting qualitatively distinct, testable predictions and its limited empirical grounding. This review addresses these concerns by systematically outlining and evaluating key predictions of active inference across decision-making in discrete time and motor control in continuous time. For each domain, we offer a formal introduction of active inference and contrast it with leading alternative accounts. In the domain of decision-making, we identify predictions that agents behave more stochastically when they are uncertain about what to do; that they explore to resolve uncertainty about hidden states and model parameters; and that preferences can be learned through accumulated experience. In the domain of motor control, we identify predictions that sensory input is attenuated early and non-specifically to facilitate movement initiation; that motor output is context-sensitive and reflects predictions of proprioceptive input; and that the brain adaptively controls and predictively corrects movements depending on estimations of uncertainty. Overall, we find that existing empirical work is broadly consistent with several of these predictions, but further research is necessary to draw more definitive conclusions. We highlight areas where evidence is promising, while emphasizing the need for theory-driven experiments that can adjudicate between accounts. In this way, active inference can move beyond a formal mathematical framework toward an empirically grounded theory of brain function.

articles.title: People Underestimate the Acceptability of Canceling Plans with Others
articles.authors: Rajarshi Majumder; Esra Aslan; Jareef Martuza
articles.abstract: Consumers frequently cancel social consumption experiences with others, such as dining out or attending events. Do cancelers correctly infer the reactions of those who are canceled on? Correct estimation, overestimation, and underestimation are all plausible predictions from existing literature. Across 11 studies (N = 6,824) with American and French participants, this research shows that cancelers underestimate how acceptable others find being canceled on. This underestimation effect is robust across different plan types, whether alternatives to the original plan exist, the level of effort exerted by the person canceled on, and justifications for canceling. Findings also reveal that the effect is partly driven by the cancelers’ beliefs that others are less forgiving and more impacted by cancellations than they actually are. Consequently, cancelers also overestimate monetary amounts of gifts and the time commitment of favors that those canceled on expect as compensation. Interestingly, upfront compensatory action only partly attenuates the underestimation effect, suggesting the misperception is deep-rooted. Together, these findings extend consumer research on misperceptions about others, from social beliefs regarding plan cancellations to compensatory gift-giving.

articles.title: Optimized cardiorespiratory state at tone onset enhances sensory responses but not associative learning in healthy adults
articles.authors: Praghajieeth Raajhen Santhana Gopalan; Miriam S. Nokia
articles.abstract: Previous studies suggest that when a tone-conditioned stimulus (CS) predicting an airpuff towards the eye is systematically presented at diastole during expiration, both sensory responses to the CS and trace eyeblink conditioning (TEBC) are enhanced. However, not all participants learn and individual variation in the body-brain relationship remains overlooked. We studied whether timing the CS based on an individually optimised bodily state enhances both sensory responses and TEBC. We assumed so, as sensory response magnitude has been suggested to reflect attention and stimulus salience: A salient CS should facilitate TEBC. Forty-one young adults and 19 older adults completed a passive auditory task while electrocardiogram, respiration and electroencephalogram were recorded. The cardiorespiratory state associated with the largest N1–P2 response was identified individually. Optimal states varied across participants and did not differ between age groups. Participants then underwent TEBC either at their optimal state (Personalised) or at diastole during expiration (Control). As expected, the CS evoked a larger N1–P2 response in the Personalised than Control group. However, conditioned responding, learning rate or the proportion of good learners did not differ between groups. Older adults learned slower, made fewer conditioned responses, and were less aware of the tone-airpuff association than young adults. These findings suggest that successful TEBC is not so much dependent on handling of the CS but possibly on further processing within distributed neural networks. Future studies should examine whether timing external stimuli to specific bodily states more effectively influences behaviour in tasks that rely directly on rapid sensory processing.

articles.title: Bridging analysis and performance in elite Para swimming: Applied case studies in external focus, constraints-based learning, and practice variability
articles.authors: Danny Powell; Greg Wood; Neil Dagnall; Carl Payton
articles.abstract: The implementation of skill change in elite sport often fails not due to a lack of biomechanical insight, but because recommended changes do not transfer effectively into stable performance. British Para Swimming created a dedicated skill acquisition role to address this gap between analysis and applied coaching practice. This paper presents two applied case studies drawn from embedded practice within an elite Para swimming programme. The first illustrates how an external focus of attention facilitated rapid performance change, retention, and transfer in a swimmer with restricted working memory capacity. The second demonstrates how constraints-based learning and structured task variability supported the (re)acquisition of an automatic perceptual–motor skill (‘spotting the wall’) through implicit learning mechanisms. Both cases are interpreted through contemporary skill acquisition frameworks, including attentional focus theory, constraints-led approaches, the Challenge Point Framework, and the Periodisation of Skill Training (PoST) framework. Together, these cases highlight how theoretically informed adjustments to instructional design can produce meaningful and durable skill change in high-performance disability sport contexts.

articles.title: From Emotions to Achievement: Positioning Language Learning Curiosity in the Nomological Network of Individual Differences
articles.authors: Nazila Fattahi; Faramarz Ebn-Abbasi; Jean-Marc Dewaele; Imelda K. Brady; Elouise Botes
articles.abstract: The present study aimed to position foreign language curiosity within the nomological network of key emotional and behavioural variables in foreign language (FL) learning. Drawing on an international sample of 345 FL learners, we examined the relationships among curiosity and enjoyment, anxiety, and boredom, as well as their links to willingness to communicate (WTC), engagement, and academic achievement using a structural equation model. Results indicated that curiosity was strongly and positively associated with enjoyment, and negatively associated with anxiety and boredom, supporting its role as a positively valenced construct within the FL emotional system. Furthermore, curiosity emerged as a significant positive predictor of both WTC and engagement, alongside enjoyment, while anxiety negatively predicted WTC. In contrast, boredom did not significantly predict WTC, suggesting differential pathways between deactivating emotions and communicative behaviour. Both WTC and engagement were found to positively predict academic achievement. These findings highlight the central role of curiosity as an active motivational and emotional driver in language learning and underscore the need for future research to further embed curiosity in explanatory models of FL learning.

articles.title: Remembering What We Did: Confidence Tracks the Distinctiveness of Action Representations
articles.authors: Weronika Domagala; Kevin O'Neill; Maia Juliet Armstrong; Daniel Yon; Silvia Seghezzi
articles.abstract: We plan by considering multiple alternatives, yet only one action is ultimately performed. How memory later distinguishes executed actions from those that were merely considered, and what metacognitive confidence reveals about this distinction, remains unclear. Across two experiments, participants planned and executed routes through mazes before judging whether specific locations had belonged to the route they took or to an unexecuted 'path not taken'. Confidence was assessed either before or after route navigation. Confidence carried reliable trial-level information about the quality of route choice, indicating that it functioned as a genuine subjective signal about action-planning quality rather than as a global response tendency. Higher confidence was also associated with greater discriminability between executed and unexecuted routes in memory, indicating more distinct action representations, but showed no reliable relationship with the criterion used to endorse a memory. These effects were equivalent whether confidence was assessed before or after action execution. Together, these findings suggest that confidence indexes the precision of action representations during planning and that false memories for unexecuted alternatives arise from reduced representational distinctiveness rather than from changes in decision policy.

articles.title: Sensorimotor coupling modulates perceived time and agency across visual perspectives
articles.authors: Shu Imaizumi; Giuseppe Lai; Anil Seth; Keisuke Suzuki
articles.abstract: Voluntary actions are associated with sense of agency and distortions of perceived duration. This study examined how sensorimotor coupling and visual perspective modulate perceived duration and sense of agency during manual actions in four pre-registered experiments using virtual reality. Participants moved their hands while observing a virtual hand moving (a)synchronously, and evaluated movement duration and their sense of agency. We found that sensorimotor coupling provided by synchronous visual feedback is associated with longer perceived duration relative to action-related time compression, compared with delayed feedback or pre-recorded movements of another person (Experiments 1 and 2). Sensorimotor coupling also modulated sense of agency and may serve as a shared basis for perceived time and agency. Comparable modulations across first- and third-person perspectives (Experiments 1 and 2), anatomical configurations (Experiment 3), and attention on external entities (Experiment 4) suggest a shared predictive system for sensory consequences of self-generated actions and others’ reactions. This modulation was observed irrespective of anatomical configuration and when attention was directed toward objects unrelated to one’s own body, suggesting that the underlying mechanism is not confined to representations specific to the self, but may instead reflect domain-general predictive computations. These findings, together with the virtual reality technique developed, offer new insights into how humans experience passage of time and agency during voluntary action.

articles.title: Altered States, Enhanced Potential: Psychedelics and Physical Performance
articles.authors: Zane Qarni; Jeremie Richard
articles.abstract: Interest in psychedelics has expanded beyond clinical treatment contexts, yet little empirical work has examined how users describe psychedelic use in relation to sport and physical activity. This study conducted a qualitative content analysis of Reddit discussions involving psychedelics and physical performance. A Reddit search for “sports psychedelics” returned 253 threads. After screening, 39 threads and 290 comments met inclusion criteria. Analysis identified eight content categories: physical performance enhancement; flow state/automaticity; perceptual and cognitive enhancement; risks, limitations, and safety concerns; reduced fatigue/pain perception; microdosing as strategy; psychological and emotional benefits; and fairness, ethics, and governance. Psilocybin mushrooms and LSD were the most frequently mentioned substances. Across accounts, users most often described psychedelics as enhancing performance indirectly through altered attention, increased mind-body connection, flow-like absorption, and reduced pain or fatigue. Some users reported greater perceived strength, speed, endurance, coordination, or overall capability during activity, while others described effortless movement, reduced self-consciousness, sharper perception, and improved focus. Reports were predominantly positive or mixed, with concerns about overexertion, injury risk, impaired judgment, and fairness in competitive settings. These findings suggest that psychedelics may be understood less as traditional performance-enhancing drugs and more as potential-enhancing substances that alter the subjective conditions under which performance occurs. However, because all accounts were self-reported, it remains unclear whether perceived performance gains correspond to measurable physiological or behavioral improvement. Future controlled research using objective performance outcomes is needed to clarify the relationship between psychedelic use, subjective experience, and physical performance.

articles.title: No single downbeat: Rethinking criteria for beat synchronization across species
articles.authors: Vani G. Rajendran; Hugo Merchant
articles.abstract:
Response to: “Data show that macaques do not synchronize to a beat” by McBride et al., 2026.

McBride et al. claim that our data do not support synchronization to subjective beat. Their argument rests on a series of narrow assumptions: (i) that there exists a single, correct human downbeat, (ii) that there cannot be multiple possible concurrent interpretations of beat, and (iii) that without identifying the phase of this “correct” beat, it is impossible to know whether “macaques are predictively tapping to this subjective beat, or if they are reacting to some salient rhythmic feature.” In essence, McBride et al. seem to have misinterpreted our claim that “macaques can synchronize to a subjective beat in real music” with “macaques synchronize to the human downbeat in real music” - a claim we never make in our article. Nonetheless, it is worthwhile to take this opportunity to critically examine the assumptions that McBride et al’s opinion is based on so that as a field, we can decide whether these assumptions are valid or whether they might require updating.

articles.title: Utility of a Subdomain‑Based ACE‑III Online Dementia Screening Tool
articles.authors: David Foxe; Omar Arnaiz; James Carrick; Sau Chi Cheung; Rebekah Ahmed; Manisha Narasimhan; Yun Tae Hwang; Nicholas J. Cordato; Muireann Irish; Samuel Warren
articles.abstract:
Introduction: The Addenbrooke’s Cognitive Examination-III (ACE-III) is a widely used dementia screening tool. It provides a total score out of 100 based on five cognitive subdomains: attention, memory, fluency, language, and visuospatial ability. Clinicians often rely on total-score cut-offs (88/100; 82/100) to detect impairment, but patterns across domains may improve diagnostic accuracy. Study aims: (i) develop an optimal model using ACE-III subdomain profiles with probability thresholds; (ii) create an interactive online screening tool.
Methods: Participants were from the FRONTIER Research Group: 431 patients with dementia, 107 controls, yielding 1,068 ACE-III assessments. Logistic regression models were developed and validated in an independent cohort from the Sydney Memory and Ageing Study (64 dementia, 273 controls).
Results: The best model used all five subdomains (Tjur’s R²=0.819), correctly classifying 91.95% of cases at 95% risk threshold, outperforming total-score models (p<0.05).
Discussion: The online ACE-III tool supports personalised dementia risk estimation based on subdomain patterns.

articles.title: One-Day Intracranial Accelerated iTBS: Antidepressant Effects and Mechanistic Insights from a Patient with Major Depression and Epilepsy
articles.authors: Joshua Tatz; Umair Hassan; Joel Berger; Madaline M. Mocchi; Benjamin Pace; Ariane Rhone; Corey Keller; Matthew A. Howard; Aaron Boes; Nicholas Trapp
articles.abstract: Major depression is debilitating and often resistant to pharmacological intervention. Accelerated, transcranial magnetic stimulation protocols involving intermittent theta-burst stimulation (iTBS) to dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) can produce rapid antidepressant effects, but durability is limited and repeated treatments are often necessary. In the present case study, we evaluated a novel therapeutic strategy involving a 20-session, single-day accelerated iTBS protocol delivered via invasive intracranial stimulation to DLPFC in a patient with depression undergoing intracranial monitoring for treatment-refractory epilepsy. Depression remitted, with symptom reduction persisting for at least nine weeks. Indices of modulation near the DLPFC stimulation site included enhanced theta oscillations in DLPFC, increased single unit firing rates in bilateral hippocampi, and changes in cortico-cortical evoked potentials (CCEPs) in the DLPFC, with early and late components showing different effects. Together, these findings provide proof-of-concept for intracranial iTBS as a therapeutic approach with novel insight into its local and network-level neuromodulatory effects.

articles.title: Using agent-based simulations to anticipate risks and mitigation measures in platform governance
articles.authors: Max van Drunen; Natali Helberger; Aytalina Kulichkina; Annie Waldherr
articles.abstract: Risk analysis and mitigation in platform governance are constrained by the difficulties of testing changes to platforms’ systems and accessing data. Other fields, ranging from public health to economics, as well as an emerging body of platform research, use agent-based modeling to overcome this obstacle by empowering researchers and regulators to test changes virtually. However, it is still unclear how and under what conditions agent-based modeling can effectively support platform governance. This article aims to reduce this uncertainty in two ways. First, it provides a theoretical framework on the benefits and risks of using agent-based modeling in platform governance, drawing on literature on platform risk assessment, the use of agent-based modeling in other fields, and anticipatory governance and evidence-based policymaking. Second, to validate this framework and clarify the conditions under which regulators can rely on evidence produced by agent-based models, the article reports the results of interviews with 18 regulators. The article finds that agent-based simulations have significant potential to complement other sources of evidence used in platform risk assessment. Their main added value is that they allow researchers and regulators to more easily, ethically, and independently explore the potential impacts of changes to platform design, and provide interactive and visual scenarios that concretize abstract platform dynamics.

articles.title: “I even saw four deer grazing”: online-offline entanglements in the psilocybin pluriverse
articles.authors: Michał Wanke; Paweł Matuszewski; Piotr Siuda
articles.abstract:
Purpose
This paper aims to examine evolving practices and discourses surrounding psilocybin mushrooms on an online drug forum. Situating use within hybrid digital and ecological contexts, it traces shifts in user engagement before and after the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic to contextualize localized realities of the contemporary psychedelic renaissance.

Design/methodology/approach
Using an agential cut framework, the authors conducted a thematic analysis of approximately 3,000 qualitative posts from the largest Polish forum (mid-2000s–2023). The study tracks how forum users integrate digital tools (mapping, smartphone verification) with environmental field observations, mapping the online-offline entanglements and more-than-human relations of foraging.

Findings
Analysis reveals a post-2020 shift from immersive, metaphysical narratives toward predictive, scientifically informed and risk-managed practices. Building directly on critical drug scholarship regarding assemblage thinking and ontological multiplicity, the authors conceptualize the forum as an apparatus within a psychedelic pluriverse, where radically different versions of psilocybin simultaneously coexist, negotiate legitimacy and sustain themselves.

Originality/value
By integrating longitudinal forum analysis with a multispecies and pluriverse perspective, the study shows how digital platforms and embodied ecological practices jointly shape contemporary psychedelic practices, highlighting their relational, contingent and ethically informed character and demonstrating how shifts in discursive framings coincide with changing modes of ecological attention and risk reflexivity.

articles.title: The role of altmetrics in citation emergence: a temporal approach
articles.authors: José Luis Ortega; Evangelina Becerra Rodero; Sergio Castro Cortacero
articles.abstract: This study aims to identify differences in the temporal distribution of altmetrics, detect associations between them, and determine which metrics have the greatest capacity to influence citation impact. Mentions and their appearance dates for 7,963 research publications were extracted from Altmetric.com. Several data mining techniques (transition matrices and association rules) and survival analysis methods (the Kaplan-Meier estimator, Cox proportional hazards models, and Accelerated Failure Time (AFT) models) were used to find longitudinal relationships between altmetrics and bibliographic citations. The results showed that there are two groups of metrics according to their temporal distribution. Attention metrics (social networks and digital media) are those that occur almost immediately after publication and have a short lifespan, while publication metrics, which come from edited publications (citations, Wikipedia, policy documents, patents), appear later and accumulate gradually over time. Social networks and digital media have greater interactions among themselves than publication metrics. Regarding the role of altmetrics in preceding citation impact, social networks have the ability to trigger the appearance of citations and shorten the time it takes for them to occur. Specifically, Research Highlights and Clinical Guidelines, two metrics associated with qualitative assessments, are those that most strongly influence the appearance of citations.

articles.title: Consent to sharing shopping data for health research: a mixed-methods study
articles.authors: Romana Burgess; Poppy Taylor; Kate Shiells; Mark Mumme; Andy Boyd; Anya Skatova
articles.abstract:
Introduction: Shopping data offer detailed, objective records of diet and lifestyle habits, providing a valuable but under-used resource for health research. While willingness to share personal data has been widely studied, no previous research has directly compared stated willingness with subsequent actual data sharing within the same cohort. Understanding whether willingness predicts actual consent, and how this varies by sociodemographic characteristics, motivations, and barriers, is essential to improve recruitment strategies and the representativeness of collected data.
Methods: We employed a mixed-methods approach using the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children (ALSPAC). Quantitative analyses combined sociodemographic variables with responses from a 2018 survey assessing willingness to consent to shopping data linkage and outcomes from a 2023 loyalty card consent campaign. These were complemented by semi-structured interviews in 2019 exploring attitudes towards sharing shopping data. 
Results: In 2018, 60.7% of participants indicated willingness to share shopping data (n = 2142, 9230 invited). In 2023, actual consent—requiring provision of loyalty card details for five UK retailers (Sainsbury’s, Tesco, Boots, Morrisons, and Coop)—was obtained from 511 of 6170 invited participants. Demographic characteristics were broadly comparable across samples. Stated willingness was associated with higher odds of actual consent (adjusted OR = 1.48, 95% CI: 1.16–1.90), indicating that willingness is a meaningful predictor of subsequent data sharing. Sociodemographic characteristics and motivations (e.g., societal benefit, scientific interest) showed modest associations with both willingness and consent and moderated the willingness–consent relationship. Interviews reinforced the importance of trust in ALSPAC and clear communication about data use, while highlighting privacy concerns as minor barriers.
Conclusion:  This study provides the first within-cohort evidence that willingness to share shopping data predicts subsequent data sharing, albeit imperfectly. Willingness surveys can therefore inform recruitment planning, but additional strategies are required to improve realised consent rates. Addressing practical barriers, clarifying data use, and highlighting societal and scientific benefits may help narrow this gap; Targeted engagement, including through informative materials and outreach events, may help to reach groups less likely to consent. These insights can inform data collection in longitudinal cohorts and wider population samples.

articles.title: Data Note: Linking Shopping Transaction Data into the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children
articles.authors: Romana Burgess; Mark Mumme; Andy Boyd; Anya Skatova
articles.abstract:
Introduction: This data note describes the collection and linkage of participant’s Sainsburys shopping data into the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children (ALSPAC). 
Methods: Data were collected via informed participant consent and Subject Access Requests under UK GDPR, securely transferred and integrated into the ALSPAC database using unique study identifiers. Participant consultation and a technical pilot ensured transparent, acceptable, and secure linkage. 
Results: The dataset contains anonymised transactions for 244 participants, covering 658,375 item purchases between July 2014 and February 2024. Transactions include an item description, price, quantity, time, date, store location, and other variables. Items fall into 82 categories (e.g., produce) and 1002 subcategories (e.g., bananas). In terms of representativeness, the cohort is skewed towards females, consists of participants of similar age, and is predominantly made up of individuals with higher educational attainment and of White ethnicity.
Conclusion: The linkage of shopping records complements ALSPACs extensive existing dataset, which comprises over 30 years of sociodemographic and health-related data. The linkage stands as a novel opportunity for future research into dietary habits, nutritional intake, and subsequent health outcomes. Access to data may be granted to approved researchers upon reasonable request, following a proposal submission through the ALSPAC website (http://www.bristol.ac.uk/alspac).

articles.title: Gendered attrition patterns in a male-typed work setting - Comparing gender differences in attrition among Dutch police staff to the public sector
articles.authors: Gemma G. M. Geuke; Amy Nivette; Isabelle W. J. van der Vegt; Eva Jaspers
articles.abstract: Research shows that women leave male-dominated work settings more often than their male colleagues. In public services such as policing, disproportionate attrition of women slows improvement of gender equality, but can also affect legitimacy and service outcomes. Typically, attrition of women in policing is studied in isolation, while sources (and thus, solutions) of inequalities may lie in societal or broader organizational dynamics. Using monthly employment register data, this study compared attrition of Dutch female policing staff to those of their male counterparts and their peers in other public subsectors. Findings indicated that, while women were more likely to leave the Dutch policing sector than men, several other subsectors showed similar gender differences. Results suggest that approaches to improve gender equality, both in policing and in other ‘gender-typed’ subsectors, should not be limited to increasing representation and should involve reviewing implicit assumptions about employees, including procedures for appraisal.

articles.title: Understanding app-diary study participants' interaction with personalized feedback using in-app paradata
articles.authors: Thijs Cornelis Carrière; Barry Schouten; Bella Struminskaya
articles.abstract:
In app-based data collection, researchers increasingly provide participants with personalized feedback based on participants’ data and study behavior. Personalized
feedback is hypothesized to have positive effects on participant engagement and data quality. However, empirical evidence on these positive effects and research on the mechanisms on the effects that feedback has on participation remains scarce. We investigate what characteristics participants have who interact with their feedback and whether feedback affects data quality in an app-based household budget diary study in the general population of the Netherlands. The timing of providing personalized feedback was varied by random assignment to two experimental conditions: half of the participants received their personalized feedback instantly, and another half received their personalized feedback after 14 days, at the end of the study. We used mobile app paradata to study actual feedback-viewing behavior. We find that most but not all participants view their personalized feedback and older participants are more likely to visit their feedback more often and for longer periods. We find no difference in data quality between participants that received instant feedback or delayed feedback, but using paradata, we find a positive relationship between data quality and the duration of viewing the feedback by participants. This finding suggests that the collection of in-app paradata can provide more nuance about the effect of personalized feedback on data quality, and that paradata can inform researchers about whether the costly efforts of implementing personalized feedback are worth undertaking.

articles.title: AI in the Loop?! A Systematic Review of the Use of Large Language Models in Survey and Public Opinion Research
articles.authors: Leah von der Heyde; Florian Keusch; Trent D Buskirk; Adam Eck
articles.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly transforming the entire pipeline of the survey and public opinion research process. The complexities of LLMs and the magnitude of their anticipated disruption warrant the specific attention of the field, but existing overviews are non-systematic, narrow, or theoretical. In this systematic, comprehensive review of the empirical literature, we investigate how LLMs are used before, during, and after data collection. We conduct a quantitative and qualitative assessment of 136 studies, synthesizing at which tasks, contexts, and research designs LLMs succeed and fail and which applications are underexplored. We find that up until 2025, research has mainly focussed on classifying text data, generating survey data, and developing survey instruments. Although there is a large diversity of possible models and interaction approaches, most research designs apply single models of the GPT family with zero-shot prompting to English-language contexts, putting the generalizability and replicability of individual studies into question. Nevertheless, patterns emerge: Across applications, LLMs perform better when approximating broad, well-represented aggregate patterns than nuanced individual attitudes, topics, or constructs. LLMs can be considered as supporting humans rather than replacing them. Going forward, careful model choices, reporting standards and survey-specific benchmarks would propel the field towards more generalizable use cases. Although the rapid technological development conflicts with rigorous qualitative assessments, survey methodologists are well-positioned to adopt LLMs, which will continue to depend on human-curated input and validation data. With this review, we expand the understanding of the potentials, concerns, and gaps of LLM use in survey and public opinion research, providing a starting point for future innovations. We make our thoroughly coded database available for future use and expansion.

articles.title: Mathematics of Forever Wars
articles.authors: Oleg Gleizer
articles.abstract: In this paper, we use a popular game-theoretical model, called the Game of Chicken, to generate a random walk in the plane that seems to model well the forever wars of the present, and of the past. The main point of the paper is to show that simple game-theoretical models have a lot of predictive power. The second point is that quantum game algorithms may offer solutions to social problems humanity was not yet able to see.

articles.title: Gender, Populist Attitudes and Voting Behaviour: A Critical Revisitation of Spierings & Zaslove (2017) in Light of EES 2014–2024 Evidence
articles.authors: Paula Roméro Cajaty Lopes
articles.abstract: This article provides a systematic critical reassessment and empirical update of Spierings and Zaslove's (2017) seminal study on the gender gap in populist voting, integrating a decade of subsequent scholarship and comparative evidence from the European Election Studies (EES) of 2014 and 2024. The original work posited that female socialization toward consensus and conflict aversion structurally distanced women from the antagonistic rhetoric of populist parties, particularly those of the populist radical right (PRR). Drawing on nine-country EES 2014 data and a fifteen-country EES 2024 dataset, we demonstrate that the aggregate PRR gender gap has narrowed considerably — from an average of −5.7 percentage points in 2014 to −3.7 points in 2024 — while exhibiting pronounced cross-national heterogeneity. We attribute this convergence to three partially overlapping mechanisms: (1) the strategic de-demonization of PRR parties under female leadership, producing a 'maternal-authoritarian' aesthetic that lowers women's psychological costs of voting radical right; (2) the emergence of femonationalism as a gendered discursive register that reframes nativist and anti-immigration stances as pro-women positions; and (3) a generational re-sorting, whereby the gender cleavage has migrated from the general electorate to Generation Z, where male respondents support PRR parties at rates 15 percentage points above their female counterparts, while young women increasingly anchor the populist radical left (PRL). These findings challenge the passive socialization model and call for analytical frameworks that treat female agency as constitutive of contemporary populism rather than merely reactive to it.

articles.title: Orphaned Knowledge: A Custodial Theory of Authorship for the Age of Artificial Intelligence
articles.authors: Babu George
articles.abstract: Scholarly publishing and intellectual property law have converged on a similar rule for generative AI: a machine cannot be an author because it cannot be accountable, and outputs without a human creator do not fit established regimes of protection or credit. This article argues that these responses are sound but conceptually incomplete because they treat authorship mainly as a matter of origination and reward. Drawing on Foucault, Merton, Shapin, and Hardwig, I argue that authorship has long bundled three functions: origination, reward, and custodianship. Generative AI disrupts that bundle by separating origination from the forward-looking human work of defending, developing, teaching, remembering, and answering for knowledge over time. The result is what I call orphaned knowledge: findings that may be true and valuable yet lack any identifiable person committed to keeping them alive. The article makes two contributions. First, it shows that both the credit economy of journals and the property economy of copyright are structured around human knowers, leaving machine-originated knowledge in a double vacuum. Second, it advances a custodial theory of authorship that redefines the author’s signature less as a retrospective claim to credit than as a prospective commitment to steward a finding. On this view, a machine may originate, but only a human or institution can undertake custody. The age of machine discovery therefore forces scholarship to confront a prior question that earlier authorship debates have obscured: not only who made knowledge, but who will keep it.

articles.title: Long-term childhood poverty in Britain: trends and drivers across the 1991-2017 birth cohorts
articles.authors: Selçuk Bedük; Anna Yong
articles.abstract: Any experience of childhood poverty affects life chances, but longer exposure is particularly detrimental to education, health, and future earnings. This study examines trends in long-term childhood poverty in Britain. Using a life-course perspective, we tracked poverty from birth to age ten among 1991–2017 birth cohorts. Our findings show that, on average, 17 per cent of children spent at least half of their childhood in poverty. Long-term poverty affected 25 per cent of those born in the early 1990s, markedly declined to 13–14 per cent for cohorts born after the post-1997 welfare reforms, and rose again to 23 per cent for children born following the 2013 austerity reforms. These trends are driven by shifts in the penalties associated with work-family risk factors, rather than by changes in their prevalence. These shifts in penalties reflect broader changes in redistribution and predistribution. The decline in the 1990s was largely due to rising employment and earnings in low-income households, whereas the post-austerity surge stems from reduced redistribution. For cohorts born in the 2000s, social transfers played a substantial role in containing long-term poverty despite worsening predistribution. Overall, the findings show that long-term childhood poverty is a major challenge in Britain and highlight the need to strengthen redistribution and predistribution.

articles.title: An inductive-deductive circular methodology of the  research process: Systematic thinking from prior  assumptions to final synthesis
articles.authors: Jo Bjørkli Helgetun; Thibault Coppe
articles.abstract:
In this essay, we reflect on the circularity of social science research and how we can attempt 
to put this circularity into a system to help structure our thinking and research practices 
through structured inductive-deductive reflection. We present our model for thinking of 
research as a holistic process from making our prior assumptions concrete and updating them 
systematically through constant comparison and a circular inductive-deductive analysis, 
towards a synthesis of the research findings.

articles.title: Contained and Uncontained Experiments
articles.authors: Kevin Munger
articles.abstract: I introduce a typology of political science experiments based on their ``containedness." Contained experiments are those for which it is plausible to ``perform the same procedure" and expect ``the same result." Uncontained experiments are those for which practitioners expect intrinsic variation to dominate; even when performing the same procedure, the goal is to understand how results differ across time and context. I propose three conceptions of containedness: pragmatic (community expectations about replication), complexity (information required to specify the procedure), and temporal (speed of experimentation relative to drift in the phenomenon). Lab experiments are generally the most contained, survey experiments occupy a middle position, and field experiments are the least contained. I provide a diagnostic rubric for evaluating containedness and argue that distinct evidentiary strategies are appropriate at different levels. For highly uncontained experiments, ``replication" is not a coherent goal; knowledge accumulation must proceed through theory rather than induction.

articles.title: Genetic Mirroring in Adoptees: Toward a Formal Construct Definition, Nomological Network, and Operationalization Framework
articles.authors: Giulia Suzzi
articles.abstract: Genetic mirroring, the developmentally recurring need to perceive reflected biological similarity in genetically related others, has gained clinical salience as an explanatory framework for the identity experiences of adoptees and donor-conceived individuals. Despite this, the construct lacks the theoretical architecture required for empirical investigation: a precise definition, differentiation from adjacent constructs, and a plausible operationalization pathway. The present paper addresses that gap through four coordinated contributions. First, a formal construct definition is proposed, encompassing three theoretically distinct dimensions: biological salience, embodied discontinuity, and mirroring responsiveness. Second, the construct is situated within a nomological network specifying its hypothesized relationships with established psychological variables, including identity exploration, narrative identity coherence, attachment security, genealogical bewilderment, and physical self-concept. Third, a theoretical case is made for incremental validity over existing frameworks: genetic mirroring is proposed to capture variance in adoptee psychological functioning that neither attachment theory nor narrative identity frameworks can fully account for. Fourth, a preliminary scale structure is proposed as proof of concept. The paper's aim is to transform a clinically emergent construct into a formally theorizable and measurable entity, enabling the empirical program necessary to evaluate its scientific and practical significance.

articles.title: Hedging Under U.S. Reindustrialization: Vietnam, Malaysia, and Indonesia in an Era of Intensifying U.S.–China Rivalry
articles.authors: Julie Yu-Wen Chen
articles.abstract: Southeast Asian states maintain deep economic ties with both the United States and China, and the region has become a major destination for supply-chain relocation from firms in both powers. Recent U.S. reindustrialization policies—including the extension of elevated tariffs to Southeast Asian exports—pose new challenges to the region's export-led growth model and to U.S. strategic positioning in the Indo-Pacific. Drawing on hedging theory, particularly Cheng-Chwee Kuik's distinction between return-maximizing and risk-contingency behaviors, this paper examines how three manufacturing-oriented, trade-dependent economies—Vietnam, Malaysia, and Indonesia—adjusted their foreign-policy and geoeconomic orientations in response to successive waves of U.S. reindustrialization from 2017 to 2026. Using a theory-driven event-coding approach, the study analyzes 66 verified foreign-policy and geoeconomic events to trace directional tilts toward Washington, Beijing, or neutrality across three U.S. administrations. The findings show that U.S. reindustrialization has not uniformly pushed Southeast Asian states toward China. Instead, different phases of U.S. policy produced qualitatively distinct hedging responses, mediated by structural factors that vary across countries. The analysis also reveals a misalignment between U.S. tariff instruments and the goals of the Indo-Pacific Strategy: tariff shocks create openings for Chinese economic leverage in the very economies U.S. industrial policy seeks to draw closer. The durability of the Indo-Pacific Strategy will depend on whether Washington can align its economic tools with its security ambitions and provide the structural conditions that enable partners to hedge coherently under intensifying great-power competition.

articles.title: eHealth Use among Adults with Chronic Illness in the United States: Health Self-Efficacy and Health Literacy as Pathways to Mental Well-Being
articles.authors: Qianfeng Lu; Wen Jiao; Qiaofei Jovi Wu
articles.abstract: Patients with chronic illness face a considerable mental health burden. However, the pathways through which eHealth use influences mental health in this population remain poorly understood. Drawing on Street's (2003) three-stage model, this study examined the effects of eHealth use on mental health among chronic illness patients, with health self-efficacy as a mediator and health literacy as a moderator. Data from the Health Information National Trends Survey 7 (HINTS 7) were analysed among U.S. adults with chronic illness (N = 2,928) using the PROCESS macro. The direct effect of eHealth use on mental health was insignificant. eHealth use was negatively associated with health self-efficacy, which was then positively linked to mental health. Health literacy moderated the relationship between eHealth use and health self-efficacy, with the negative association significant at lower literacy levels but diminishing and trending positive at higher literacy levels. Consequently, the indirect effect of eHealth use on mental health through health self-efficacy was contingent on health literacy, being negative at lower levels and diminishing as literacy increased. These findings suggest that health literacy determines whether eHealth use burdens patients with chronic illness. Therefore, ensuring adequate patients health literacy is important in eHealth interventions targeting this population.

articles.title: Sinks and Sluices: Contrasting Dynamics in Spatial Assimilation Pathways
articles.authors: Kristian Gade Kjelmann; Anja Jørgensen
articles.abstract:
This study examines how neighbourhoods can be understood through their functional roles in immigrant settlement. Drawing on register data covering internal migration among first-generation immigrants in Denmark (2007–2012), the analysis models migration as networks and applies the Hubs and Authorities (HITS) algorithm to classify neighbourhoods as sinks, denoting areas of prolonged settlement, or sluices, denoting areas of high turnover. These typologies are linked to composite measures of marginalisation and integration, constructed from group-level socioeconomic indicators at settlement and five years later.
Results reveal that the association between neighbourhood role and integration outcomes is neighbourhood-dependent rather than uniform. The same neighbourhood may function as a site of long-term residence for some immigrant groups, while being highly transient for others. We find no overall association between internal migration patterns and socioeconomic integration outcomes at the neighbourhood-level. Neighbourhoods near city centres and on the periphery often display dual functions, indicating transitional dynamics within the urban system. Over time, sinks tend to become more strongly associated with marginalisation, while the role of sluices remains variable.
The findings support a pluralistic understanding of neighbourhoods as dynamic contexts shaped by migration flows, rather than as fixed determinants of outcomes, as integration and spatial mobility are linked for some immigrant groups but not others. Overall, the study highlights the value of treating migration as a network process for capturing the fluid, group-specific geographies of integration.

articles.title: Who Overreports? Regime Support and Preference Falsification in Iran
articles.authors: Daniel L Tavana; Kevan Harris; chi shun fong; Amir Farmanesh
articles.abstract: In authoritarian contexts, individuals often express greater support for the regime in public than they hold in private. This gap---overreporting---is typically treated as a measurement problem rather than a political behavior to be explained. We argue that overreporting deserves greater attention in survey-based studies of regime support and is itself shaped by which social referents are salient to respondents during survey interviews. We develop and test an original theoretical framework that identifies three sources of overreporting: expressive identification, behavioral conformity, and civic resources. Drawing on a pre-registered, nationally representative telephone survey of residents of Iran (N=1,999) with an embedded double list experiment, we estimate regime support from the list experiment at 47 percent---roughly ten percentage points below the 57 percent recorded by direct questioning. Our findings indicate that expressive identification matters most: national pride and religiosity both predict substantial overreporting. Preference falsification in Iran is shaped less by the coercion citizens face from the state than by the identity-based attachments the regime encourages---findings with implications for how scholars measure and interpret regime support in closed societies.
