Adjudicating between these accounts is difficult because election outcomes are endogenous to conflict dynamics, and data linking electoral results to local violence is rare. We overcome these challenges using a regression discontinuity design and two original datasets from separatist conflict in Punjab, India. We find that national-party legislators significantly and substantially reduced security force fatalities across three electoral cycles. Reductions benefited both state and central security forces, and depended on dense pre-treatment telecommunications infrastructure—suggesting that national party legislators used superior bureaucratic connections to facilitate selective, effective counterinsurgency. Our findings highlight an overlooked way that elected officials influence the conduct of civil wars.

articles.title: When Does AI Pay for Itself? A Cost-Benefit Analysis of AI-Assisted Police Report Writing
articles.authors: Erik Alda
articles.abstract: Police departments across the US are rapidly adopting AI-assisted report-writing, yet its cost-benefit case remaines largely unexamined, and the field-trial evidence can be read as either favorable or unfavorable. This paper presents a probabilistic cost-benefit analysis of one such deployment at the Manchester Police Department, varying how the tool's cost is attributed and how much of the measured productivity gain is credited to the AI rather than to organizational learning. Under per-officer subscription pricing, the tool pays for itself. For a medium department, the average net present value is $2.3 million under a favorable, frontier-based reading of the productivity evidence and $0.8 million under a conservative reading anchored to the null result of the department's randomized controlled trial, with a positive return likely in both. Attributing a bundled vendor contract to the program instead yields a negative return. Cost attribution, therefore, determines the sign of the result, while the extent to which the gain is credited to the tool governs how confident a department can be in adopting this technology.

articles.title: Ahead of the Firm: Cultural Lag, Employee Expectations, and the HR Predicament in Corporate Sustainability.
articles.authors: Alessandro Reati
articles.abstract: As corporate sustainability has migrated from the voluntary, ethics-based vocabulary of corporate social responsibility (CSR) toward the measured, disclosure-driven logic of ESG, human resource functions have been charged with aligning organizational culture to the ESG expectations of top management. This paper argues that the assignment is structurally constrained by two desynchronizations rather than by execution failure alone. First, the timescale on which organizational culture genuinely changes is, on average, longer than the tempo set by ESG's market and institutional pressure, producing a cultural lag that tempts firms toward symbolic conformity. Second, employee expectations, especially around the social pillar that ESG inherited from CSR, frequently run ahead of what firms believe they are doing and communicating, producing an expectation premium experienced, in daily working life, as a say–do gap. Drawing on organizational culture theory, the literature on temporal tensions and decoupling, and the micro-foundations of CSR, the paper reframes the HR predicament as the management of two unsynchronized clocks and an expectation gradient, and considers when employee anticipation is a liability and when it is a usable asset.

articles.title: Who outsources domestic work in the Swedish welfare state? The role of absolute and relative resources
articles.authors: Elien Dalman; Therese Nilsson; Annika Elwert; Christofer Edling; Lisa Eklund
articles.abstract: Drawing on social stratification and relative resource theory, and gender ideology theory, this article investigates how class and gender shape outsourcing domestic work patterns within the Swedish welfare state, a forerunner in terms of gender equality. Using full-population longitudinal administrative registers from 2007 to 2022, we include individual-level information on domestic outsourcing following a generous tax deduction reform in 2007. The results reveal a strong class gradient in outsourcing, something which has shrunk markedly over time, suggesting a normative shift towards increased acceptance of domestic services. Regardless of absolute and relative resources, women outsource more than men. Multivariate analyses demonstrate outsourcing to be most common among (single and partnered) mothers, while childless singles rarely outsource domestic work. In couple-level analyses we demonstrate that outsourcing is not only shaped by absolute resources and time constraints, but also by gender ideology and relative resources. Without recognizing this intersection, analyses risk overstating the role of income while overlooking how relative resources and time constraint determine outsourcing. Specifically, results show that women who out-earn their partners outsource more, rather than renegotiating the within-couple division of unpaid labor. This suggests that outsourcing may be conducive to upholding the first phase of the gender revolution, by enabling women to pursue their high(er)-earning jobs, while hampering the second phase by removing incentives for men’s greater engagement in unpaid housework. By showing that couples from higher-class origins outsource more at a given income, we highlight how outsourcing may reinforce rather than reduce class as well as gender divides. The article further puts forward directions for future research.

articles.title: When the Standard Becomes the Shield: SA 600, Group Audit Failures, and the Case for Convergence with ISA 600 in India
articles.authors: Srivatsan Lakshminarayan
articles.abstract: This paper examines whether, and how, the design of India's group auditing standard, SA 600 (2002), shaped the interpretive conditions in which audit deficiencies arose in corporate failures, and evaluates the case for convergence with ISA 600. The paper draws on documentary analysis of fifty NFRA enforcement outputs — audit quality review reports (AQRRs) and disciplinary orders issued between 2019 and 2024 across six public interest entities — supplemented by NFRA meeting minutes, the regulator's 2024 consultation on a revised standard, and thirty-two comment letters. The analysis is situated against the international literature on group audit quality and divided auditor responsibility. NFRA's enforcement record reveals a consistent pattern: principal auditors treated SA 600 (2002)'s division of responsibility clause as a basis for limiting their engagement responsibility for subsidiaries and associates, even where fraud indicators were visible. The dominant failures were of implementation — non-compliance with requirements SA 600 already imposed — rather than of design; yet the standard's design structured the interpretive environment so that non-compliance became predictable and defensible. The Institute of Chartered Accountants of India (ICAI) did not submit a response to NFRA's public consultation, which effectively constitutes an institutional impasse; while most respondents supported revision, practitioners were divided. Although NFRA's enforcement outputs have featured in Indian professional commentary, they have not previously been mobilised as a systematic empirical corpus in the group audit literature, and this paper is among the first to do so. It demonstrates that standard divergence can create interpretive conditions in which non-compliance becomes institutionally defensible. This has implications for jurisdictions weighing whether and how far to converge national group auditing standards with international requirements on auditor responsibility.

articles.title: Will China Become a Christian-Majority Nation? Regulation, Demand, and an Agent-Based Test of the Supply-Side Theory
articles.authors: Ruiqian Li
articles.abstract: Whether China will become a Christian-majority nation in the twenty first century is among the most visible disputes in the social science of religion. Influential projections forecast sustained Protestant growth toward a majority by mid-century, resting on the supply-side premise that religious demand is high and roughly constant and that state regulation drives growth by pushing demand into underground churches. Survey evidence instead shows the Christian share plateauing at a few percent. This study adjudicates the theoretical claim rather than the data by formalizing the supply-side mechanisms as an agent-based model and asking whether they can generate the projected growth under the theory's own logic. The model is built for explanation, not prediction: it makes explicit the distribution of latent demand the theory leaves unspecified, and grants Christianity every structural advantage. The mechanisms produce rapid growth only when the population is assumed receptive before any evangelism begins. And under the regulation China imposes, the Christian share plateaus and never approaches a majority. The finding holds across extensive sensitivity and robustness analyses, breaking only when suppression is allowed to manufacture demand without limit. We conclude that supply-side mechanisms sort and intensify existing demand but cannot create it, and that demand governs the growth trajectory.

articles.title: Prediction Markets, Gamblification, and YOLO Capitalism
articles.authors: Usman W. Chohan
articles.abstract: This paper examines prediction markets through the theoretical framework of YOLO capitalism, arguing that many major critiques of prediction markets reflect deeper transformations occurring within the digitally-mediated dimensions of late-stage capitalism. While prediction markets are often presented as efficient mechanisms for aggregating collective intelligence and forecasting future events, they increasingly operate within speculative environments shaped by virality, emotional participation, meme culture, and platformized finance. The paper analyzes five major critiques of prediction markets: manipulation and narrative distortion, insider informational asymmetries, perverse incentives surrounding catastrophe speculation, irrational crowd dynamics and memetic volatility, and the broader financialization of reality itself. It argues that prediction markets increasingly transform collective uncertainty into continuously tradable speculative and cultural commodities.

articles.title: Reconstructing Visual Discourses at Scale: Integrating Unsupervised Clustering and Iconological Interpretation
articles.authors: Tobias Kamelski
articles.abstract: Reconstructive approaches to visual analysis have historically operated through close interpretation of small case selections, which limits the analysis of visual practices at field level. This paper proposes a five-stage procedure that re-scales documentary picture interpretation to large visual corpora while preserving its reconstructive logic. The procedure substitutes embedding-based computational clustering for the first, descriptive stage of image analysis, retains iconological reconstruction as a fundamentally interpretive operation, and adds a distributional layer through which discursive patterns become traceable across a field of practice. Operating on a corpus of 73,206 images from 13,000 Tinder profiles, it moves through (1) embedding-based clustering, (2) cross-coding of image attributes, (3) iconological reconstruction of constructions of attractiveness, (4) profile-level clustering into repertoires, and (5) socio-genetic distributional analysis. The procedure shifts the object of reconstruction from the tacit orientations of individual picture producers to the discursive orders of knowledge collectively articulated across a field, and clarifies the principled division of labour between computation and interpretation that such a shift requires.

articles.title: Beyond Thoughtless Reflexes: A Moderate Approach to the Quantification of Qualitative Data
articles.authors: Florentina Scârneci-Domnișoru
articles.abstract: The article proposes a critical analysis of how quantification is understood and used in social research. Extreme positions are often found in the specialized literature, which includes quantitative approaches that tend to quantify everything, transforming numbers into an end in themselves, as well as qualitative approaches that avoid or reject the use of numbers, viewing them as an inherent threat to understanding. The article advocates for a moderate approach, demonstrating that scientific knowledge exists even in the absence of counting, quantification, and measurement, but also that a reality described or explained without numbers is not necessarily thicker, but rather incomplete. Both the benefits of quantification and its major risks are explored and illustrated by examples drawn even from visual research. The article argues that numbers should not be eliminated a priori from sound research, but also that quantification must never be an automatism, but a rational, transparent, and fully assumed option. The researcher's expertise lies not in their paradigmatic radicalism, but rather, among other things, in their deliberate decisions regarding when, how, and why to quantify.

articles.title: Gaging LLMs’ strengths and weaknesses in political content analysis
articles.authors: Cornelius Puschmann; Hendrik Meyer
articles.abstract: Recent innovation and growth in the capabilities of generative artificial intelligence have opened options for the scholarship of political communication. While large language models (LLMs) have much to offer to improve content analysis workflows in political communication, there is as of yet no consensus regarding which options deliver the best results to the field and scale best computationally. This paper investigates the performance of five distinct classification models across three content analysis tasks of varying complexity: identifying political content, classifying political issues, and detecting stance towards climate policies and movements in news articles. Our automated coding is validated by 36,320 individual human coding decisions. We show that (political) task complexity is a crucial determinant for model selection. While simpler, efficient models perform well on lower to moderate complexity tasks, both proprietary and commercial LLMs exhibit superior performance on the nuanced stance detection task when appropriately instructed. These findings underscore the need to balance performance gains with computational costs and CO2 emissions, advocating for a “frugality approach” to automated coding.

articles.title: Extracurricular Activities and Educational Expectations of Children with Undocumented Backgrounds: Evidence from Los Angeles
articles.authors: Jeehye Kang; Philip N. Cohen
articles.abstract:
Executive Summary
	The academic benefits of extracurricular participation for disadvantaged children are well known. However, whether such participation would promote educational expectations for children of undocumented families is unclear, given the formidable institutional barriers facing them. Using the Los Angeles Family and Neighborhood Study (L.A. FANS), we investigate the role of extracurricular activities in immigrant-origin youth’s academic expectations, focusing on children of undocumented immigrants. Major findings include the following: 
•	While extracurricular participation is positively associated with educational expectations for US-origin children, the benefit is unclear for immigrant-origin youth. 
•	Compared with US-origin children, extracurricular participation of any type (sports-oriented, and non-sports-inclusive) is associated with smaller gains in high school expectations for both children of documented and undocumented immigrants. 
•	Compared with US-origin children, sports-oriented activities are associated with smaller gains in college expectations for children of undocumented immigrants. 
•	The disparities were salient among children (ages 12-17) than among younger children (ages 9–11). 
These findings are consistent with segmented assimilation theory and racialized organizations theory, where structural inequalities embedded in extracurricular settings operate in ways that marginalize racial-ethnic minorities. To better serve these minoritized youth, practitioners should create more inclusive environment where mutual respect for racial and ethnic differences is established and maintained. More importantly, educational and immigration policies should address structural inequalities and legal barriers facing immigrant-origin youth.

articles.title: Epistemic Filters in Survey and Platform Research: Sampling Visibility, Measurement Limits, and Uncertainty Communication in Media Studies
articles.authors: Sherif Rashwan
articles.abstract: This paper develops epistemic filters as a conceptual-methodological framework for connecting survey inference, platform data visibility, measurement limits, and uncertainty communication in media and social research. The framework is grounded in established concerns in survey methodology, computational social science, construct validation, and public communication of uncertainty. It introduces the non-play-dice paradigm only as a bounded analogy: apparent stochasticity may arise when a researcher observes a projected subset of a more complex system. The physics analogy is used to clarify the logic of restricted observability, not to claim that social systems obey mechanical laws. In social research, epistemic filters operate through sampling frames, platform access, questionnaire design, nonresponse, weighting, factor analysis, and analytic modeling. The paper defines survey operators for sampling, measurement, and analysis; proposes a five-construct instrument as a validation agenda rather than an already validated scale; outlines reliability, construct-validation, platform-implementation, and Bayesian updating routines; and provides reproducible R scripts using simulated data for demonstration only. Applications are developed for polling, journalism, media literacy, digital trace research, misinformation studies, influencer economies, and hidden or low-visibility populations.

articles.title: THE ROLE OF VERBAL AND NONVERBAL COMMUNICATION IN WIDYAISWARA'S TEACHING PERFORMANCE AT GOVERNMENT TRAINING INSTITUTIONS
articles.authors: Hasdiyanto Hafied; Samson Laurens; Rido Dominggus Latuheru
articles.abstract: This study examines the role of verbal and nonverbal communication in shaping the teaching performance of widyaiswara (government trainers) at state training institutions in Indonesia. Teaching performance in adult education contexts depends heavily on a trainer's communicative competence, yet the interplay between verbal clarity and nonverbal expressiveness remains under-examined in the Indonesian civil- service training environment. Using a descriptive qualitative approach, data were collected through in- depth interviews, non-participant observation, and document analysis involving widyaiswara and training participants at a regional training agency (Badan Pengembangan Sumber Daya Manusia). The findings indicate that verbal communication—encompassing language clarity, terminological accuracy, vocal variety, and responsive questioning—forms the cognitive backbone of effective instruction, while nonverbal communication—gesture, eye contact, proxemics, facial affect, and paralanguage—functions as the affective channel that sustains learner attention, builds credibility, and reinforces message retention. The two modalities operate complementarily: incongruence between them reduces source credibility and learner trust, whereas congruence strengthens andragogical engagement. The study concludes that widyaiswara teaching performance is best understood as an integrated verbal–nonverbal communicative practice and recommends communication-competence training as a core element of trainer certification.

articles.title: Workforce determinants influencing regional public oral healthcare delivery in Queensland: A context-sensitive qualitative study
articles.authors: Chaminda Jayasekara; Kelly McGowan; Nicolie Jenkins; Alexia Rohde; Sundresan Naicker
articles.abstract: In Australia, adult public dental care is available to specific eligible populations and is limited to a specified range of services. Waiting times to access care can be compounded in regional and peri-urban areas by workforce shortages, isolation and socioeconomic disadvantage. Limited evidence on workforce perspectives in these settings hinders targeted solutions. This study addresses this gap by examining factors that enable or constrain workforce performance in a regional oral healthcare service. It aims to identify and map the context-specific barriers and enablers affecting the oral health workforce in delivering care for eligible adults within a regional Hospital and Health Service in Queensland, Australia. A cross-sectional qualitative study was undertaken using semi-structured interviews to explore workforce determinants. Participants were purposively selected from diverse workforce roles. Interviews were conducted in person, online or via telephone, using a theory informed guide. Data were audio-recorded, transcribed verbatim and analysed using a hybrid inductive-deductive approach. Reflexive thematic analysis identified emergent themes, which were subsequently mapped to TDF domains through content analysis. Twenty-eight participants representing clinical, administrative, support and leadership roles were interviewed. Inductive analysis identified five overarching themes: perceptions of community and patients; communication and collaboration; service delivery; professional identity; organisational environment and wellbeing. Deductive mapping revealed that barriers and enablers were concentrated within three TDF domains: environmental context and resources, social influences, and skills, accounting for approximately 80% of all relevant determinants. This study offers critical insights into contextual and behavioural determinants influencing workforce practices in regional public oral healthcare. Addressing these challenges requires integrated strategies that strengthen professional identity, enhance culturally safe communication, expand workforce roles and leverage digital initiatives. Future research should focus on co-designing and evaluating theory-informed strategies to improve public oral healthcare services in regional settings.

articles.title: Beyond Citations: A Dual-Dimension Evaluation Framework for Assessing Cognitive and Practical Competence of Top Scholars
articles.authors: Yung Hsu
articles.abstract:
Traditional bibliometric indicators such as the H-index and C-score often fail to distinguish theoretical researchers, practical engineers and speculative scholars effectively. To resolve this evaluation defect, this study revises the definitions of the cognitive index (HC) and practical index (HP). On this basis, a dual-index model incorporating information-theoretic weighting method and linear weighting is constructed to derive the comprehensive competence index (HE).
Different from conventional single-dimensional evaluation systems that rely heavily on citations, the HE index emphasizes both cognitive level and practical performance when measuring academic talents. This study conducts empirical analysis on representative scientists ranked within the top 20 of the C-score list including Geoffrey Hinton, and the results prove that the proposed HE index can well reflect the actual industrial influence of researchers. Simulation tests covering educational stages from high school to doctoral level further verify the stability and validity of the model.
In the digital era, academic evaluation increasingly requires practical value orientation. The HE evaluation framework provides a more reasonable and comprehensive way to assess the overall capacity of scientific and engineering talents. The HE index represents a paradigm shift: it posits that talent evaluation must prioritize a dual assessment of cognition and practice over a singular reliance on academic citations.

articles.title: Reconceptualising LLM-Mediated Writing Practices: A Relational Approach
articles.authors: Mihye An
articles.abstract:
Since the launch of ChatGPT in 2022, large language models (LLMs) have fundamentally
transformed writing practices across various domains. This study reconceptualises human–LLM
writing through Michel Serres’s relational epistemology, considering LLMs as ‘quasi-objects’
that mediate collective knowledge production. For this purpose, it begins by proposing two
complementary metaphors of relational writing: knotting and parasitising. Knotting
demonstrates how LLMs serve as connective quasi-objects that synthesise disparate domains
into hybrid knowledge spaces, while parasitising captures the capacity of LLMs to introduce
productive interference that disrupts established writing patterns, creating novel meaning-
making pathways through asymmetrical exchange. Then, through an analysis of academic
writing, online content generation, creative storytelling and an educational experiment, I discuss
how the relational dynamics of knotting and parasitising transform the ethical concerns of
authorship, diversity, creativity and authenticity, as matters of relational responsibility. This
study concludes by advocating for LLM literacy that emphasises contextual sensitivity to
human–artificial intelligence (AI) configurations, richer discursive figurations and posthuman
pedagogical approaches.

articles.title: Measuring Functional Access, Not Service Presence: The CORE Framework for Community Resource Deprivation with Context-Dependent Weights and Quantified Uncertainty
articles.authors: Ushasi Kar
articles.abstract:
Widely used United States community-needs indices—including the Area Deprivation Index
(ADI), the Social Vulnerability Index (SVI), the USDA Food Access Research Atlas (FARA),
and federal shortage designations—generally measure population disadvantage, vulnerabil
ity, shortage, or service proximity. They do not jointly measure whether a population can
functionally use nearby services. This paper introduces CORE (Community Opportunity &
Resource Engine), a ZCTA-level framework that scores functional access across ten essential
resource pillars through four multiplicative determinants: Reachability by the travel mode
residents actually use, Economic accessibility at actual disposable income, Availability at
the times and capacities residents need, and Linguistic–cultural relevance (REAL). Com
posite scores use severity–exposure dynamic weights in which pillar weights emerge from
local demographic exposure and measured severity, addressing an open question raised in
the deprivation-index literature; a fixed-weight national reference score is retained for com
parability, and the resulting convex severity property is derived explicitly and grounded
in the Foster–Greer–Thorbecke squared-poverty-gap tradition. A 10,000-draw Monte Carlo
analysis with tier-scaled independent parameter error and correlated community-level bias
yields realistic score precision of approximately ±5–6 points and a formal reporting rule. In
an eleven-ZCTA pilot across ten states, two adjacent Cambridge, Massachusetts ZCTAs 0.9
miles apart score 83.2 and 66.0—a 17-point functional-access gap, positive in 99.8% of sim
ulation draws, not reproduced by the comparison indices evaluated here. Pilot parameters
are disclosed as structured expert estimates calibrated to federal data frameworks; a staged
validation agenda (Delphi consensus, criterion regression, resident ground truth) is specified.

articles.title: Extracurricular Activities and Educational Expectations of Children with Undocumented Backgrounds: Evidence from Los Angeles
articles.authors: Jeehye Kang; Philip N. Cohen
articles.abstract:
Executive Summary
	The academic benefits of extracurricular participation for disadvantaged children are well known. However, whether such participation would promote educational expectations for children of undocumented families is unclear, given the formidable institutional barriers facing them. Using the Los Angeles Family and Neighborhood Study (L.A. FANS), we investigate the role of extracurricular activities in immigrant-origin youth’s academic expectations, focusing on children of undocumented immigrants. Major findings include the following: 
•	While extracurricular participation is positively associated with educational expectations for US-origin children, the benefit is unclear for immigrant-origin youth. 
•	Compared with US-origin children, extracurricular participation of any type (sports-oriented, and non-sports-inclusive) is associated with smaller gains in high school expectations for both children of documented and undocumented immigrants. 
•	Compared with US-origin children, sports-oriented activities are associated with smaller gains in college expectations for children of undocumented immigrants. 
•	The disparities were salient among children (ages 12-17) than among younger children (ages 9–11). 
These findings are consistent with segmented assimilation theory and racialized organizations theory, where structural inequalities embedded in extracurricular settings operate in ways that marginalize racial-ethnic minorities. To better serve these minoritized youth, practitioners should create more inclusive environment where mutual respect for racial and ethnic differences is established and maintained. More importantly, educational and immigration policies should address structural inequalities and legal barriers facing immigrant-origin youth.

articles.title: Moral Adaptation: Sustaining Climate Activism When Futures Fail
articles.authors: Daphne Fietz
articles.abstract:
How does collective action persist when the futures that once motivated it become implausible? Existing scholarship highlights moral commitments and expectation management, yet it typically treats these dimensions in parallel. Based on a two-year longitudinal study of a German climate resistance group, this paper argues that moral grammars are temporally structured and subject to reorganization.
As doubts about the preventability of climate catastrophe grew, the group engaged in moral adaptation: shifting from a consequentialist orientation anchored in catastrophe prevention toward a virtue-ethical orientation centered on responsible conduct under conditions of crisis. Unlike temporal work, which preserves future horizons, moral adaptation relocated moral evaluation away from anticipated outcomes and toward present conduct, redefining the meanings of strategy, obligation, and community, and preceding the group’s later organizational transformation.
The paper theorizes moral adaptation as a mechanism of resilience distinct from identity maintenance, emotional management, and temporal work. In doing so, it advances the case for treating moral grammars as dynamic, evaluative structures whose temporal orientation can shift under adverse conditions, contributing to the integration of the sociology of morality and the sociology of temporality.

articles.title: Factors associated with disclosure of protected characteristics: a systematic review and meta-synthesis
articles.authors: George Wood; Rachel Connolly; David Nicholls; Jeremy Dietz
articles.abstract:
Background: Employers increasingly collect equality, diversity and inclusion data, and reducing non-disclosure rates is often used as a key performance indicator. However, why people with protected characteristics choose either to disclose or withhold this information at work has not, to our knowledge, been systematically reviewed. This review is a thematic synthesis of qualitative studies examining the experiences of disclosure in the workplace of people with protected characteristics according to UK law including disability, gender reassignment, race and sexual orientation. 

Methods: A grey literature search was conducted in October 2022 using Google, Lens.org and Web of Science to identify English-language systematic reviews and qualitative studies on adults’ workplace experiences of disclosing or not disclosing protected characteristics. A thematic synthesis was developed from these studies, and the search was rerun in July 2025 to identify additional evidence and update the themes. The risk of bias of each study was assessed using the CASP qualitative checklist.

Results: Findings from 60 papers were included in the review, including two meta-ethnographies and a further 19 studies. The thematic synthesis yielded five main themes from the experiences and views on disclosure in the workplace from adults with protected characteristics: 1) Disclosure is an important, ongoing life event that has significant implications; 2) When people do not disclose, they may develop strategies to moderate disclosure that require energy to maintain; 3) Moderators of disclosure; 4) Positive consequences of disclosure; 5) Negative consequences of disclosure.

Limitations: There was limited evidence on the experience of people with visible disabilities, bisexual people, and trans people, and no evidence discussing the experiences of people disclosing information about ethnicity. No studies were found discussing intersectionality of identities and how this might affect disclosure. Limited information was reported regarding participants' jobs, and some of the studies were older and conducted in contexts that might be less likely to reflect UK culture today.

Conclusions: Employers who focus on creating a trusting, supportive environment; actively mitigating the risks of disclosure; reassuring staff about how disclosures will be handled; and meeting accessibility and adjustment needs can improve the workplace experiences of people with minority identities and potentially increase disclosure rates.

articles.title: Real Estate Wealth Is Not a Monolith: Non-Primary Real Estate and Its Role in Wealth Concentration
articles.authors: Pietro Valetto; Stefano Filauro; Ive Marx; Janet C. Gornick; Carolyn Fisher
articles.abstract: Housing wealth is widely recognized as the most equally distributed major asset class in advanced economies, anchoring middle-class wealth portfolios and overall wealth inequality. Yet, in this paper we observe how treating real estate as a monolith obscures a critical divide. Disaggregating net housing wealth into primary real estate equity and non-primary real estate equity reveals different distributional patterns that fundamentally alter our understanding of housing's role in the wealth distribution. Using harmonized microdata from the EU HFCS (2021) and U.S. SCF (2022), we show that while primary residences are indeed more equally distributed than overall wealth, non-primary real estate such as vacation homes, rental properties, land, and business properties makes up the most highly concentrated component of household portfolios, surpassing even financial assets. Our Gini factor decomposition analysis across various European countries and the U.S. reveals that non-primary real estate exhibits extreme concentration, with Gini coefficients ranging from 0.88 to 0.96, and contributes disproportionately to overall inequality relative to its wealth share. Therefore, the equalizing effect of housing relative to other asset classes emphasized in prior research holds only for owner-occupied homes. These findings suggest that policies treating all housing uniformly may inadvertently amplify wealth concentration, particularly in Europe where non-primary property ownership is nearly twice as prevalent as in the United States. (Stone Center Working Paper Series)

articles.title: The Cost of Gold: Commodity Shocks and Violence Against Peasants in Brazil
articles.authors: Bruno Pantaleão; Daniel Rio Tinto; Sofia Antonelli Amaral
articles.abstract: Commodity booms can generate economic gains and social costs at once, particularly where institutions are weak. This paper provides evidence that gold-price shocks raise violence and agrarian conflict in the Brazilian Amazon, and that they do so by expanding gold extraction itself. We build a shift-share (Bartik) design---interpretable as a continuous difference-in-differences---that combines pre-sample municipal exposure to mining with international gold prices and exchange-rate variation, together with satellite-based measures of mining activity. Exogenous increases in gold profitability expand artisanal (garimpo) and overall gold mining, and this expansion raises homicide rates, the number of agrarian conflicts, and the number of peasant families in conflict, with the largest and most precisely estimated homicide effects borne by Black and mixed-race populations. The effects are concentrated in municipalities with exploitable gold endowments and differ across extraction regimes: garimpo mining maps onto lethal violence, while broader gold-mining activity maps onto land conflict. We find no comparable effects for industrial extraction, and evidence that official homicide counts understate the true mortality burden of mining expansion. The findings challenge the view that formalization and property rights alone can mitigate the social costs of extractive booms, and point to entrenched criminal landscapes that benefit from state inaction.

articles.title: Can Nonprofits Survive their Founders? Assessing Succession Effects in Private Museums
articles.authors: Johannes Aengenheyster
articles.abstract: Executive succession has widely been seen as disruptive for nonprofit organizations, negatively affecting performance in general and survival in particular. However, quantitative research on the succession-survival link in nonprofit organizations is limited, and findings are mixed. To extend knowledge in this area, a study on succession effects is conducted for a population of private art museums. These arts organizations show heavy founder involvement in funding, governance and vision, and have hence been argued to be vulnerable to executive succession. Succession effects in event-history analysis (368 organizations, 4371 organization-years) are only moderate and insignificant, yet might be more detrimental in some cases, such as for organizations that are small and opened by male founders. It is argued that considering the contingencies of organizational succession is required to comprehensively assess its hazards and opportunities.

articles.title: Transport-induced gentrification, perceived accessibility, and mobility practices: What happens to those who stay in their communities?
articles.authors: Rebeca Froés de Assis; Carlos Felipe Grangeiro Loureiro; Ignacio Tiznado-Aitken
articles.abstract: Transport-induced gentrification (TIG) has received growing scholarly attention, often focusing on displaced groups. Much less is known about the experiences of low-income incumbents who remain in place, particularly in unequal cities as the ones in the Global South. This paper addresses this gap, investigating if TIG generates reliance on motorized modes due to the social exclusion of low-income incumbents. We argue that urban reshaping near newly implemented transport infrastructures and services may isolate low-income communities as nearby opportunities become less affordable, prompting incumbents to adapt their mobility practices, reshaping where and how they travel to engage in out-of-home activities. Using perceived accessibility as an analytical lens, we applied a qualitative approach named process tracing method in an informal settlement impacted by a Light Rail Transit system in Fortaleza, Brazil. Evidence was collected through 29 semi-structured interviews and assessed using process tracing tests. Our findings suggest that while incumbents benefit from the proximity to new supermarkets, they feel increasingly excluded from nearby leisure and commercial opportunities. Consequently, many rely on motorized modes and on-demand transport services to reach more distant destinations. These findings contribute to the transportation research agenda by framing TIG as a distinct type of Transport-related Social Exclusion and demonstrate how urban reshaping near transit stations may undermine perceived accessibility for low-income incumbents, even when objective accessibility appears to improve.

articles.title: Stalled or distorted? Tempo Effects in Africa’s Fertility Transition
articles.authors: Duke Mwedzi
articles.abstract: Fertility rates in Africa have remained high, declined slowly, and stalled before reaching replacement level. However, there is little consensus on which countries truly stalled as existing research uses a variety of competing methods that are disconnected from fertility theory, do not account for tempo effects in the Total Fertility Rate, and rely too heavily on subjective researcher judgment. This study revisits fertility stalls using the first and second derivatives of tempo-adjusted TFR and a piecewise regression approach. This method reveals four previously unidentified stalls in Sudan, Botswana, Mauritania, and South Africa, as well as nine further stalls in other countries. Stalls in Botswana, South Africa, and Zimbabwe are particularly severe. While tempo effects do not fully explain stalls, they are positively associated, suggesting that stalls may partially reflect changes in the timing rather than total amount of fertility.

articles.title: Sanctioned Scholars: The Price Of Speaking Freely In Today’s Academy
articles.authors: Nathan Honeycutt; Sean T. Stevens
articles.abstract: The Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE), a nonprofit organization committed to defending and sustaining the individual rights of all Americans to free speech and free thought, maintains a database documenting the ways and reasons scholars have faced calls for sanction because of their speech. This report presents the results of a survey of 209 scholars in this Scholars Under Fire database who were targeted for sanction because of their speech between 2020 and 2024.

articles.title: Mining Transparency: Assessing Open Science Practices in Crime Research Over Time Using Machine Learning
articles.authors: Michael Beck
articles.abstract: This pilot study addresses the current lack of systematic, large-scale evidence on Open Science Practices (OSPs) adoption in criminology and legal psychology. A scalable, machine-learning-based text classification pipeline is introduced to map the prevalence of Open Access (OA), Open Data (OD), Open Materials (OM), and Preregistration (PR). The analysis is based on publication metadata and a year-stratified sample of full texts from the top 100 journals in Criminology & Penology, Law, and Psychology (2013-2023). After identifying articles containing statistical inference (SI) via a high-performing classifier, the author utilized GPT-assisted coding and supervised learning to train specific classifiers for OD, OM, and PR. OA was classified using publicly available metadata. Among 1,763 SI articles with usable full text, design-based estimates reveal a significant disparity in OSP adoption. OA is relatively common (40.9%, 95% CI: 38.8-43.1) and has steadily increased from approximately 20% in 2013 to 50% in 2023. By sharp contrast, trends for OD, OM, and PR cannot be reliably quantified. Extreme class imbalance and the minimal number of positive cases indicate a very low underlying true prevalence for these practices in the assessed field. Methodologically, the study confirms that GPT-assisted coding supports accurate SI detection, but robust prevalence estimation for extremely low-frequency OSPs remains challenging for downstream classifiers. Overall, this project establishes a transparent and reproducible pipeline and provides critical baseline estimates for future, larger-scale assessments of research transparency in crime-related fields.

articles.title: When do companies abandon workplace flexibility interventions?: the case of the four-day week
articles.authors: Juliet Schor; Wen Fan; Guolin Gu; Jared B. Fitzgerald
articles.abstract: While workplace flexibility interventions have attracted considerable scholarly attention, there is less known about why organizations continue or discontinue these experiments, particularly when organizational outcomes have been positive. We consider this question in the context of worktime reduction. Drawing on novel data from 197 companies around the globe that participated in a shift to four-day, thirty-two-hour workweeks with no reduction in pay, we find that 90% of companies continue this schedule. From neoclassical and Marxian theories of the workplace, and ideal worker theory, we test three factors. We find that improvements in well-being are associated with continuation, as neo-Marxian theory predicts. As expected from Marxian labor process theories, some employers revert to a five-day schedule after achieving efficiencies. Consistent with ideal worker theory, organizations with more white males are also more likely to revert.

articles.title: Relational Wealth: Ownership Diffusion and Ownership Confusion in Wealthy Families
articles.authors: Doron Shiffer-Sebba
articles.abstract: Economic elites have accumulated staggering resources. But scholars tend to conceptualize their wealth as singularly owned by individuals or households, neglecting long-established relational aspects of economic behavior. Using 30 interviews and six months of in-depth ethnographic observation at a “family office” that manages large fortunes, I ask how relationships shape elite perceptions of wealth ownership and the subsequent flows of family wealth. I find that when assets are legally shared (“diffused”), ownership perceptions can mismatch, and ownership can be “confused”. When owner- ship is confused, diffusing actors like family offices can influence ownership perceptions, shaping how elites wield wealth in practice. These shifts usually, but not exclusively, benefit “patriarchs.” Understanding the increasingly consequential resources of economic elites requires us to examine the assumption of unambiguous ownership: elite wealth is fundamentally entangled in familial and professional relationships.

articles.title: HIỆN TRẠNG VÀ TƯƠNG LAI HỆ SINH THÁI BÁN DẪN VIỆT NAM 2026: Phân tích các hoạt động xây dựng hệ sinh thái và vai trò của Nhà đầu tư then chốt
articles.authors: Willem Smit; Tobias Burgers; Quan Le; Phuoc Sac Tran
articles.abstract:
Nghiên cứu này xem xét quá trình tiến hóa của hệ sinh thái bán dẫn Việt Nam trong giai đoạn 2006–2026 và đánh giá vai trò của các tập đoàn đa quốc gia lớn trong việc định hình sự phát triển của hệ sinh thái này. Dựa trên khung lý thuyết hệ sinh thái, lý thuyết cụm ngành và góc nhìn chuỗi giá trị toàn cầu (GVC), báo cáo này phân tích cách thức các doanh nghiệp, cơ quan hoạch định chính sách, và cơ sở giáo dục - đào tạo đã cộng hưởng nguồn lực để đưa Việt Nam trở thành chủ thể quan trọng trong ngành công nghiệp bán dẫn thế giới. Thông qua việc khai thác các văn bản chính sách, dữ liệu ngành, tài liệu học thuật, hồ sơ doanh nghiệp và phỏng vấn chuyên sâu các bên liên quan, nghiên cứu đánh giá toàn diện bảy hoạt động kiến tạo hệ sinh thái: phát triển chính sách, thu hút đầu tư, kiến tạo hạ tầng, nâng cao năng lực đổi mới sáng tạo, chuyển đổi lực lượng lao động, nâng cấp nhà cung ứng và kết nối chuỗi giá trị toàn cầu.

Kết quả nghiên cứu chỉ ra rằng Việt Nam đã có bước chuyển mình mạnh mẽ từ hoạt động lắp ráp điện tử đơn thuần trở thành nơi sản xuất bán dẫn có quy mô phức tạp công nghệ cao, đặc biệt là ở phân khúc lắp ráp, kiểm thử và đóng gói (ATP). Tiến trình chuyển đổi này tăng tốc vượt bậc từ năm 2018 nhờ sự tác động tổng thể từ những biến chuyển địa chính trị, xu hướng đa dạng hóa chuỗi cung ứng, các chính sách công nghiệp có chủ đích và dòng vốn đầu tư bền bỉ từ các tập đoàn đa quốc gia. Nghiên cứu sử dụng trường hợp (case study) của Intel Products Vietnam (IPV) để minh chứng cách thức một nhà đầu tư trực tiếp nước ngoài (FDI) thực hiện nhiều vai trò của một Nhà đầu tư then chốt (Keystone Investor) từ 20 năm trước. Theo đó, tầm ảnh hưởng của IPV đã lan tỏa vượt ngoài phạm vi đóng góp kinh tế trực tiếp, thông qua việc xây dựng nguồn nhân lực, nâng cao năng lực thể chế, nâng cấp mạng lưới nhà cung ứng, tham vấn chính sách và điều phối hệ sinh thái.

Báo cáo đề xuất một khung chiến lược để hiểu rõ hơn cách thức các nhà đầu tư tiên phong đóng góp vào việc phát triển hệ sinh thái tại các nền kinh tế mới nổi, đồng thời nhận diện lộ trình chiến lược tiếp theo để Việt Nam bứt phá từ vị thế tập trung vào ATP đơn thuần sang các hoạt động có mức độ đổi mới sáng tạo và giá trị gia tăng cao hơn. Các hoạt động này bao gồm: đóng gói tiên tiến, thiết kế vi mạch, nghiên cứu và phát triển (R&D) định hướng ứng dụng và sản xuất công nghệ cao. Những phát hiện nghiên cứu sẽ đóng góp thiết thực vào các thảo luận về chiến lược nâng cấp công nghiệp, phát triển hệ sinh thái tự cường và vai trò của các doanh nghiệp đa quốc gia trong việc hình thành năng lực công nghệ nội địa.

articles.title: ROI and the Misclassification of Criminal Justice Education Programs: How Accountability Metrics Shape Public Universities and the Evaluation of CJ Education
articles.authors: Sandra L. Trappen
articles.abstract: Criminal Justice (CJ) education programs at public universities are increasingly evaluated through state level ROI frameworks that tie program value to short term earnings and workforce alignment. This article shows how these models, now embedded in many funding and accountability regimes, tend to misclassify CJ education programs. Descriptive data from the National Center for O*NET Development, BLS, College Scorecard, and state salary databases reveal three key structural mismatches: most CJ occupations do not require a bachelor’s degree; public sector wage structures depress early career earnings; and ROI classifications shift with occupational and temporal definitions. As a result, ROI models capture labor market composition and state funding priorities rather than program value, obscuring CJ’s civic and community roles.

articles.title: CONVERGENT FAULTS : A Quantitative Forensic of Private Credit's Synchronized Systemic Risk (2026–2027)
articles.authors: Djellal Djouad
articles.abstract: Private credit has gone from roughly $400 billion in 2009 to about $3 trillion in mid-2026. U.S. business development companies (BDCs) now hold close to half a trillion of that, split across three architectures with very different liquidity and disclosure properties. The official line, repeated by the Federal Reserve, the Financial Stability Board and most sell-side credit desks, calls the resulting risk "limited and manageable." Fund-level leverage is low, defaults are below the long-run leveraged-loan average, and bank exposure looks bounded. We argue that the official line works only when each stress channel is examined alone, and breaks down once the channels are read together. This paper documents five channels: software and SaaS valuation compression under AI disruption; bank-to-non-bank credit tightening, with JPMorgan as first mover; retail liquidity gating at perpetual non-traded BDCs; the "Bermuda Triangle" of related-party lending between Apollo and Athene, KKR and Global Atlantic, Brookfield and its affiliated insurers; and the 2026–2027 refinancing wall hitting a sector that has just lost its bid. Using Bloomberg Intelligence proprietary research, regulatory data and the public filings of the largest BDCs, we show that these channels are coupled. Each one tightens the others under plausible macroeconomic conditions, and the joint stress horizon is short enough to read on a calendar. The cascade is already visible. On 4 June 2026 Blackstone Private Credit Fund, the largest BDC, $82.1 billion of holdings, gated quarterly redemptions at 5% of NAV against requests of 10%. Its $1 billion 6% 2032 notes trade 150 basis points wide of triple-B financial peers, against an adviser (Blackstone Inc.) rated A+/A+. We map the catalysts that lie ahead, assign probabilities to three outcome scenarios, and identify a convergence window centred on Q1–Q2 2027.

articles.title: Satellite Sociology: Interpreting Spatial Traces of Human Activity from Earth Observation Data
articles.authors: Yuichiro Otani
articles.abstract:
We introduce Satellite Sociology, a framework for observing and interpreting social processes using Earth observation data. The framework treats artificial satellites as devices that capture the material traces through which human activity becomes expressed in space. These traces are not limited to urban environments but include any spatial configurations shaped by human behavior, institutions, and economic processes.

A central feature of Satellite Sociology lies in the definition of the unit of analysis. Spatial units—such as buildings, grids, or administrative regions—are not treated as neutral technical choices, but as explicit constructions of the social system under investigation. Different unit definitions yield different representations of social structure, enabling multiple interpretations of the same spatial domain.

The framework interprets spatial patterns as observable traces from which underlying behavior can be inferred, and emphasizes the feedback relationship between activity and its spatial manifestations. Spatial configurations both reflect accumulated decisions and influence subsequent behavior, linking observation with process.

To illustrate one application, we analyze urban space as an accumulated outcome of social decisions and examine its structural persistence. Using Shinagawa Ward (Tokyo) and Christchurch (New Zealand), we construct two building-level indicators: the Building-Level Vegetation Exposure Index (BVEI) and the Built--Vegetation Imbalance Index (BVII).

The results reveal distinct allocation regimes and identify spatial configurations consistent with structural constraints on environmental redistribution. These findings demonstrate how satellite-derived spatial patterns can support inference about the processes that generate and stabilize spatial structure.

Satellite Sociology provides a general framework for interpreting spatial data as traces of human activity and for linking Earth observation with sociological analysis.

articles.title: Political News Personalization in Conversational AI: Experimental Evidence from Three Large Language Models
articles.authors: Zachary P Dickson; Miriam Sorace; Thomas Samuel Robinson
articles.abstract: As AI is increasingly integrated into everyday digital life, a growing share of users turn to AI chatbots for information about current events. We run a series of experiments to test whether three large language models (LLMs)---GPT-4o-mini, Grok, and DeepSeek Chat---tailor political content to implicit user signals embedded in conversation. Two computational experiments each employ a demographic arm---in which cues about gender, life stage, and occupation are established in conversation before a political request---and an ideological arm in which cues spanning left-wing to right-wing orientations precede the same request. Across 2,000+ trials per arm, demographic cues systematically predict variation in political news provision across all three models, with occupation and life stage as the strongest predictors. Ideological cues show little effect on historical event framing for GPT-4o-mini but produce substantial framing effects for Grok and DeepSeek. Ideological cues also strongly predict news provision across all three models. Search query analysis reveals that GPT-4o-mini and Grok additionally vary their retrieval queries based on user signals, adding an upstream layer of tailoring.

articles.title: State Atrocities, Rebel Governance, and Public Opinion on Humanitarian Intervention
articles.authors: Jungmin Han; Hyunjung Park
articles.abstract: How do rebel groups shape democratic publics’ attitudes toward humanitarian intervention? Many state-led atrocities occur during civil wars, yet rebels are often portrayed solely as sources of instability rather than as governing actors who shape external perceptions. We argue that rebels’ modes of governance—whether inclusive or exclusive in service provision and decision-making—fundamentally shape public opinion on intervention. Inclusive governance that embodies equality and participation reinforces identification with democratic values, strengthening the moral imperative to protect and the instrumental confidence that intervention would be both justified and effective. In contrast, exclusive governance, marked by coercion or discrimination, undermines perceptions of shared identity and moral legitimacy, making state repression appear justified and intervention less defensible. We test these claims through survey experiments in the U.S. (N=1,615) and the U.K. (N=1,583). Across both countries, inclusive governance boosts perceived legitimacy and credibility of rebels, fostering positive attitudes toward humanitarian intervention, while exclusive governance undermines these perceptions and weakens public endorsement.

articles.title: Die Zukunft der Sexarbeit. Rechtsmodelle, Plattformökonomie und Arbeitsorganisation im internationalen Vergleich. Eine theoretische Synthese.
articles.authors: Susanne Bleier Wilp
articles.abstract:
This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of the future of sex work along
three interlocking axes: legal governance models, platformisation, and labour
organisation. Drawing exclusively on peer-reviewed research and
methodologically transparent evaluations - including the systematic review by
Platt et al. (2018), the Lancet series on HIV and sex work (Shannon et al.
2015), the New Zealand evaluation literature following the Prostitution
Reform Act 2003, the German federal evaluation of the
Prostituiertenschutzgesetz (KFN 2025), and emerging implementation
research on Belgium's 2024 labour law framework - it argues that the future of
sex work will be decided less in criminal law than in labour law and digital
infrastructure. The paper identifies a structural paradox: while the
epidemiological and criminological evidence increasingly supports
decriminalisation and labour rights, the political momentum in Europe,
reinforced by the European Parliament's 2023 resolution and the European
Court of Human Rights' 2024 ruling on the French client criminalisation law,
points in the opposite direction. Simultaneously, platform capitalism and
financial infrastructure exercise a form of private, extraterritorial regulation -
exemplified by FOSTA-SESTA's global chilling effects - that increasingly
overrides national legal frameworks. Three scenarios for 2040 are developed:
labour-law normalisation (the Belgian path), neo-abolitionist expansion, and
platform-mediated informalisation. The paper concludes that the third
scenario is currently the most probable default, and that only deliberate
institutional design - of the kind pioneered in Belgium - can shift the
trajectory.

articles.title: The Geopolitics of Cryptography:  End-to-End Encryption at the Intersection of Technology, Security, and Sovereignty
articles.authors: Masayuki Hatta
articles.abstract: End-to-end encryption (E2EE) has become a central site of conflict among privacy rights, national security, and digital sovereignty. This paper integrates comparative policy analysis, technical feasibility assessment, and multi-stakeholder analysis to examine the international dynamics of E2EE regulation across twenty jurisdictions. It develops an original five-part typology—prohibitionist, conditional-access, permissive, hybrid, and network-shutdown-dependent—and argues that regulatory divergence is shaped less by technical constraints than by how digital sovereignty is conceptualized, the character of political regimes, and institutional linkages with the lawful-interception industry. The paper further demonstrates that backdoor demands have persisted for thirty years despite their technical infeasibility. This persistence is explained by a self-reinforcing cycle in which organizational reputation maintenance, security-industry interests, and political framing around child protection and counterterrorism mutually reinforce each other. Platform companies are repositioned as quasi-sovereign actors whose norm-making capacity over encrypted digital communications rivals that of states. These private entities exercise strategic resistance through technical architecture and cryptographic discourse, doing so selectively as a function of market scale and state coercive capacity. The paper incorporates events through May 2026, including the January 2025 UK Technical Capability Notice to Apple, the subsequent US-brokered withdrawal of that demand, and the April 2026 expiry of the EU CSAR 1.0 interim scanning regime. From this analysis it derives four principles of regulatory design: prior technical feasibility assessment, proportionality, international interoperability, and multi-stakeholder governance inclusive of the Global South.

articles.title: When technology levels: the cost of arms and wealth inequality in the ancient world
articles.authors: Diego Castañeda Garza
articles.abstract:
A long tradition in historical sociology holds that broad military participation makes societies more
egalitarian, but the idea is hard to test. This paper uses a global archaeological database of
settlement-level wealth Gini coefficients (Bogaard et al. 2025) to test a version of the Andreski
(1954) hypothesis: that iron, a cheap, abundant, and mass-producible metal, lowered inequality by
broadening who could be armed, while bronze, an expensive and elite-controlled metal alloy,
favored the concentration of wealth by keeping the means of violence tightly controlled. In the
Eurasian ancient world, where both metals were indigenous, three econometric designs support
the “democratization of violence” thesis. First, within the same locality and holding the
appropriability of agricultural production constant, iron is associated with a Gini of wealth about
0.07–0.10 lower, while bronze carries no association. Second, an event study around iron’s local
adoption shows a similar effect. Third, in a spectrum of technologies, iron is the only one that
reduces inequality: bronze, the plow and animal traction, cavalry, and writing are all null, as the
cost structure of technology predicts. The estimates are robust to plausible error in the coded
adoption dates. The findings are exploratory and concentrated in the Iron Age Mediterranean and
Britain.

articles.title: DEFENSIVE DECISION ARCHITECTURE: EXPLAINING MANAGERIAL CHOICE UNDER CONDITIONS OF AUDIT INTENSITY
articles.authors: RALPH RENDELL TOLEDO
articles.abstract:
This study develops a theory-building framework explaining how audit intensity reshapes managerial decision-making in high-scrutiny organizational environments. Existing research commonly attributes conservative managerial behavior to risk aversion, compliance culture, blame avoidance, or organizational capacity limitations. While these perspectives provide important insights, they do not adequately explain how sustained evaluative scrutiny becomes translated into a stable decision logic that systematically influences managerial choice.
Drawing on audit culture theory, prospect theory, and behavioral decision research, the study introduces the concept of Defensive Decision Architecture (DDA). DDA refers to the systematic reordering of managerial decision criteria under conditions of sustained audit intensity, whereby procedural defensibility, audit resilience, and exposure minimization become prioritized over substantive optimization, innovation, and adaptive performance. Unlike related concepts such as risk aversion, blame avoidance, and procedural compliance, DDA emphasizes the institutional restructuring of decision criteria rather than isolated behavioral responses.
The framework proposes that audit intensity functions not merely as an external constraint but as an institutional condition that reshapes how managers evaluate alternatives and define acceptable risk. As defensibility becomes increasingly salient, behaviors such as documentation expansion, procedural standardization, discretion avoidance, and reliance on precedent emerge as rational adaptations to accountability environments characterized by continuous scrutiny.
The study contributes to organizational decision theory by providing a mechanism-based explanation of conservative managerial behavior and offers analytically derived propositions to guide future empirical research on audit intensity, accountability, and organizational decision-making.
